1. Problems with containers ^IsCpPmsS

Containers revolutionized the way applications are packaged and shipped. They made it easy 
to run the same application across different environments without configuration issues. 
But in real industry conditions—large user traffic, multiple teams, frequent updates, and 
distributed systems—containers alone are not enough. They introduce a new set of challenges
that become unmanageable when applications grow. ^BQ2wZaUj

1. Containers Multiply Very Quickly: ^ig0GCtcK

> In real applications, traffic is unpredictable. During peak hours, an application that normally 
  uses three containers may suddenly require twenty or fifty.
> Without orchestration, you must manually start and stop containers and track which 
  container is running on which server. This becomes impossible when the number grows. ^NbmF08jK

Example: ^DxPsWAix

> An e-commerce website launches a flash sale. The checkout service suddenly needs 40 
  containers instead of 5. Manually launching and tracking these containers becomes chaotic 
  and error-prone. ^flo8ONAp

How Orchestration Solves It: ^YVVlVLwT

> Orchestration decides where and how many containers should run. You simply say 
  “run 40 containers,” and the system automatically distributes them across available machines. ^QFuJ0WFL

2. Manual Deployment Is Slow and Risky: ^gEofRJG0

> Running a container isn’t just “docker run.” Real applications require environment variables, 
  ports, volumes, networks, and secrets. Managing these manually for dozens of containers 
  leads to mistakes. ^IOHcB6Ef

Example: ^GtOFMbsO

> A developer launches a new version of the payment service but forgets to set one 
  environment variable. The entire payment flow breaks in production. ^CCuSEAns

> You define everything (images, ports, variables, storage) in a Deployment/Task definition. 
  The orchestrator automatically pulls the image, applies configs, and starts containers the 
  same way every time. ^L33QXuxt

3. Containers Crash and Disappear: ^FId5s0Ub

> Containers are lightweight; if something goes wrong, they simply exit. Without automation, 
  this causes outages. ^H1m4yiDv

Example: ^B6EQsn1G

> A Flask backend crashes at midnight due to memory leak. Nobody notices until morning. Users
  see errors for hours. ^707BxUI1

> Orchestration continuously monitors containers. If one fails, it restarts or replaces it 
   immediately. This is known as self-healing. ^0r2EDYPA

4. No Built-In Load Balancing: ^BoSn3dC1

> Containers cannot share incoming traffic by themselves. If one container gets too much load, 
  it becomes slow or crashes. ^zCdMuklw

Example: ^aqsfGxgp

> Your website runs five NGINX containers. All users accidentally hit only the first container 
  because there is no load balancer. That container gets overloaded and fails. ^GHHW5Ogi

> Orchestrators create a Service or Load Balancer that automatically routes requests to all 
  active containers evenly. If one container dies, traffic is redirected instantly. ^a7MrBgm5

5. Updates and Rollbacks Are Hard: ^wOdT77hu

> Deploying a new version often means stopping old containers and manually starting new ones. 
  If something goes wrong, rolling back is slow. ^0Mob2PdL

Example: ^V7Siwp4K

> You stop v1 containers and deploy v2. It has a bug. Rolling back takes 10–15 minutes. 
  During this time, users experience downtime. ^5SgZteHR

> Orchestration supports rolling updates and instant rollbacks. It replaces containers gradually
  and if errors increase, it automatically switches back to the previous version. ^54TJE06p

Container orchestration platforms act as the automated brain responsible for managing 
everything containers cannot do by themselves. They automatically deploy, monitor, scale, 
network, load-balance, and heal containers. They support safe updates, secure secrets, 
persistent storage, and rollbacks. In short, orchestration converts a fragile containerized 
system into a highly reliable, self-managing, production-grade environment. ^cBFNHX3g

6. Networking Between Containers Is Complicated: ^BwwnPws1

> Containers get new IP addresses every time they restart. Without proper networking 
  management, services cannot find each other. ^83wLmMnx

Example: ^uW64ZhO5

> Order Service calls Payment Service using its IP. Payment container restarts and gets a new
  IP. As a result, all payments fail. ^G9p3RExc

> Orchestrators provide service discovery. Instead of calling by IP, Order Service calls 
  “payment-service,” which always points to the correct container no matter how many times 
  it restarts. ^5iPVOfam

> Container Orchestration is the automated process of deploying, managing, scaling, networking,
  and monitoring containers so that applications run reliably and efficiently in production.
> In simple words, it is a system that automatically handles everything needed to keep 
  containerized applications running smoothly — including starting containers, restarting them 
  if they fail, distributing traffic, adding more containers during high load, updating them 
  safely, and connecting them with each other. ^2dKgVAOX

What is Container Orchestration? ^0UlBh6o6

Problem with Containers and how container orchestration solves them? ^842qFpTk

Container Orchestration tools: ^wP5W6hUK

Container orchestration tools automate the entire lifecycle of containers.
They handle tasks such as:

> Deploying containers
> Distributing them across machines (nodes)
> Monitoring their health
> Restarting them when they fail
> Scaling up or down based on load
> Performing rolling updates without downtime
> Managing networking between services
> Managing storage for containers ^UDsX7RWP

> Kubernetes (often called K8s) is an open-source container orchestration platform 
  originally created by Google. Its purpose is to automatically manage containers running
  across multiple servers.
> Kubernetes ensures that applications always run in the desired state. For example, if 
  you say you want 5 containers running, Kubernetes keeps 5 running at all times. If one 
  container dies, Kubernetes replaces it automatically. ^YgDnRrCf

What is Kubernetes? ^gzjx9fYA

> Automatically restarts failed containers.
> Automatically reschedules containers on healthy machines.
> Can scale up or down based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics.
> Provides built-in service discovery and networking.
> Supports rolling updates and instant rollbacks.
> Works on any cloud provider or even on-premises. ^VKpdewB4

Some major features include: ^rGQ91apr

History: ^iEfuIPYr

> Google was using internal system called as "Borg" which is used for running large scale
  containerized application.
> Google decided to create open source container orchestration platform and in the year
  2015 they launched Kubernetes as free and open source tool.
> In the year 2016, CNCF (Cloud native computing foundation) registried kubernetes as
  free and open source. ^wSHYJNWJ

Kubelet ^YVjxc3VY

Kube-proxy ^HGNdQQyU

Container
Engine ^2oFyrGSU

1. A user sends a request (like creating a Pod) using kubectl.
2. The API Server receives the request and validates it.
3. The API Server writes the desired state (Pod definition) into etcd, the cluster’s database.
4. The Controller Manager constantly watches etcd to see what the desired state is.
5. When the Controller Manager notices a new desired object (e.g., a Pod), it decides that action is needed.
6. The Controller Manager informs the Scheduler that a Pod needs to be placed on a node.
7. The Scheduler evaluates all nodes and chooses the best one based on CPU, RAM, taints, affinity, etc.
8. The Scheduler sends the scheduling decision back to the API Server.
9. The API Server notifies the Kubelet on the chosen node.
10. The Kubelet pulls the required container images and creates the Pod using the Container Runtime 
    (containerd, Docker, CRI-O).
11. The Kubelet continuously monitors the Pod and sends status updates back to the API Server.
12. The Kube-Proxy on the node sets up networking, gives the Pod an IP address, and configures routing 
     rules.
13. If the Pod crashes, the Controller Manager detects the mismatch between desired and actual state.
14. The Controller Manager instructs Kubernetes to recreate the Pod on the same or a different node.
15. The API Server updates etcd with the new actual state of the cluster.
16. This cycle continues constantly, ensuring Kubernetes maintains the desired state across the cluster. ^jbTu4HUj

Here is the Kubernetes architecture flow: ^c3xxKn68

When a Pod dies, here is exactly what happens: ^oJ6XIni6

1. Kubelet reports to the API Server that the Pod is no longer running.
2. The API Server updates etcd — actual state is now “Pod is dead.”
3. The Controller Manager notices the mismatch between:
    > Desired state (Pod should exist)
    > Actual state (Pod is dead)
4. The Controller Manager does NOT directly talk to the Scheduler. Instead, it updates the API Server by 
    creating a new unscheduled Pod object that needs placement.
5. The Scheduler watches the API Server and sees the new Pod that has no node assigned.
6. The Scheduler selects a node and sends that decision to the API Server.
7. The API Server informs the Kubelet on the chosen node.
8. The Kubelet recreates the Pod using the container runtime. ^6lxO8hc6

The Controller Manager communicates only with the API Server.
The Scheduler also communicates only with the API Server.
They never talk to each other directly. ^fXfUCWAQ

To get api-version and kind of any resource ^xO1PE9xg

kubectl api-resources | grep <kubernetes_object_name> ^ZOx4CW50

2. Pods: ^i9fmOpY1

> A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes.
> A Pod is NOT a container.
> A Pod is a wrapper around one or more containers.
> A container cannot survive alone in Kubernetes. It needs:
-> networking identity (a unique IP)
-> storage
-> lifecycle management
-> logging context
-> health checks
->restart policies
> Kubernetes gives all this to the container by placing it inside a Pod.
> So a Pod is like a host and the containers are guests living inside it. ^e9uNMwqt

Note:
> Even though most Pods in real life contain only ONE container, Pods were
  designed to support multiple containers that work together.
> A Pod is a collection of one or more containers. It is conceptually correct
  even if most real-world workloads use single-container Pods. ^QE6psv0E

> Pods are recreated automatically if containers crash.
> ReplicaSets and Deployments scale Pods horizontally. ^Gw9MAiDo

Why are Pods important? ^KfFCiFUJ

1. Application Workloads (most common use case): ^YWEo8ffJ

> Every microservice in production is deployed as a Pod.
  Examples:
-> backend service → one Pod = one container
-> frontend service → one Pod = one container
-> worker service → one Pod = one container
-> cron job → one Pod = one container ^YbSK0PGs

2. Sidecar Architecture: ^OiLUsQx1

> Some Pods run multiple containers because they work together.
> Typical sidecar examples:
-> Container 1: Main application (e.g., Java service)
-> Container 2: Sidecar container (e.g., Log forwarder, Nginx reverse proxy) ^oBoe1Y0b

3. Sharing Storage: ^6Ggmqo1q

> Containers in a Pod can share:
-> Volumes
-> Files
-> Persistent storage ^IkxtEG2L

4. Sharing Networking: ^f2otjpVf

> Containers in a Pod:
-> share the same IP
-> can communicate over localhost
-> share ports
> This makes communication extremely fast. ^vtCn2Jd6

5. Scaling and Self-Healing: ^ySkmSWYs

Delete all Pods in a namespace: ^3yI3p0Ul

1. Pod created → Pending
2. Scheduler assigns Pod to a node → still Pending
3. Container images download → ContainerCreating
4. Init containers run → Init:0/X
5. Main containers start running → Running
6. Health checks determine Ready status
7. Pod exits normally → Succeeded
8. Pod exits with error → Failed
9. Node unreachable → Unknown
10. Pod deleted → Terminating ^caMEft4M

pod lifecycle: ^e3FmRWeY

Types of Containers Inside a Pod: ^KAd9F0ox

A Pod can contain 3 types of containers:
1. Main / Application Containers
2. Init Containers
3. Sidecar Containers ^9xdaOvdp

1. Main / Application Containers: ^tlor4aU6

These are the primary containers running the actual 
workload. ^MAij6pfc

Examples:
> frontend service
> backend service
> cart service
> payment service
> database client ^KYaSBJlB

Key points:
> Always run until the Pod dies
> Restarted if Pod has a restartPolicy
> Part of the service your users access
> Usually only one per Pod (industry best practice)
> Use Main containers when: You want to run your application (frontend, backend, 
   worker, API). ^7R2Bi9mb

2. Init Containers (run BEFORE main containers): ^f6oD8Glh

Init containers are special containers that:
> run before the main application container starts
> run sequentially (one after another)
> MUST complete successfully
> Pod NEVER starts the main container until all init containers finish
> They are used for setup work.

Examples:
> Download configuration files before the app starts
> Check database availability before starting
> Waiting for external service (Redis, MySQL, API)
> Creating necessary folders
> Fetching secrets or certificates
> Security validation

Key behaviors:
> Init containers do not restart unless they fail
> Once they complete, they never run again
> They have their own images and environment variables
> They have no limit on number (you can have 1 or 20)
> Use Init containers when: You need to prepare something before your app starts. ^SDHJm0Vm

3. Sidecar Containers: ^jPiDYOTO

> Sidecars are secondary, helper containers that run alongside the main application container.
> They run at the same time as the main containers.
> They extend functionality of the main container without modifying its code.

examples:
> Logging sidecar: collects logs and sends to CloudWatch/Datadog.
> Proxy sidecar: Handles traffic routing, retries, tracing.
> Monitoring sidecar: collects and exposes metrics to Prometheus.
> Security sidecar: Scans and detects anomalies. Adds authentication / authorization logic
> File-watcher sidecar: Watches file changes.

> Run in parallel with main containers
> Share:
-> network namespace
-> localhost
-> volumes
> Very common in production
> Use Sidecars when: You need extra features but don’t want to modify app code. ^OVJC6Rqz

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: <pod-name>
  namespace: <namespace-name>
spec:
  containers:
    - name: <container-name>
      image: <image-name>:<tag>
      ports:
        - containerPort: <port-number> ^bbG16N7e

Creating a pod using YAML file ^vMglf0QK

Syntax: ^yIcuw57E

apiVersion: v1 ^euSo49lL

> This tells Kubernetes which API version to use when reading this YAML file.
> Pods belong to the "core" API group, and their version is always v1.
> Kubernetes needs this because different objects (Deployments, Services, Jobs) can belong to
  different or updated API versions. ^ywCK7Xca

kind: Pod ^hmzHj1Xt

> This tells Kubernetes what type of object you want to create.
> Here, the YAML file is creating a Pod, which is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes. ^GhxckVmL

metadata: ^9t54Tk6Q

> Metadata contains identifying information about the Pod. ^Qkq39Vam

name: <pod-name> ^BvszsOFs

> This is the name of the Pod.
> It must be unique inside a namespace.
> Example:
-> mypod
-> nginx-pod
-> cart-pod ^j4VQUi2L

> Tells Kubernetes which namespace this Pod belongs to.
> If this line is missing, the Pod goes into the default namespace.
> Namespaces like dev, qa, prod help in isolating environments in real industry setups. ^alXEKTVM

namespace: <namespace-name> ^cMFUpK5t

> This section defines the actual Pod specification.
> Everything inside spec describes how the Pod runs. ^SfsbGSja

spec: ^3vsCaNFg

> A Pod must contain at least one container.
> This field lists all the containers inside the Pod. ^O6BugHal

containers: ^QgrsYYGg

> Each entry starting with "-" represents one container.
> This is the name of the container inside the Pod, not the Pod itself.
> Example:
-> nginx-container
-> backend-container
> This name is required because logs and exec commands use container names. ^n5s1YwsO

- name: <container-name> ^rgc17YOu

> This tells Kubernetes which Docker image to pull and run inside the container.
> Examples:
-> nginx:latest
-> python:3.10
-> redis:7.0
> If you don’t specify a tag, Kubernetes assumes :latest. ^250eEVFU

image: <image-name>:<tag> ^csuacwhi

> Defines the ports that the container exposes internally.
> This is only informational and helps Kubernetes understand traffic rules. ^UVMpBLNI

ports: ^No2wAYeU

> This tells Kubernetes which port inside the container your application listens on.
> Examples:
-> 80 → nginx
-> 3000 → Node.js
-> 8080 → Java Spring
-> 5432 → PostgreSQL
> This port is not exposed to the outside world unless you create a Service — it only defines the 
   container’s internal open port. ^fdhgNvo7

- containerPort: <port-number> ^acvstM7l

> A ReplicaSet is a Kubernetes object whose job is to ensure that a specific number of identical Pods are 
  always running.
> It continuously watches the cluster, and if any Pod that it manages is deleted, crashes, or becomes 
  unhealthy, the ReplicaSet automatically creates a new Pod to maintain the desired number of replicas.
> A ReplicaSet decides which Pods it manages based on label selectors.
> It creates Pods using a Pod template that contains the complete Pod specification (container image, 
  resources, ports, probes, labels, etc.). ^uulHMvgc

3. ReplicaSet: ^kNEAGVjc

Why do we use ReplicaSet? ^jmCzGB1g

> Pods are not self-healing. If you create a Pod manually and it dies, Kubernetes will not recreate it.
> ReplicaSet gives Pods:
• automatic replacement
• guaranteed count
• resilience
• stable scaling
> APIs that cannot afford downtime use ReplicaSets to maintain multiple Pods across nodes. If any node 
  crashes, ReplicaSet instantly spins up new Pods on healthy nodes.

> web apps (NGINX, Node.js, Java Spring APIs, Go microservices) typically need multiple replicas for:
• load balancing
• high availability
• ensuring zero downtime
> ReplicaSet ensures N copies always run so traffic from Services is always served.

> ReplicaSets are used indirectly during canary releases.
A Deployment creates:
• One ReplicaSet for the stable version
• One ReplicaSet for the canary version
> Rollout strategy then shifts traffic gradually. ^qG8uCtyW

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: ReplicaSet
metadata:
  name: frontend-rs
spec:
  replicas: 4
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: frontend
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: frontend
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: nginx
          image: nginx:1.25 ^WwfI7HX0

Compose file for ReplicaSet ^ugbaYbjZ

ReplicaSet Commands in Kubernetes ^xs35Aw1p

1. Create a ReplicaSet: ^5nBNHeoR

Creates or updates the ReplicaSet from the YAML file. ^pCVCOV8e

kubectl apply -f <file>.yaml ^5hPsyBsX

Syntax: ^vOEQyhs0

kubectl apply -f frontend-rs.yaml ^OFNoi3Np

Example: ^m1KZyeVk

kubectl apply -f frontend-rs.yaml -n dev ^v8aaPCWN

Namespace version: ^vBifJs0J

2. List all ReplicaSets: ^A6GZ51ND

Shows all ReplicaSets running in the cluster. ^5IRitPG2

kubectl get rs ^8qGnbnQc

Syntax: ^x61dvV2q

kubectl get rs -n dev ^8zz6diyP

Namespace version: ^BslpWan6

4. View Pods created by a ReplicaSet: ^4Y8Ynk2g

ReplicaSets manage Pods using labels.
This command filters and shows only those 
Pods. ^1CFuhaUT

kubectl get pods -l <label-key>=<value> ^5stuy8Mm

Syntax: ^B4QcuAA5

kubectl get pods -l app=frontend ^yO8jc7nP

Namespace version: ^jlXhotD4

Example: ^q7GMc4y0

kubectl get pods -l app=frontend -n dev ^PFtwTyBe

3. Describe a ReplicaSet: ^NCHDNDpE

Shows labels, selectors, Pod status, events—used for 
debugging. ^2U5PpIvA

kubectl describe rs <rs-name> ^tRWFfDB6

Syntax: ^6Y1tiwFH

kubectl describe rs frontend-rs ^92n1UhKj

Namespace version: ^5ZOt5VVA

Example: ^KZdybVmz

kubectl describe rs frontend-rs -n dev ^uBn4ii1I

5. Scale a ReplicaSet: ^hIbokwYs

Increases or decreases the number of Pods managed by the 
ReplicaSet. ^VbFHpZOM

kubectl scale rs <rs-name> --replicas=<number> ^LH9R1gSP

Syntax: ^Yy6zFhGu

kubectl scale rs frontend-rs --replicas=10 ^dqwQ5SPn

Namespace version: ^DLZfXsSI

Example: ^JcHY3fq2

kubectl scale rs frontend-rs --replicas=10 -n dev ^fyIr0HqF

6. Delete a ReplicaSet: ^BcAXqOT9

Deletes a replicasets and pods created by replicaset. ^Earu8z6x

kubectl delete rs <rs-name> ^GlBSxGaW

Syntax: ^07BM7XCo

kubectl delete rs frontend-rs ^HOk7m9UF

Namespace version: ^Cwo3HqqT

Example: ^9J6aynMg

kubectl delete rs frontend-rs -n dev ^4WuOfkPi

To delete RS but keep Pods (important): ^R9Rk3kbl

kubectl delete rs frontend-rs --cascade=orphan ^BCu9ulvJ

7. Dry-run a ReplicaSet before creation (validate YAML): ^UtMbeJgs

Validates your YAML before actually creating the ReplicaSet. ^eUPci1ut

kubectl apply -f <file>.yaml --dry-run=client ^2WfoAqEO

Syntax: ^6hndghCz

kubectl apply -f frontend-rs.yaml --dry-run=client ^XE7orQPY

Namespace version: ^OVCyyP4t

Example: ^R8OJbMcm

kubectl apply -f frontend-rs.yaml --dry-run=client -n dev ^SSV79jSi

With YAML output: ^KpgGSQu5

kubectl apply -f frontend-rs.yaml --dry-run=client -o yaml ^komE7PSl

This specifies which Kubernetes API version the ReplicaSet belongs to. Using the correct apiVersion ensures Kubernetes 
understands the object structure and fields correctly. ^Ys3LfLmC

apiVersion: apps/v1: ^gGhoq4Qu

This tells Kubernetes that you are creating a ReplicaSet object. A ReplicaSet ensures a specific number of identical Pods are 
always running. ^UfyXzLp9

kind: ReplicaSet ^v5ZedfAO

Metadata contains identifying information about the ReplicaSet, such as its name. Kubernetes uses this section to track and 
manage the object. ^OQNLJ6gx

metadata: ^F3e6i6yS

This is the name of the ReplicaSet. It uniquely identifies the ReplicaSet inside the namespace. ^fWyElvqN

metadata → name: frontend-rs: ^kUs3ylzD

This section defines the desired state of the ReplicaSet. Kubernetes continuously works to match actual state to this desired 
state. ^ZSxF98CN

spec: ^Vbg7JSzJ

This tells Kubernetes that four Pods should always be running. If any Pod fails, the ReplicaSet will automatically create a new one. ^SulaSKpN

spec → replicas: 4 ^7Il9cC7T

The selector tells the ReplicaSet which Pods it should monitor and control. It matches Pods based on labels. ^ZeatBGoP

spec → selector: ^I0o7hQI3

This defines the label-based filtering for selecting Pods. Only Pods with the matching label "app: frontend" are considered part of
this ReplicaSet. ^AY9DUJSj

spec → selector → matchLabels: ^oG7gK5o7

The Pod template describes how new Pods should be created by the ReplicaSet. It acts like a blueprint for each Pod. ^spqqONvj

spec → template: ^B4WJ8WPw

These labels are applied to every Pod created from this template. They must match the selector labels so the ReplicaSet can track
them. ^mac245Z7

template → metadata → labels: ^CqpkuHeu

This describes the actual Pod configuration. It contains details like containers, images, ports, and other runtime settings. ^2CKTTKYo

template → spec: ^mVHBKquU

This defines the list of containers that should run inside each Pod. Most Pods contain at least one container. ^5bzVPSxA

template → spec → containers: ^lEYpF5Pm

This assigns a name to the container inside the Pod. Kubernetes uses this name for logging, debugging, and referencing the 
container. ^RRxGiGcw

containers → name: nginx ^IM269qPx

This tells Kubernetes which Docker image to use for the container. Here it pulls the Nginx version 1.25 image from a container 
registry. ^Dw2ELqDZ

containers → image: nginx:1.25 ^l9HQ6kww

ReplicaSet
YAML file ^PSeSBnAo

Selector ^I96stwNC

Label:
    app:frontend ^vErWiKt0

POD3 ^AQgc03Ko

    app:frontend ^wSjAqwpz

POD2 ^DytXuuzF

POD1 ^s4DG5Zok

ReplicaSet 
commands ^s4FvsN2P

Label selectors are used to filter Kubernetes objects (Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments, Services) based on the labels they contain.
They are mainly used by:
> ReplicaSets
> Deployments
> Services
> Jobs
> CronJobs
> kubectl commands ^FtZbxP8z

Label Selectors in Kubernetes: ^pW9QGsSe

Label selectors decide:
> Which Pods a Deployment should control
> Which Pods a ReplicaSet should keep running
> Which Pods a Service should send traffic to
> Which Pods a NetworkPolicy should allow/deny
> Which Pods should be scaled by HPA
> Which Pods should be deleted during maintenance
> Which Pods monitoring tools should collect metrics from ^WhHqjfqL

Example of labels: ^RtqpWM5Z

app: frontend
env: dev
tier: backend
version: v1
region: asia ^46MH5z26

> Kubernetes does not group things by folder or hierarchy.
> Instead, it uses labels and selectors.
> Example: A Deployment needs to know which Pods belong to it → It identifies them using selectors. ^zq2gMJA1

Why do we use Label Selectors? ^PDwXno2B

1. Kubernetes must group objects logically: ^DdpbSEAK

> Services use label selectors to route requests to Pods.
> Example: Service selects all Pods with
-> app=frontend ^wQ7cjTmi

2. Services must know where to send traffic: ^q1iV5gxu

> Companies deploy multiple environments inside the same cluster.
> Labels separate them:
-> env=dev
-> env=qa
-> env=prod
> Services, Deployments, HPAs, NetworkPolicies use selectors to target only the intended Pods. ^tbrCr9zy

3. Workload separation (dev, test, prod environments) ^uIHNAldl

> During rolling update, Deployment creates a new ReplicaSet with new labels (ex: version=v2).
> The Service continues to route traffic only to stable Pods using selectors. ^eN0UHNYW

4. Zero downtime deployment (rolling updates): ^YEzj1IAI

Types of Label Selectors: ^sP8gVIrV

There are two types:
> Equality-based selectors
> Set-based selectors ^vz4nAjZu

1. Equality based selector: ^yvbeV5r1

> Default selector in kubernetes is known as equality based selector.
> These selectors select the key-value pairs from the labels.
> The equality based selector in YAML file is defined as "matchLabels" ^BZlqqR4Y

2. Set-based selector: ^kdpcEsfI

> Set-based selectors use set logic to match multiple values based on the condition/operator.
> Operators:
-> In
-> NotIn
-> Exists (just label key)
-> DoesNotExist (label must not exist) ^go4O5ERU

selector:
    matchExpressions:
      - key: app
        operator: In
        values:
            - frontend
            - backend ^l9Cc9oJg

In operator: ^Z9aY8q9r

template:
    metadata:
      name: <pod-name>
      labels:
        app: frontend/backend ^QhMS3i17

When using In operator, key and any one of the values of the selector should be same as 
key: value of templete. ^eAcYk1Ab

selector:
    matchExpressions:
      - key: app
        operator: NotIn
        values:
            - frontend
            - backend ^UtXEmO6S

NotIn operator: ^vJXvBYBa

template:
    metadata:
      name: <pod-name>
      labels:
        app: database ^UfwGeIdd

When using NotIn operator, key of the selector should be same as key the of templete 
of pods not values (values should not match) ^ghbcNqW3

selector:
    matchExpressions:
      - key: app
        operator: Exists ^oQEBDuFf

Exists operator: ^IECMjlM5

template:
    metadata:
      name: <pod-name>
      labels:
        app: database ^8uLB4nfa

When using Exists operator, key of the selector should be same as key the of templete. ^b3TdR5q5

selector:
    matchExpressions:
      - key: app
        operator: NotExist ^5eLxueTf

NotExist operator: ^xed4HnCP

template:
    metadata:
      name: <pod-name>
      labels:
        demo: database ^irS9UFR7

When using NotExists operator, key of the selector should be not be same as key the of templete. ^Ezgh03Pr

> A Deployment is a Kubernetes object used to manage and control stateless applications. It defines how many copies
  (Pods) of an application should run, which container image to use, and how updates should be rolled out.
> In simple terms, a Deployment ensures that your application is always running in the desired state, even if Pods crash,
  nodes fail, or updates are applied.
> A Deployment does not create Pods directly. It manages ReplicaSets, and ReplicaSets create and maintain Pods. ^5m6pjw2R

4. Deployment ^vaxKdhNf

How Deployment Works (Step-by-Step Flow): ^YLX7I0wi

1. When you create a Deployment, the following happens internally:
2. You submit a Deployment YAML file using kubectl apply.
3. The request goes to the API Server, which stores the Deployment configuration in etcd.
4. The Deployment Controller continuously watches the desired state defined in the Deployment.
5. The Deployment Controller creates a ReplicaSet that matches the Deployment configuration.
6. The ReplicaSet creates the required number of Pods.
7. The Scheduler assigns each Pod to an available node.
8. kubelet on each node pulls the container image and starts the container.
9. If a Pod crashes or a node fails:
10. ReplicaSet detects fewer running Pods.
11. New Pods are automatically created to match the desired count.
12. If you update the Deployment (for example, change image version):
-> A new ReplicaSet is created.
-> Old Pods are gradually terminated.
-> New Pods are gradually created (rolling update).
-> Zero or minimal downtime occurs.
> This continuous comparison of desired state vs actual state is the core idea behind Deployments. ^mz1ZVJr8

> Maintains the desired number of Pods
> Enables rolling updates with zero downtime
> Supports rollback to previous versions
> Automatically recreates failed Pods
> Scales applications up or down easily ^NYgqSoJi

Key Responsibilities of a Deployment: ^eFsCWz8P

Real-Life Applications of Deployment in Industry: ^XVusddPH

1. Web Applications: ^bCOQq5oC

> Most production web applications use Deployments.
> Deployments allow teams to update application versions without service interruption. ^1JDMwlMz

2. Microservices Architecture: ^eXM2jjWa

> In microservices-based systems, each service runs as a separate Deployment.
> Example:
-> user-service Deployment
-> payment-service Deployment
-> order-service Deployment
> Each service can be scaled and updated independently. ^XsVoRTdu

> Deployments are a core part of DevOps automation.
> Example flow:
-> Code pushed to Git
-> CI tool builds Docker image
-> Image tag updated in Deployment YAML
-> CD tool applies Deployment to Kubernetes
> This enables continuous delivery with controlled rollouts. ^dLqCUMzg

3. CI/CD Pipelines: ^ApOTKCnm

> Companies frequently release bug fixes and new features.
> Example:
-> Version v1 → v2 of an application
-> Deployment gradually replaces old Pods with new Pods
> If issues occur, teams can rollback instantly. ^vb4a4m9B

4. Rolling Updates in Production: ^ncHVEP0n

> Deployments automatically recover from failures.
> Example:
-> Pod crashes due to memory issue
-> Node goes down
> No manual intervention is needed to restore services. ^OxGB5McA

5. Auto Healing and Reliability: ^kEW0AIgk

Docker ^xyVv2uHC

Docker flow ^u9AmfOgb

Types of Applications ^TF6d0z0G

1. Standalone application ^bQtjpBTy

> A standalone application (or desktop application) runs independently on a single computer. It does not 
  require an internet connection or an external server to function once installed.
> It is installed locally on a user’s computer.
> It uses system resources such as CPU, RAM, and local files.
> It cannot be accessed remotely.
> Updates need to be done manually on each machine. ^ABa7vV4k

Examples: ^VA4vUKJR

> Microsoft Word, VLC Media Player, notepad, calculator. ^KzcsdADM

2. Web-based Application ^smicrUiH

> A web-based application runs on a web server and is accessed through a web browser using a URL. It does 
  not need installation on the user’s device.
> It is a type of application which requires stable internet connnection and are accessed using browser.
> It is accessible over HTTP or HTTPS.
> It can be hosted on cloud or on-premise servers.
> Easy to update since all changes happen on the server. ^yDMfNtRa

Examples: ^FpNhhOvh

> Jenkins, SonarQube, Grafana ^p9KfoyY6

3. Client–Server Application ^3Fj1jK4F

> A client–server application is one where the client sends a request, and the server processes it and sends 
  back a response. The key concept is that communication happens over a network.
> Clients communicate with servers using protocols like HTTP or TCP/IP.
> Can be two-tier (client ↔ database) or three-tier (client ↔ app server ↔ database).
> Scalable — multiple clients can interact with a single or multiple servers. ^rOtr9uDp

Examples: ^VuqTshdD

> Whatsapp, Flipkart, Instagram ^CgCNbCkK

Types of application architecture: ^Ah5yuCcK

1. Monolithic Architecture ^KD14CeUK

> A monolithic application is built as one single, large unit. All parts — the user interface, business logic, and database are 
  combined and deployed together as one application. ^UhriTliN

How it works (workflow): ^dqpZtriT

> A user sends a request (for example, logging in or buying an item).
> The entire application receives the request.
> Inside the same codebase, the request is processed through the user interface 
  logic, business logic, and database operations.
> The final response is sent back to the user. ^aZftbOjM

> An early version of Instagram — where photo uploads, comments, user authentication, 
  and notifications all existed in one single application. ^7msnuzXD

Example: ^u2iu4IcY

Disadvantages: ^etzzitq1

> If one small part fails, the whole system can crash.
> To make any change, you must rebuild and redeploy the entire application.
> Hard to scale specific parts — you can’t scale just “upload” or “login”.
> Code becomes messy and tightly coupled (dependent on each other) as it grows. ^cyMHDDCN

3. Microservices Architecture ^A8AJbAPk

> A microservices architecture is when an application is divided into many small, independent services, and each service 
  performs a specific function. ^3S2UkFO8

Each microservice: ^6xFTi8UF

> Has its own codebase
> Can be developed, tested, and deployed separately
> Often has its own small database
> Communicates with other services using APIs
> All these small services together form one large application. ^swvdj0eH

> A user performs an action — say, ordering an item on Amazon.
> The Order Service receives the request.
> It communicates with other services:
> Inventory Service to check stock.
> Payment Service to process the payment.
> Notification Service to send an email or SMS.
> Each service runs independently, can use different programming languages, and has its own database.
> The user receives the final result after all services complete their tasks. ^AV8PrFKB

How it works (workflow): ^37nB9GNf

2. 3-Tier Architecture: ^Ifcifdwa

Instead of keeping everything mixed, 3-tier architecture divides the app logically into three layers:
> Presentation Layer – handles the user interface (like HTML, React, or mobile app UI).
> Application Layer – handles business logic (like Java, Python, or Node.js code).
> Data Layer – handles database operations (like MySQL, PostgreSQL). ^aNDbwNXi

> A user performs an action on the presentation layer (for example, clicking “Login”).
> The request goes to the application layer, which checks credentials and logic.
> The application layer queries the data layer to verify the user.
> The result (success or failure) is sent back to the presentation layer for display. ^fRnXihGf

How it works (workflow): ^XVp1QXhS

Example: ^33fvsLZ7

A banking web portal:

> Frontend (React web page)
> Backend (Java Spring Boot server)
> Database (Oracle or MySQL) ^AZdabXGv

> Better structure — clear separation of responsibilities.
> Easier to maintain or replace one layer (e.g., new database or new UI).
> Still a single deployable unit, but more organized. ^0MH6QExq

How it improved over Monolithic: ^02Qjl9Va

Remaining problems: ^kfmEICNg

> All layers still belong to one big application, so redeployment affects all.
> Slightly slower due to multi-layer communication.
> Can’t scale individual features (In a 3-tier setup, sometimes you want to scale only one feature — say, 
  the cart feature, it is difficult to scale up or down without touching others.)
> If layers are tightly coupled (one layer dependent on other), small changes can break dependencies. ^TZu2OvJW

How it improved over 3-Tier: ^jLej3gOd

> Each service can be developed and deployed independently.
> If one service fails (like notifications), others still run.
> You can scale only what’s needed — if orders increase, add more order servers.
> Teams can use different programming languages or databases for different services.
> Faster innovation — small teams manage small pieces. ^eK9Y8GfW

> Managing and monitoring many services is complex.
> Needs solid DevOps tools (Docker, Kubernetes, 
  CI/CD).
> APIs between services must be well-designed.
> More network communication → slightly 
  more latency.
> Each service may need a different environment 
  (Java, Node, Python). ^7pyfrlgc

Challenges: ^laTr3Zdc

Example: ^gzHASxdb

In Amazon, there are separate services for:
> Cart
> Product
> Payment
> Notification
> Each one runs independently, can be deployed separately, and 
  even use a different programming language. ^RKpZJEAV

> Before we talk about Docker or containers, we must first understand what kind of applications 
  we’re trying to deploy and how they’re structured.
> Otherwise, we’ll never appreciate why Docker is useful or what problem it actually solves. ^zINkl7XQ

Purpose of Learning Application Types and Architectures: ^J1ho5EXW

Introduction to Containers ^W5E0FeZA

1. Physical Servers: ^w9jMz2Kp

> A physical server is a single, dedicated machine (computer) that runs one operating system directly on its 
  hardware. Applications are installed directly on this OS. ^fvIT62bj

> Imagine you have one physical server that runs a web application using Windows Server and another one 
  that runs a Java app on Linux. Each app needs a different OS, so you end up using separate servers. ^5Y4RSzN9

Example: ^OmnbWu4f

Problems: ^WmNecCLg

> Low resource utilization: The CPU, RAM, or storage may not be fully used, but they’re reserved for that 
                                 single app.
> High cost: Each new application may need a new physical server.
> Scalability issues: You can’t easily add or remove resources; you’d need to buy more hardware.
> Environment conflicts: Two apps needing different dependencies can’t easily coexist. ^fCZkSwU2

2. Virtual Machines (Virtualization): ^YrI3jW8m

> Virtualization is the technology that allows multiple operating systems to run on a single physical server 
  using a software layer called a hypervisor. Each of these operating systems runs inside its own Virtual 
  Machine (VM). ^NJuf3eFt

> Imagine you have one physical server that runs a web application using Windows Server and another one 
  that runs a Java app on Linux. Each app needs a different OS, so you end up using separate servers. ^oSYqQ2Mt

Example: ^lhslJZnO

> The hypervisor divides hardware resources (CPU, RAM, disk) among multiple VMs.
> Each VM runs its own guest OS, independent of the others.
> Applications run inside these VMs as if each one were a separate physical machine. ^ZYTJ2yya

How it works: ^t8vOBCyg

Suppose you have one physical server with 16 GB RAM. You can create three VMs:
> VM1: Linux + Web App
> VM2: Windows + Database
> VM3: Ubuntu + Testing Tools
Each acts like its own independent computer. ^IQYgv7k9

Example: ^3tk0X0fu

Advantages: ^WaW0Pof4

> Better utilization of a single server.
> You can run multiple OSes at once.
> Easier to isolate and manage applications. ^mvGhRgeJ

> Each VM needs its own full OS, which consumes memory and storage.
> Boot time is slow because every VM behaves like a full computer.
> When hundreds of microservices or apps need to run, VMs become heavy and inefficient. ^b8Z2XpuN

Limitations: ^1jCYz1Ej

Hardware ^8QRFgu0X

Hypervisor ^cEW8IpQs

VM-1 ^LZ7tmE6F

VM-2 ^IMTFFhNQ

Application
(Linux) ^5xlW1n9c

Application
(Windows) ^V9pyNo7h

Configuration:
512 GB ----> Storage
8 GB ---> RAM ^jzBQA6Op

Configuration:
30 GB ----> Storage
3 GB ---> RAM ^Pq5gwX8W

Configuration:
30 GB ----> Storage
2 GB ---> RAM ^U1oGysd4

Host OS ^DevPoNZO

3. Containers: ^XUf7iEiH

> Containers are lightweight, standalone environments that package an application with all its required dependencies, libraries, and configurations 
  — but instead of running a full OS, they share the host operating system’s kernel. ^O9mmode8

How it works: ^16rq9Owo

> Containers isolate applications from each other, but they all share the same OS kernel.
> This makes them faster and more resource-efficient than VMs.
> Containers start instantly because there’s no need to boot a full OS. ^kXyeaufG

Example: ^w2CcU1Sm

On a single Linux machine, you can run:
> Container 1: Node.js app
> Container 2: Python API
> Container 3: Java service
All three share the same Linux kernel but have their own isolated runtime and libraries. ^vZ6G6tig

> Very lightweight and fast.
> No need for a full OS inside each container.
> Easily portable between systems — works the same everywhere.
> Perfect for microservices architecture, where each service runs in its own container. ^DVhcaX5C

Advantages: ^eAWMXflq

Limitations: ^QxLP5Um6

> Containers depend on the host OS kernel — a Linux container can’t natively run on Windows OS and vice versa (without using special runtimes).
> If host OS crashes, then all the containers will collapse. ^k7VIaNVq

Hardware ^1nkwgaRj

Container Engine ^vVFoo2IT

Application-1 ^WrVMQ8nN

Host OS ^yLkzjiGZ

Application-2 ^0Nvv3jeH

Container-1 ^mAaqtn3M

Container-2 ^MGYOWe0N

> A Docker container runs exactly one main process. As long as this process continues running, the container 
  stays alive. When this process finishes or exits, the container stops.
> So the lifetime of a container depends on the command or application running inside it. ^NEhfMTwZ

What is a Container Process? ^Hs7llhiC

> A short-running container is one that runs a task and exits immediately after completing it. These 
  containers are not expected to stay active for a long time. They behave like running a single command in a 
  Linux terminal. ^2jQ6fBKf

Short-Running Containers ^ABHfdRaC

Examples of short-running cases: ^0GfrJLom

> Running a script that ends
> Running a simple command like echo, ls, cat, or pwd
> Running a build process like mvn package or npm install and exiting ^rwREwnEn

Example command: ^9fu4XLtx

docker run alpine echo "Hello World" ^tPDcpJ6Y

What happens: ^O63beQE3

> The container starts.
> The command echo "Hello World" runs.
> Because the command finishes immediately, the main process exits.
> The container stops.
> Short-running containers do not need -d because they don’t run long enough to be useful in background 
  mode. -it is only needed if you want to interact with the command manually (like opening a shell environment). ^GLpUuYnh

> A long-running container is designed to keep running continuously until manually stopped. These containers 
  run services and applications like servers, databases, or applications that wait for connections. ^1HGiuWhe

Long-Running Containers ^7uVD0XRF

Examples of long-running cases: ^GYw1P4YT

> Nginx or Apache HTTP server
> Tomcat or Spring Boot applications
> MySQL, PostgreSQL, Redis, or MongoDB databases
> Message queue systems like Kafka or RabbitMQ ^eyWcIkho

Example command: ^gU8R9lhp

docker run -d nginx ^IWWYkMAG

What happens: ^0ssDDcxN

> Here, the nginx process continues running and does not exit automatically. 
> Since it keeps the container alive, the container runs indefinitely until stopped manually.
> Long-running containers are typically used with the -d flag so they run in the background without blocking 
  the terminal. ^GqfUNzpZ

Sometimes you want to:
> Run a container like a service (background mode)
> Still have the option to enter it later
> In such cases, use both:
> This keeps the container alive even though no command is actively running inside. Later, you can enter it 
  using: ^vDPhWRW2

Using Both: -dit ^8n4KHiF0

docker run -dit ubuntu ^S1rXDXRC

docker exec -it <container_id> bash ^ypWdYub5

> Docker is a containerization platform that allows you to package an application and all its dependencies 
  into a single unit called a container, so it runs consistently across any environment.
> It is a free and open source platform where we will run the application in the form of containers. ^AME2wAdX

Introduction to Docker ^VwLmRgin

History of Docker: ^KGBXdK3s

2010: ^oSbstcDq

Soloman Hykes invented dotCloud where he was able to create containers. ^6S04hDQe

2013: ^VTUGnnjr

Docker was invented as a free and open source platform where we can create containers for storing
for storing the applications. ^8es6iUnx

2014: ^jMJ9O7vi

Docker Inc launched "dockerHub" for storing images for the containers. Along with this, dockerswarm 
and docker compose got introduced. ^vQeT82cs

Features: ^s14LgA4f

> Lightweight – Shares the host OS kernel, uses fewer resources than virtual machines.
> Portable – Runs consistently across any environment or operating system.
> Isolated – Each container runs independently without affecting others.
> Consistent – Ensures the same behavior across development, testing, and production.
> Fast – Containers start and stop within seconds.
> Modular – Supports microservices; each service runs in its own container.
> Versioned – Images can be tagged and rolled back easily.
> Integrated – Works smoothly with DevOps tools and cloud platforms.
> Reusable – Uses pre-built images from Docker Hub to save setup time.
> Secure – Provides process isolation and resource control for safety.
> Free and open source.
> Supports containerization technique.
> It has dockerHub for storing images.
> It is easy to use
> It can be integrated with other tools like jenkins. ^Cj64Y4XH

Docker architecture: ^Nj82C7jr

DOCKER CLI ^aARh49uF

DOCKER HOST ^yA6je5L6

DOCKER REGISTRY ^Z4mBlcHO

Docker Daemon ^irkDfAtC

Docker follows a client–server architecture.
It has three main components:

> Docker Client: The tool you use to interact with Docker (through commands).
> Docker Daemon (Server): The background service that does all the work — builds, runs, and manages 
                                  containers.
> Docker Registry: A storage place where Docker images are stored (like Docker Hub). ^hgxivN4T

Docker file
(to create our own image) ^l1faRLDV

1) Docker Client (Docker host): ^Pou7XZzS

> It is the command line interface for Docker.
> You use it to run commands like: ^Ilk0Z78D

> The client communicates with the Docker Daemon using REST API over HTTP/HTTPS. ^uTtTnJ2d

Example: ^F4i2v2KQ

> When you type:
> The Docker Client sends this request to the Daemon to find the Nginx image, create a container, and 
  start it. ^Kovp8bKk

docker run nginx ^Mmkz4NMg

docker pull

docker run

docker build

docker push ^mpVjBY1P

Responsibilities of the Daemon: ^15qSV2GD

2) Docker Daemon (Docker engine): ^v8KKmpOL

> The core service running in the background.
> It does all heavy work like:
-> Building images
-> Running containers
-> Managing networks and volumes
> It listens for commands from the Docker Client and executes them. ^AKh1Izh9

> Receives API requests from the client
> Pulls images from the registry if needed
> Builds and runs containers
> Manages all container lifecycle operations ^zzdw5ghL

3) Docker Images: ^Lnv8x3gT

> A Docker image is a read-only template used to create containers.
> It contains:
-> Application code
-> Runtime environment
-> Dependencies
> It contains instructions to run the app.
> Images are built using a Dockerfile, which contains step-by-step instructions.
> If any changes or modification happens to the existing image then it will be considered as "new image". ^lgsbILwX

4) Docker Containers: ^5d2rwtcs

> A Docker container is a running instance of an image.
> It’s isolated from other containers and runs on the host machine using shared OS kernel features 
  (namespaces and cgroups).
> Containers can be:
-> Started
-> Stopped
-> Paused
-> Deleted ^93mxnsfn

> A Docker Registry stores and distributes Docker images.
> The most popular public registry is Docker Hub.
> You can also create private registries for your organization.
> We can get pre-built images like ubuntu, nginx.....
> If required we can push our own images into the registry. ^1pRENWzk

5) Docker Registry: ^j40LoTH2

Containers ^2wa5ArOg

Images ^xzXRT8JX

Pre-built
Images ^bCWgGmMw

> nginx
> ubuntu ^vSDzKIQ6

nginx ^UGCH5Rqf

nginx ^M3S6dZwd

own image ^2z7WlU2b

Own Image ^qKZ0UPfi

docker pull
docker run
docker build
docker push ^xAmO52t5

1. Podman: 
> A daemonless, Docker-compatible container engine that runs containers securely without needing root access.
> Docker has a more mature ecosystem, better tooling (like Docker Compose), and simpler setup for beginners.

2. containerd:
> A lightweight container runtime used mainly under the hood by Kubernetes for managing containers.
> Docker provides a complete platform (CLI, build system, registry tools), while containerd is low-level and 
   harder for direct use.

3. CRI-O:
> A Kubernetes-native runtime built specifically for running containers inside Kubernetes clusters.
> Docker is more versatile, works outside Kubernetes, and is easier for standalone development and testing.

4. LXC/LXD:
> System-level container tools that act like lightweight virtual machines running full Linux OS instances.
> Docker focuses on application-level containers, which are faster, smaller, and better suited for modern app 
deployment.

5. rkt (Rocket):
> A secure and standards-based container runtime created by CoreOS (now deprecated).
> Docker is actively maintained, more popular, and fully integrated with modern DevOps workflows, unlike the 
  discontinued rkt. ^QRKOW81e

Alternative tools: ^TcRVvj8l

Basic commands: ^4bVvdZ36

> docker pull: ^vtVQL4RU

This command is used to pull the image from Docker Hub ^KBofHHqC

docker pull <image_name>:<tag> ^W9W5zTPV

syntax: ^aRnUUMoQ

docker pull nginx:latest ^dMpcTNo7

Example: ^8fws1odY

> docker images: ^ARU5hXac

This command is used to list all images available locally on your system. ^pURf5ksb

docker images [flags] ^IZ9QaNnF

syntax: ^IuRmjylh

docker images
docker images -a ^EWyssTWw

Example: ^sqivuKYg

→ Shows all images (including intermediate 
    layers). ^Mw71hSZf

> docker rmi: ^J0F15N38

This command is used to remove one or more images from your system. ^gn9GMEu4

docker rmi <image_id> [flags] ^oMJ9bzOz

syntax: ^2IFNc6GD

docker rmi <image_id> 
docker rmi <image_id> -f ^VuS8fek3

Example: ^zluYdn88

→ Force remove an image (even if it’s 
    used by a container). ^sprmUCTx

> docker build: ^nGfDERAv

This command is used to build a Docker image from a Dockerfile. ^ps818oBJ

docker build -t <image_name>:<tag> <path> ^Bbvw25y0

syntax: ^xYWXfwry

docker build -t myapp:v1 . ^Sf8X2PiU

Example: ^6baOjM84

-t → Tags the image with a name and optional tag (version).
. → location of Dockerfile (current folder) ^MRs2l8Ne

> docker tag: ^VY6MlKTi

This command is used to assign a new name or version tag to an existing
image. ^aIkEi6I0

docker tag <source_image>:<tag> <target_image>:<new_tag> ^1Ahy95ng

syntax: ^ZIwpaLhF

docker tag myapp:v1 myapp:latest ^ZBjLOW56

Example: ^Ho4L76XN

If you already built an image myapp:v1, you can tag it as myapp:latest 
for convenience. ^WuxakceB

> docker run: ^HOkkHej7

This command is used to create and run a container from an image. ^GsViAhMq

docker run [flag] <image_name> ^xprY8fzl

syntax: ^MmbzvCcg

docker run nginx ^FDko82XY

Example: ^eB2X2gAh

Common Flags:
> -d → Run in detached (background) mode.
> -it → Interactive + terminal mode (useful for shells).
> --name <container_name> → Assigns a name to the container.
> -p <host_port>:<container_port> → Maps a container port to host.
> -v <host_path>:<container_path> → Mounts a volume. ^MhP3WAD5

> docker stop: ^G6dS3qYP

This command is used to stop a running container. ^0AHCwLsZ

docker stop <container_id>/<container_name> ^KFtLaVO1

syntax: ^kxTELX9e

docker stop mynginx ^NiMZ5CtI

Example: ^iuYyzyIJ

> docker start: ^DMiNNRDt

This command is used to start a stopped container. ^qJmGeOMy

docker start <container_id>/<container_name> ^aw7UTQYn

syntax: ^M9vIkq8o

docker start mynginx ^Kk1ocZhP

Example: ^eXvrVf1p

Flags:
> -a → Attach to the container’s output. ^soWYFJWq

> docker restart: ^Gk7a09lP

This command is used to restart a container. ^m8DOKNZh

docker restart <container_id> ^UTCfBSta

syntax: ^Ijk80vtv

docker restart mynginx ^rw1pOg1N

Example: ^6Kq2RAio

> docker rm: ^nOL3KkJj

This command is used to remove a stopped container. ^XxYKmML0

docker rm <container_id>/<container_name> ^GXvgDG5G

syntax: ^8XYWx89A

docker rm mynginx ^x5FT7G5Q

Example: ^AB5Q2cX9

Flags:
> -f → Force remove a running container.
> -v → Remove associated volumes. ^3tn4tAyg

> docker exec: ^dwkWDfwA

This command is used to execute a command inside a running container. ^X2DdXGt3

docker exec [options] <container_id> <command> ^6fprlpai

syntax: ^JYPC9f6z

docker exec -it mynginx bash ^y0iJcD8K

Example: ^wawBZLxe

Flags:
> -i → Keep STDIN open.
> -t → Allocate a terminal. ^KngOzFpC

> docker logs: ^Q9wHyxf1

This command is used to display the logs of a container. ^eJIM7ph4

docker logs <container_id> ^gaEN23Ak

syntax: ^IbVF2NNn

docker logs mynginx ^sf5aGln9

Example: ^cLQOQglY

Flags:
> -f → Follow log output (live view).
> --tail <number> → Show only the last N lines. ^Ae8PVUQa

> docker stop $(docker ps -q): ^T2NoJZ3S

This command is used to stop all the containers. ^Fb3em9ZR

Explanation:
> docker ps -q → lists only container IDs of running containers.
> $(...) → passes those IDs to docker stop.
> So this stops every running container on your system. ^nptPQKjB

> docker start $(docker ps -aq): ^DLNhIEI1

This command is used to start all the containers. ^jRWGYnDX

Explanation:
> docker ps -aq → lists all container IDs (including stopped ones).
> So this starts every container present on the system. ^oOkiqlFI

> docker ps: ^RkcFt4Fn

This command is used to list running containers. ^5SZOycbn

docker ps ^pW0OdrYA

syntax: ^yLfAwHTz

docker ps ^RvKvcFtR

Example: ^K8viDs7b

Flags:
> -a → Lists all containers (running + stopped).
> -q → Shows only container IDs. ^keV6z403

> docker rm $(docker ps -aq): ^yut1yGqs

This command is used to delete all the containers. ^EbQeDmgA

Explanation:
> Removes all containers, whether they are running or stopped.
> If some containers are still running, you may need to force 
  remove them.
> Force remove (even running containers):
> docker rm -f $(docker ps -aq) ^2patHNWL

> docker rmi $(docker images -q): ^tzY47Gbp

This command is used to remove all the images. ^HOf0uZ3V

Explanation:
> Removes all containers, whether they are running or stopped.
> If some containers are still running, you may need to force 
  remove them.
> Force remove (even running containers):
> docker rm -f $(docker ps -aq) ^nvE44jaA

> docker rmi -f $(docker images -q): ^jiigUVIN

This command is used to force remove all the 
images. ^OQz3eBsf

Explanation:
> Adds the -f flag (force) to remove even those images currently 
  used by containers.
> Use this with caution, as it will delete everything. ^yyU74ZV6

> docker image prune -a: ^AdgRvrDn

This command is used to clean up unused images. ^U68a61Ap

Explanation:
> Deletes only unused images (not associated with any container).
> Safer than removing all images blindly.
> You’ll be prompted for confirmation — use -f to skip it.
> Example: docker image prune -a -f ^YGUL9f9f

> docker login: ^biTRwTHu

This command is used to authenticate your local Docker CLI with a 
container registry (like Docker Hub). ^vQXSQfrD

docker login <registry_url> ^71dfGtNP

syntax: ^WI8bLZMD

docker login ^1wFQHTHd

Example: ^FwpXi6cP

Flags:
> -u <username>: Specifies Docker Hub username.
> -p <password>: Specifies password (not recommended directly for 
                     security).
Note: If the registry URL is not given → Defaults to Docker Hub. ^4iGHAsqQ

> docker tag: ^IrdPnT3l

> This command is used to create a new tag for an existing image.
  It essentially renames or re-labels a local image so it can be pushed to 
  a registry.
> This command also used to create a new tag for an existing image, 
   usually to prepare it for pushing to a remote registry like Docker Hub 
   or ECR. Tagging does not copy the image — it just adds another 
   reference name to the same image ID. ^mSOtfrAv

docker tag <source_image> <target_image> ^SDl4nSdo

syntax: ^Ah4jCKXs

docker tag petclinic:latest sumukha47/petclinic:1.0 ^RDyDxmV1

Example: ^Q1WsRAB7

What It Does:
> Creates a new reference to the same image ID.
> Used mainly before pushing images to registries.
> Doesn’t create a new image — only adds a new "label". ^GCF9qEEN

> docker push: ^Xu1SApDm

This command is used to upload a tagged image from your local system 
to a container registry like Docker Hub. ^E6LR60tA

docker push <image_name> ^TFKW1J8C

syntax: ^m5l07yaH

docker push sumukha47/petclinic:1.0 ^Dm3OlJfr

Example: ^YMP7o4be

Important Notes:
> You must be logged in using docker login.
> The image must be properly tagged with your Docker Hub username.
> Push uploads image layers to the remote registry. ^ISADlgFv

> docker rename: ^CZTrIGFb

This command is used to rename an existing container without 
stopping or recreating it. It simply updates the container’s name in 
Docker’s metadata. ^q9nl43gb

docker rename <old_container_name> <new_container_name> ^5uHrEeKP

syntax: ^3Uci6sZC

docker rename petclinic_container petclinic-app ^0wY50GRe

Example: ^0fyANVjh

Important Notes:
> The container must already exist.
> Rename does not change the image name — only the container name.
> Running, stopped, or paused containers can be renamed. ^FUUHmBK3

> docker inspect: ^r9SrTCCh

This command is used to get detailed JSON-formatted metadata 
about a Docker object. ^0BK0UTMj

docker inspect [OPTIONS] <Container_name_or_id> ^IRLl5EV4

syntax: ^XHdJ9eua

docker inspect my-container
docker inspect nginx:latest
docker inspect --format='{{.Size}}' nginx:latest ^Vvwil4gO

Example: ^DfZ3gbS9

Flags:
> --format: Instead of parsing the huge JSON, you can use Go 
              template syntax to extract info. ^1f97MMAA

docker inspect [OPTIONS] <image_name_or_id> ^DnPMbIz4

> A Dockerfile is a text file that contains step-by-step instructions on how to build a Docker image.
> Without a Dockerfile, you would have to manually:
-> Install dependencies
-> Configure the environment
-> Copy code
-> Run setup commands
> A Dockerfile automates all this. ^K7aTSNrR

Dockerfile: ^lsDLIOPJ

Basic Example: ^ruOIOsNY

FROM ubuntu:latest
WORKDIR /home/app
COPY . /home/app
RUN  apt update && apt install openjdk-17-jdk -y
CMD ["echo", "hello"] ^j2fxvpsT

> To build image using Dockerfile: ^Jt6NSYZC

docker build -t sample:v1.0 . ^NLmVj04z

> To run the container from the image: ^kt8zGdAd

docker run -it sample ^a9f5EFQ5

Common instructions in a Dockerfile: ^7FsMZDJE

1. FROM: ^NSmgVr2I

Defines the base image from which your Docker image will be built. ^Ph0nJyag

Syntax: ^CyjvRfQr

FROM <image>:<tag> ^iA45HtOE

Example: ^B5W9uaHJ

FROM ubuntu:22.04 ^zZbe1k97

Meaning: Build your image on top of Ubuntu 22.04. ^8Ve0W0ty

2. WORKDIR: ^ruBIfROC

Sets the working directory inside the image. All following commands run from this location. ^wKc93EIk

Syntax: ^eOqnQjVc

WORKDIR /path ^MDzsvFZ9

Example: ^uFH9yegh

WORKDIR /app ^fOCXNWBQ

Meaning: If /app does not exist, Docker will create it. ^Ve4DpCIB

3. COPY: ^L04fpMUs

Copies files from your host system into the image. ^A1YIuY36

Syntax: ^KydkwVnr

COPY <source> <destination> ^wa1Cdtxr

Example: ^jZfffPWw

COPY . /app ^brZ49Nf7

Meaning: Copy everything from your current directory to /app inside the image. ^lXU20mVz

4. ADD: ^RSNl7R6V

Similar to COPY but supports URLs and automatically extracts tar archives. ^naEyNIdA

Syntax: ^esModSfr

ADD <source> <destination> ^QMbmDOhl

Example (URL): ^Mn6E1WxV

ADD https://example.com/file.tar.gz /opt/ ^sWR3oYfr

Note: Use COPY unless you specifically need ADD features. ^mdW8Ud8v

Example (local tar): ^9p7PXLS7

ADD app.tar.gz /app ^bqvKJypc

5. RUN: ^TNs34TDd

Executes commands at build time (not when the container runs). ^0kXzyxuT

Syntax: ^8bvj04kZ

RUN <command> ^oR8MkCUR

Example: ^ivFUkvH2

RUN apt update && apt install -y openjdk-17-jdk ^Vn9L8Uu2

Meaning: Installs Java inside the image during build. ^WchIQOT8

6. CMD: ^1DdJcreB

Specifies the default command that runs when the container starts. ^urK9E7jl

Syntax: ^YpuclE36

CMD ["executable", "arg1", "arg2"] ^4VVUVtaN

Example: ^iPoooKcD

CMD ["python3", "app.py"] ^cyKes9zH

Note:
> Only one CMD instruction is allowed.
> CMD is overridden if you pass a command in docker run. ^c1R27mWw

7. ENTRYPOINT: ^1Vve3rBG

Similar to CMD but cannot be easily overridden. It defines the "main executable" of the 
container. ^fGxTvhJc

Syntax: ^oG8wUAOf

ENTRYPOINT ["executable", "arg"] ^YHCxhZJi

Example: ^6hni1aBO

ENTRYPOINT ["java", "-jar", "app.jar"] ^A3Ser1RM

Meaning: The container will always run this Java application. ^Zof7RiKm

8. EXPOSE: ^TgJCBveU

Documents which port the container listens on. It does not publish the port by itself. ^CWYfNep8

Syntax: ^hJFsBI6I

EXPOSE <port> ^iPcBLGQU

Example: ^1d8rpcg5

EXPOSE 8080 ^De3viWdY

> To actually map port:
> docker run -p 8080:8080 image ^HqfoAQAn

9. ENV: ^r5rHmz6a

Sets environment variables inside the container. ^xAmxtY2y

Syntax: ^HGAGS1KX

ENV KEY=value ^K00MjvUp

Example: ^G1oj6ZzJ

ENV APP_ENV=production ^BZBkbMn4

Meaning: Inside the container:
           echo $APP_ENV
           production ^hnIZDyTZ

10. VOLUME: ^CVsURVfy

Used to provide a dedicated storage for storing the data of the containers This data will 
stay even if the container gets deleted. ^7ne4kMxd

Syntax: ^vZ37Rm4t

VOLUME ["/path"] ^K4yjmQnJ

Example: ^cvtswlz8

VOLUME ["/var/lib/mysql"] ^vs6wAllX

Meaning: This directory will store data even after the container is deleted. ^iIoS10XQ

11. USER: ^QPC3j9Hl

It is used to change the user under which the commands run. ^AXNxgkSl

Syntax: ^k7SNe5zJ

USER <username or UID> ^ux4Lxxz8

Example: ^C5vraTsc

USER appuser ^tF9cMMha

Meaning: Commands now run as appuser instead of root. ^OpbcBmBu

12. LABEL: ^B4qsM5hB

Adds metadata to your image. ^2lC1N13L

Syntax: ^Y7IB96b6

LABEL key=value ^llJbyb0l

Example: ^3L2nwKCV

LABEL maintainer="sumukha" ^8ysBgzUq

13. ARG: ^cYfAr0lH

Defines variables available only during the build stage. ^TwCgQxWO

Syntax: ^UBMTWN8g

ARG <name>=<default> ^ybXrTXMm

Example: ^nnGmVFSd

ARG VERSION=1.0
Using ARG inside Dockerfile: ENV APP_VERSION=$VERSION ^mOeRrRkR

14. HEALTHCHECK: ^oCrqVqI4

This command is used to check the health of the container. ^q5rRLCy3

Syntax: ^H0MH4fFZ

HEALTHCHECK CMD <command> ^bCM8uEHv

Example: ^xZyEwYLs

HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=5s --retries=3 \ 
  CMD curl -f http://localhost:8080/actuator/health || exit 1 ^sOpmIeMZ

Meaning: If the command fails, Docker marks the container as unhealthy. ^AH64tX8z

15. SHELL: ^po4txOnz

It is used to specify the shell to be used inside the container. ^yswD4CAL

Syntax: ^EdMMJR1I

SHELL ["shell", "option"] ^02JJCN9x

Example: ^tyeSnOMo

SHELL ["/bin/bash", "-c"] ^49HfHob8

note: 
> /bin/bash → the shell you are using
> -c tells bash to run the command you pass next. ^FGg0vsqF

Example for spring-petclinic: ^roZnbdtL

> docker build -t petclinic:latest .
> docker run -d -p 80:8080 --name petclinic petclinic:latest
> docker login
> docker tag petclinic:latest sumukha200147/spring-petclinic:latest
> docker push sumukha200147/spring-petclinic:latest ^qLq1UIjt

Example for static-web-hosting: ^9eenJzLb

To push the Docker images to DockerHub ^iaoQf9Gd

1) Go to a browser and search for "Docker Hub" and login.
2) On the homepage you will be getting an option called as "Visit catalog now" click on it.
3) Then in the sidebar you will be getting an option called as "Repositories" click on it.
4) Here you will be getting a button called as "Create a Repository" click on it.

    and then click on create. This is how you create your own repository.

Multi-stage Dockerfile: ^yop362bZ

> A multi-stage Dockerfile is a Dockerfile that contains multiple FROM 
  instructions, where each stage is used for a different purpose (build, test, 
  run, etc.), and only the final output is copied into the final image.
> Each stage starts fresh because FROM creates a new build layer. ^UFroZTjV

Why Do We Use Multi-Stage Dockerfiles in Production? ^8QpbNakL

1. Reduced Image Size (Major Benefit): ^d5NOLaxX

> Development tools (Maven, Node, JDK, compilers, etc.) make the image huge.
> In production you only need:
-> JRE (not JDK)
-> Final artifact (JAR/HTML bundle)
> Multi-stage removes unnecessary tools. ^377voZVU

2. Cleaner CI/CD Pipelines: ^GQuwVoy0

> Build, test, run inside the same Dockerfile.
> No need to install Maven/Node on Jenkins agents. ^OpEYqn04

> Smaller image → faster push/pull from Docker Hub or ECR. ^IcQMXAs3

3. Faster Deployment: ^khdBB2m8

> No build tools in production = fewer vulnerabilities.
> No source code leaked inside the final image. ^a1azfekz

4. Improved Security: ^1pINOtWh

# Stage 1: Build the JAR using Maven
FROM maven:3.8.5-eclipse-temurin-17 AS build
WORKDIR /app
COPY pom.xml /app
COPY src /app/src
RUN mvn clean package -DskipTests

# Stage 2: Create lightweight runtime image
FROM eclipse-temurin:17-jdk-alpine
WORKDIR /spring-petclinic
COPY --from=build /app/target/*.jar app.jar
EXPOSE 8080
CMD ["java", "-jar", "app.jar"] ^yotiJEjl

FROM maven:3.8.5-eclipse-temurin-17
WORKDIR /app

COPY pom.xml /app
COPY src /app/src
RUN mvn clean package -DskipTests
EXPOSE 8080
CMD mv ./target/*.jar app.jar && java -jar app.jar ^iRlbgItU

Copies the src into the working directory and within that inside src folder. ^zZkCz0NQ

Container port 8080 (default port for spring-petclinic) ^6gCqVgVe

# Build stage

FROM maven:3.8.5-eclipse-temurin-17 AS build
WORKDIR /app
COPY pom.xml /app
COPY src /app/src
ENV port=8080
RUN mvn clean package -DskipTests

# Deploy stage
FROM eclipse-temurin:21.0.9_10-jre-ubi9-minimal
ADD https://github.com/Sumukha47/spring-petclinic/blob/main/README.md /home/Docker_User/README.md
ARG jar_file="spring-petclinic.jar"
RUN useradd -m -s /bin/bash Docker_User
RUN chown Docker_User:Docker_User /home/Docker_User/README.md
USER Docker_User
WORKDIR /home/Docker_User
SHELL [ "/bin/bash","-c" ]
COPY --from=build /app/target/*.jar /home/Docker_User/${jar_file}
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=30s --start-period=5s --retries=3 \
 CMD curl -f http://localhost:8080/actuator/health || exit 1
EXPOSE ${port}
VOLUME [ "/data" ]
ENTRYPOINT [ "java", "-jar"]
CMD [ "spring-petclinic.jar" ]
 ^CVSirAQh

Example 2: ^bl1sbAvx

Example 1: ^cqpvwqzc

Note:  If we have multiple Dockerfiles (like: Dockerfile.dev, Dockerfile.test ......)
        then to specifically run a Dockerfile, we use the command: ^u3DBldIg

docker build -f Dockerfile.dev -t myapp-dev .
docker build -f Dockerfile.test -t myapp-test . ^qUHVjqkM

Docker Volumes: ^gFDTNNOa

> Docker Volumes are a mechanism used to store data outside a container’s writable layer so that the data 
  can persist even if the container is deleted, recreated, or updated.
> A Docker Volume is a folder created and managed by Docker where container data is stored permanently. ^rud5d99t

Why Docker Alone Cannot Store Persistent Data: ^1cKNLBNz

> Works until the container crashes, is upgraded, or removed.
> Backups must be manually handled. ^XFAaGn3l

1. Container filesystems are temporary: ^9LJOgaiW

> Each container has a writable layer.
> When the container is deleted or recreated, the writable layer is deleted.
> Result: all data stored inside the container is lost. ^tzoRXwaz

2. Storing data inside the container filesystem is unreliable: ^teE2ITSo

3. Bind mounts create dependency on the host system: ^aiTJ1ktn

> Path differences across machines cause portability issues.
> Host files may be exposed to container risks. ^en7riVNM

Docker volumes commands: ^CyxHwVlm

1. docker volume create: ^mlvx1uAA

Creates a Docker-managed volume. ^KyOk6cip

docker volume create [OPTIONS] [VOLUME_NAME] ^4otSC5sy

Syntax: ^P6GXYisb

Common Flags: ^G8H34CKd

docker volume create --name mydb-volume ^CY7S1oF2

Example: ^oriTEJyH

With options: ^NIxPDcQG

docker volume create --driver local -o device=/dev/sdb -o type=ext4 dbvolume ^aecrs9yc

2. docker volume ls: ^tYA2tANN

Lists all available volumes on the system. ^JzAFhSxc

docker volume ls [OPTIONS] ^GFePf2m9

Syntax: ^gSP6dbKD

-q: Quiet output (only list volume names)
--filter: Filter results (labels, dangling, name) ^LHnLp9Zp

Common Flags: ^a2P75GxP

docker volume ls ^TzdyedSI

List all volumes: ^zgW9t7Cy

List only volume names: ^z9mTXNnr

docker volume ls -q ^kzpJD9QV

Filter dangling (unused) volumes: ^LBTzHb6I

3. docker volume inspect: ^68bpTmKL

Shows detailed information about a specific volume (mount path, driver, labels, 
usage). ^33iJPur3

docker volume inspect [OPTIONS] VOLUME_NAME ^d3uYMHkB

Example: ^OOFgEFQ9

4. docker volume rm: ^PCM26cq3

Deletes one or more volumes. ^er1gDedt

docker volume rm [OPTIONS] VOLUME_NAME... ^Fyl83tFi

Syntax: ^T25vpRmX

-f / --force: Remove even if a container is using it ^J9tbEIim

Common Flags: ^Pt41iIMd

Example: ^ROXo2wVW

Force delete: ^0Mwh2EHq

5. docker volume prune: ^2Qz5tqHq

Removes all unused volumes to free disk space. ^2ZZ6HAIN

docker volume prune [OPTIONS] ^yinAOxsE

Syntax: ^eT9l2zp9

-f/--force: Skip confirmation prompt
--filter: Apply conditions (ex: remove only volumes older than X hours) ^HWjtoCQo

Common Flags: ^k4HKVFsf

docker volume prune ^A0MPENxW

Example: ^jk0BXr1C

Without confirmation: ^nKafGYPH

docker volume prune -f ^sJUOVEew

Filtering example: ^9G6IHFtV

docker volume prune --filter "label=env=dev" ^fwZ1xq2p

> Bind mounting is another way of attaching storage to a Docker container — but instead of using a 
  Docker-managed volume, it directly links a folder from your host machine to a path inside the container.
> So whatever exists (or gets created) in that folder on the container also appears in the host 
  folder — and vice-versa. ^2XxzjDsD

Bind mounting: ^cWf3g0BY

Meaning: ^x0JDmMC6

Syntax: ^mSFynJmP

docker run -v /path/on/host:/path/in/container IMAGE ^fT9Urza9

or using newer syntax: ^QGXmbC8r

docker run --mount type=bind,source=/path/on/host,target=/path/in/container IMAGE ^w48IAFUG

Example: ^HA5CcBxa

Suppose you have a folder on your machine:
> C:\project\website   (Windows)
                or
> /home/user/website  (Linux/Mac)
> Run: docker run -d -v /home/user/website:/usr/share/nginx/html nginx ^jVNU1Dc4

> /home/user/website → host folder
> /usr/share/nginx/html → Nginx web directory inside container
> Now:
> If you edit files in /home/user/website, they immediately update inside the running container.
> No need to rebuild the image again and again. ^SrUNNtk8

1) Local development: When you're developing an application (Node.js, React, Spring Boot static files, Web 
                           hosting), bind mounts allow you to modify code on your laptop and instantly see the 
                           result inside the container. (“Live sync between host and container.”)
2) Sharing config or sensitive files: Docker should use host machine config files (SSH keys, credentials, SSL 
                                            certs, config.json) without copying them into the image.
3) Avoid rebuilding images during testing: Every time you change code, you shouldn’t build the image again. 
                                                   Bind mount solves this. ^DMuUeasI

Why bind mounts are used? ^jJSDTxcE

--driver: Specify storage driver (default: local. Others: AWS EBS storage, NFS remote storage, Azure Disk)
--label: Attach metadata labels
-o/--opt: Pass additional driver-specific options like: permissions, size limits, filesystem type, mount type etc.  ^xhqOY4hv

6. Running a container with a named volume: ^sCILGd8N

docker run -d --name mysql-container -v mysql-data:/var/lib/mysql mysql:latest ^elgfHc0I

> docker run → Command to start a new container.
> -d (detached mode) → Runs the container in the background instead of showing its logs on screen.
> --name mysql-container → Assigns a custom name to the container. Without this, Docker assigns random 
                                  names like sleepy_mongo.
> -v mysql-data:/var/lib/mysql → Attach a named volume called mysql-data to the folder /var/lib/mysql inside 
                                         the container.
> mysql-data (before colon) → The actual Docker volume stored outside the container on the host machine.
> /var/lib/mysql (after colon) → This is where the MySQL server stores database files inside the container.
> mysql:latest → Image to create/run the container. ^nCPAuwC7

Explaination: ^ce1OsmUq

What this achieves: ^qhDrVAIA

> If the container stops, crashes, or is deleted: docker rm -f mysql-container
> The database files still remain in the mysql-data volume.
> So when you run a new MySQL container and attach the same volume, your data comes back. ^kpNI8ze0

7. Running a container with an anonymous volume: ^eT4M73CO

docker run -d -v /app nginx ^jLDUwW6S

> docker run → Start a container.
> -d → Run detached in background.
> -v /app → Docker sees only one path (no name: prefix), so it auto-creates a random unnamed volume, 
                something like: f3c29b5329e9b541f48c91c1939273102177:/app
> nginx → Container image. ^W2CMtUVK

Explaination: ^ikoeRiDE

What this achieves: ^36yVCcTW

Anonymous volumes are useful when:
> You don’t care about the data later.
> You want temporary storage.
> Logs or cache will be discarded.
> But the downside is:
   -> Hard to track the volume name
   -> Old unused anonymous volumes pile up → waste disk space. ^us1UeGgW

8. Running a container using --mount syntax: ^bso55NCo

docker run -d --mount type=volume,source=myvol,target=/app nginx ^MT9lz2Wa

> docker run → Create/start a container.
> -d → Background mode.
> --mount → Attach storage using the more explicit format.
> type=volume → Defines storage type. (Other options: bind, tmpfs)
> source=myvol → Name of the Docker volume. If the volume doesn’t exist, Docker will create it automatically.
> target=/app → Directory inside the container where the volume will be mounted.
> nginx → Image used to run the container. ^GDsUe6SR

Explaination: ^exCwHx3B

What this achieves: ^KaK7UXuZ

--mount is clearer and recommended for production because:
> Each part is specified explicitly.
> It avoids confusion seen in -v format.
> Supports more advanced options (read-only mode, labels, NFS, etc.) ^GYzi88GS

Docker Networks ^NEO2qNca

> Learning Docker networking is important because containers are rarely used individually. Most real-world applications
  involve multiple containers working together, such as a frontend container, a backend API container, and a database container.
> Without understanding networking, these separate containers cannot communicate efficiently or securely, making the system
   unusable or exposed to risks.
> Docker networking teaches how containers communicate with each other internally and with the external world, including how 
   requests travel through the network.
> Networking knowledge allows you to control access. Not every container should be accessible to every other container. For 
  example, a database should be accessible only to the backend, not exposed publicly.
> It supports building secure infrastructure, ensuring only required services can communicate and unauthorized access is prevented.
> Docker networking becomes crucial when scaling systems. Microservices-based applications often replicate services, and networking 
  ensures these replicated containers can discover and communicate with each other automatically.
> Understanding networking in Docker makes transitioning to production-level platforms easier, as Kubernetes, Docker Swarm, and 
  cloud deployments use the same concepts such as routing, service discovery, and load balancing.
> It also helps with troubleshooting issues. If something fails, it may not be a coding problem but a networking issue like blocked 
  ports, wrong mode, unreachable DNS, or missing routing rules. ^lqfI650l

Docker networking is a system that allows containers to communicate with each other, with the host machine, and with external 
networks in a controlled, secure, and structured way. ^Y4zraoms

What is the purpose of Docker networks? ^BcjEls4D

Docker Networking Commands ^MCi1aA2h

Lists all Docker networks available on the system. ^0btFtP6h

1. docker network ls: ^8YfM6uHM

Syntax: ^CMuR0p7R

docker network ls ^mBmLUXIy

Explanation: ^JDzDCi5i

> Shows network names, IDs, drivers (bridge, host, overlay), and scope.
> Helps identify existing networks before creating or connecting containers. ^U2c2kuTW

Displays detailed information about a specific network (IP range, containers connected, driver details). ^xRkJyfcI

configuration. ^EMbOoved

> --driver bridge: Creates a network using bridge driver.
> --subnet: Ensures fixed network range.
> Useful for controlled IP assignment and isolation. ^9zc7Wky8

--subnet: Defines custom subnet

Connects an existing container to a network. ^3EV3nQD7

To allow the container to communicate with services on another network. ^j1PSeRlf

6. docker network rm: ^4HNWFtVa
