Cleanup unused networks after testing. ^XW7tUTMy

docker network rm mynetwork ^UoSomMQq

Example: ^qR33EICe

Types of Docker Networks ^Lj6Wz5aF

1. Bridge Network: ^eussYt7a

> A bridge network in Docker is a private internal network created on the host machine where containers can communicate with each 
  other using private IP addresses. 
> It isolates containers from the external world unless ports are explicitly mapped or exposed. ^W915eNFS

> When Docker is installed, it automatically creates a default network called bridge.
> When you run a container without specifying a network, Docker attaches it to the default bridge.
> Each container gets:
   -> A unique private IP address
   -> Access to internal DNS to communicate using container names
> Containers inside the same bridge network can communicate directly.
> To talk to the outside world (like a browser accessing a container), the container must use port mapping (-p). ^txUhqSip

How Bridge Networking Works: ^hwevteAW

Real-Life Use Cases: ^nNlkDYPi

> Bridge networking is useful in scenarios where multiple services run on the same machine and need to communicate privately. ^4KFJXbg8

Examples: ^B0pqpnkP

> Local Development Environment
  --> Frontend container (React)
  --> Backend container (Spring Boot)
  --> Database container (MySQL)
  --> Testing Microservices
> Each service runs in its own container
> They communicate using container names
> Single-node deployments
> Applications that run everything on one server (VM or workstation) ^uNapsoxG

Advantages of Bridge Network: ^VSm0Fjvv

> Isolation: Containers are isolated from the host and other unrelated container networks.
> Automatic DNS: Containers can communicate using names instead of IP addresses.
> Security: Services are not publicly exposed unless manually mapped.
> Easy to Use: No complex setup is needed for local development.
> Supports Port Mapping: You control which services are reachable from outside. ^Kae45Oa4

Disadvantages of Bridge Network: ^ogHJSjyl

> Limited to Single Host: Containers on different Docker hosts cannot communicate using bridge.
> Manual Port Exposure: You must map ports if you want external access.
> Scaling Challenges: Once you have many services requiring external access, port mapping becomes messy.
> Not Ideal for Production Clusters: For distributed systems, overlay networking or Kubernetes networking is preferred. ^f9tKdKe9

docker network create --driver bridge mynetwork
docker run -d --name app --network mynetwork nginx
docker run -d --name db --network mynetwork mysql
docker exec -it app bash
ping db ^2rD6pWdh

Example: ^Rk9w8zwv

If communication works, you should see responses like:


> Now app can reach DB using hostname db. ^YhU9aCSh

PING db (172.18.0.3): 56 data bytes
64 bytes from 172.18.0.3: icmp_seq=1 ttl=64 time=0.150 ms ^ekcp4sg5

To create your own custom bridge network ^puitFtBP

Running two containers on the same network ^i6bFbjWP

Going inside one container and trying to access the other. ^098wKhbd

testing whether the hostname db resolves using ping command ^AwVPwb8s

docker network inspect devnet ^ufx3yDNm

Inspect Network: If you run inspect command, you should see both containers listed under "Containers". ^FYF6MFNL

Host networking is used when:
> You need high performance and low latency networking, such as monitoring tools or proxies (Prometheus, Nginx, Traefik).
> You run applications requiring direct access to host networking (VPNs, DHCP servers, system-level networking tools).
> You want to avoid overhead of NAT/port forwarding. ^YP4hHEC0

When Host Network Is Used in Real Life: ^1K1yZanY

Advantages of Host Networking: ^hY51ORFd

> No NAT or port mapping required: Containers are immediately accessible using the host machine's IP address.
> Better performance: No virtual networking layer means less overhead and lower latency.
> Simplified configuration: Useful for software that expects direct host access. ^2GxTI8tl

Disadvantages of Host Networking: ^W0A5P0L5

> No network isolation: Containers share the host's ports — if a container uses port 80, the host can’t use it for another 
                             service.
> Less secure: The container has more direct exposure to the network, increasing attack surface.
> Not available on Windows and macOS with Docker Desktop: Full host mode works only on Linux hosts.
> Not suitable for running multiple containers of same kind: Since they share host ports, conflicts happen easily. ^GV0pQGRw

When to Use Host Network vs Bridge Network: ^mwFf64qV

Use Host Network when:
> You need performance or low latency.
> You are running system-level or network service containers.

Use Bridge Network when:
> You want isolation.
> You need multiple containers communicating securely.
> You want port mapping flexibility. ^Y8qcuOt0

docker run -d --name webserver --network host nginx

docker ps


curl http://localhost

docker inspect webserver ^LjR9Q0mz

Example: ^HGQ9sRaj

Run NGINX in Host Mode ^beCIVgSN

Verify Container Is Running (You should see the container running without a port mapping column because 
ports aren’t mapped — they're inherited.) ^kt3BwWqv

Test from Host Machine (If the host networking is working, you’ll see NGINX default HTML 
response.) ^c6r0YwOZ

Check Container Network Info ^4UnKDqmc

3. None Network: ^t12UMYhq

The none network in Docker is a special network mode where a container is created with no network access at all.
When a container uses the none network, it:
> Gets no IP address
> Cannot communicate with other containers
> Cannot access the internet
> Has no DNS resolution
> Does not map any ports ^LqU3KABB

Why Does none Network Exist? ^RAd9hZGz

It is useful when:
> You don't want a service to communicate externally.
> You want maximum isolation for security testing.
> You are performing offline workload execution.
> You want to manually configure networking yourself (advanced use cases).

Examples:
> Running malware analysis
> Sensitive scripts or computation workloads
> Scientific workload where network access is not required ^0DO4waNp

docker run -d --network none --name secure-container nginx
docker exec -it secure-container sh
ping www.google.com ^xBWJZEwH

It’s useful when:
> You run a background task (processing images, logs, ML, etc.) that does not need network
> You perform security or penetration testing
> You need a high-security isolated workload
> You want to apply custom networking manually (like using your own namespaces or VPN handling) ^GNVLuF9a

When none Network Is Used in Real Life? ^Dxb5v9fd

A company runs a sensitive script that processes confidential files.
They do not want any chance of data leaking over the network.
So they run the script in a Docker container using the none network, ensuring complete network isolation. ^5xY58tvn

Example real-life scenario: ^LkGgXb0Z

Advantages of none Network: ^ZX2YfCLK

> Full isolation: No external attack surface.
> Zero chance of accidental outbound data.
> Great for testing controlled environments.
> Useful for strict security compliance workloads. ^JNKzRdgy

Disadvantages of none Network: ^mkL7uiG6

> No internet access for updates or APIs.
> Cannot communicate with other containers.
> Not useful for services that must send or receive network traffic. ^Va80k4qS

Create a Container Using none Network ^0RL69SPR

Example: ^tq3bX0K7

Enter the container ^jUk7GOE7

Test network connectivity: ping: bad address 'google.com' ^tAmwlyVy

2. Host network: ^umiTHGH6

> A host network in Docker removes the virtual network isolation between the container and the host machine.
> Instead of getting its own private IP address, the container uses the host machine’s network stack directly.
> The container does not get a private IP like in the bridge network.
> It does not need port mapping (-p).
> Services running inside the container are directly accessible using the host’s IP or localhost. ^cxwnTWTl

Docker Compose ^ZVKZIO3K

> Docker Compose is a tool used to define and run multiple containers as a single application. 
> Instead of running each container manually using separate docker run commands, Docker Compose allows you to write everything
  in one YAML file, usually named docker-compose.yml, and then start all services together with one command.
> Docker Compose is especially useful when an application needs multiple components such as a backend service, frontend service, 
  and database. 
> Instead of managing all of them individually, Compose brings them up together and handles networking between them 
  automatically.
> The main purpose of Docker Compose is simplicity and automation. It reduces repetitive commands and ensures consistent 
  environments across development, testing, and deployment. ^yUZGjzqT

How it works? ^XCevM5QD

> You write all service definitions in docker-compose.yml file. Then you run the command docker compose up. Docker Compose reads
  the file, creates containers, builds images if necessary, configures networking, and starts everything in the correct order. ^nDmMjUDV

1. docker compose up: ^VngW4fNL

Docker Compose Commands: ^2lPjKiJe

Starts all services defined in the docker-compose.yml file. If images are not built, it will build them 
automatically. ^upXV6NiY

Syntax: ^v5RU9ZwW

docker compose up [OPTIONS] ^CEO3vmin

> -d: Runs containers in detached (background) mode.
> --build: Forces rebuild of images before starting.
> --remove-orphans: Removes containers that are not defined in the current compose file.
> --force-recreate: Recreates containers even if configuration is unchanged. ^xS7bJmjq

Flags: ^4WpDKc7D

Example: ^KuoYnVOZ

docker compose up -d --build ^ncARIddV

This command reads the docker-compose.yml file, creates networks, builds images if needed, and starts the 
containers. ^pkpLCYez

2. docker compose down: ^vXPURXdt

Stops and removes containers, networks, and temporary resources created by docker compose up. ^EZP1DOiQ

Syntax: ^jMQ9fsUH

docker compose down [OPTIONS] ^7sdhcqJZ

> --volumes: Removes associated volumes.
> --rmi all: Removes all images built by Compose. ^8oUJB4O5

Flags: ^6QnKeahz

Example: ^IoD8JhrU

docker compose down --volumes --rmi all ^cNKKLTN8

This command stops the running services and removes everything including volumes and images if specified. ^Pq6ChBqo

3. docker compose ps: ^pth8wbsB

Displays the status of services managed by Docker Compose. ^fKPhZhBg

Syntax: ^2sU0ATfb

docker compose ps [OPTIONS] ^AaXljpOy

> -a: Shows all containers including stopped ones. ^KGKMKrAv

Flags: ^5pCDEF0P

Example: ^zneWUZDu

docker compose ps -a ^0JHWYtGW

Shows running and exited containers and their associated ports and states. ^g5Vrdh7w

4. docker compose logs: ^6wDLJXQt

Displays logs for services ^TNO57h49

Syntax: ^JBbcV2a8

docker compose logs [OPTIONS] [SERVICE] ^al4tJm5a

> -f: Follow logs in realtime (like tail -f)
> --tail n: Shows only last ‘n’ number of log lines. ^hOa8jH8E

Flags: ^bXZFhfa2

Example: ^8hqd5kGt

docker compose logs -f backend ^mLnxRn4X

Shows real-time logs only from backend service. -f keeps the command running and streams updates. ^gO0HirOF

5. docker compose start: ^S9cBm1Rn

Starts existing stopped containers without recreating them. ^Oz4PTLXh

Syntax: ^EF3tArnq

docker compose start [SERVICE] ^vR1mU09o

Example: ^rV5HcBcW

docker compose start frontend ^FQkVU9lu

Starts only the frontend container if it was previously stopped. ^OkNnhBef

6. docker compose stop: ^W2FmY8yI

Stops running containers but does not remove them. ^BDdyoG5W

Syntax: ^3Ue51NQw

Example: ^8crIiiM3

7. docker compose restart: ^brAjO2qT

Stops and restarts running containers. ^BMpiJXit

Syntax: ^JFiK8ytq

docker compose restart [SERVICE] ^q3O0W3WO

Example: ^4rI64rIO

Restarts only the backend service without affecting others. ^UneIumrL

8. docker compose build: ^VrDGk5vK

Builds or rebuilds images defined in the compose file. ^zshQPzmw

Syntax: ^7LFwmzzT

docker compose build [OPTIONS] ^54y60jf3

> --pull: Always attempts to pull the latest base image. ^ALHRMeSb

Example: ^AezjJJXs

10. docker compose exec: ^QNCC1We1

Example: ^web6UmwH

> -f: Force removal without confirmation ^ZJ05if8z

> It simplifies launching and managing multi-container projects.
> It keeps configurations in code, which improves consistency and avoids manual errors.
> It allows easy scaling of services by increasing the number of container replicas with a single command such as 
  docker compose up --scale web=3.
> It supports environment file integration, making it easier to store credentials and configuration separately.
> It is useful for both local development and automation pipelines. ^JYcLf5dB

Example scenario: ^qBrH6qIv

> If you are running a web application using Spring Boot and MySQL, normally you need to start the backend container and then 
  manually connect it to the running MySQL container. 
> With Docker Compose, you define both services in one file. Compose automatically creates a shared network where both services
   can communicate using service names instead of IP addresses. ^ppFHUSzV

Advantages of Docker Compose: ^p2gKr3uu

When to use Docker Compose: ^1LxKaX19

Docker Compose is used to run multi-container applications on a single machine, mainly for development and testing. It simplifies 
managing multiple services like backend, frontend, and database using one YAML file. It does not support auto-scaling or 
distributed clustering. ^onO9I2fd

When to use Docker Swarm: ^osBVveCk

Docker Swarm is a lightweight orchestration system used to run containers across multiple machines. It provides basic scaling, 
load balancing, and automatic failover with simple commands. It is easier than Kubernetes, but less feature-rich. ^yqPWUIB2

When to use Kubernetes: ^1ZiM5f0p

Kubernetes is a powerful container orchestration platform designed for large-scale, production-grade workloads. It supports 
automatic scaling, self-healing, rolling updates, and advanced networking. It is more complex but ideal for enterprise and cloud 
environments. ^XGS8DSDC

> YAML stands for "YAML Ain’t Markup Language."
> It is a human-readable data format used mainly for configuration files.
> YAML focuses on readability. It uses indentation (spaces) instead of brackets or tags. 
> In Docker, Kubernetes, GitHub Actions, Ansible, and many DevOps tools, configurations are written in YAML because it is easy 
  for humans to read and edit. ^J17xWGVa

What is a YAML File? ^ePVc9xiR

pipeline {
    agent any

    environment {
        DOCKERHUB_USER = "sumukha200147"
        IMAGE_NAME = "petclinic"
    }

    stages {
        stage('Git-Clone') {
            steps {
                git branch: 'main', url: 'https://github.com/Sumukha47/Docker-Spring-Petclinic.git'
            }
        }

        stage('Build') {
            steps {
                bat 'mvn clean package -DskipTests'
            }
        }

        stage('Build-Image') {
            steps {
                bat 'docker build -t %IMAGE_NAME%:latest .'
            }
        }

        stage('Pushing image to Dockerhub') {
            steps {
                withCredentials([string(credentialsId: 'dockerhub-pass', variable: 'TOKEN')]) {
                    bat """
                        echo %TOKEN% | docker login -u ${DOCKERHUB_USER} --password-stdin
                        docker tag ${IMAGE_NAME} ${DOCKERHUB_USER}/${IMAGE_NAME}:latest
                        docker push ${DOCKERHUB_USER}/${IMAGE_NAME}:latest
                    """
                }
            }
        }

        stage('Create a container using Docker Compose') {
            steps {
                bat "docker compose down || exit 0"
                bat "docker compose up -d --build"
            }
        }
    }
} ^OlMiUJ0s

Basic Syntax: ^PbQOhidu

version: "<version>"

services:
    <service_name_1>:
        image: <image_name>
        build: <build_context_path>
        environment:
            <ENV_KEY_1>: <ENV_VALUE_1>
            <ENV_KEY_2>: <ENV_VALUE_2>
        ports:
            - "<host_port>:<container_port>"
        volumes:
            - "<volume_name>:<container_path>"
        networks:
            - <network_name>

    <service_name_2>:
        image: <image_name>
        build: <build_context_path>
        environment:
            <ENV_KEY_1>: <ENV_VALUE_1>
            <ENV_KEY_2>: <ENV_VALUE_2>
        ports:
            - "<host_port>:<container_port>"
        volumes:
            - "<volume_name>:<container_path>"
        networks:
            - <network_name>

volumes:
    <volume_name>:

networks:
    <network_name>:
        driver: <driver_type>
 ^gfJtQEm1

Specifies the Docker Compose file format version (e.g., "3.9") ^TealpZ6c

Defines all services (containers) in this application ^pNy2rau8

Name of the first service (e.g., backend, frontend, api) ^WXvGlNR8

Image to use for this service (skip if using build) ^jFy79Fwp

Build context folder (e.g., "."), used when building an image ^HBodbvxx

Environment variables passed to the container ^9lNXcFET

Example environment variables ^QUZtM8zV

Port mapping between host and container ^P4D9uQun

Maps host port to container port ^lTLSc64j

Storage volumes attached to the container ^dTwnYENQ

Named volume mapped to a path inside container ^7BMsVADr

Networks this service is connected to ^FS6ET2oG

The network name ^4mkGqy7a

Same as above service ^cSMF5FsC

Define named volumes here ^A4jcHzSI

Volume name; Compose automatically creates it ^os61W1Sb

Define custom networks here ^m0cLAzx6

Name of the network ^4zn5amoB

Driver type (usually "bridge" for local Docker setups) ^8g8X7Q1a

version: "1.0"
services:
  web:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "120:80" ^IipmpXc1

Writing a compose file for spring-petclinic project and integrating Docker with Jenkins: ^XeL36upP

1) Clone spring-petclinic project and then write Dockerfile like this:







2) After writing Dockerfile, create a .env file containing environment variables separately so that:
    > your Compose file stays clean
    > sensitive values are not written directly in docker-compose.yml
    > you can change environment values easily without modifying the compose file
    > you can reuse the same compose file in dev, test, and prod just by changing .env 
    > You should not commit passwords into Git. Instead, .env stays in .gitignore.














3) After creating .env file, write docker-compose.yaml file (You can either delete the already existing file or modify the same) like 
    this:  ^J8B2zoTL

FROM eclipse-temurin:21.0.9_10-jdk
WORKDIR /demo
COPY target/*.jar /demo/petclinic.jar
EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT [ "java","-jar" ]
CMD ["petclinic.jar"] ^KD2S8nqM

version: "3.9"

services:
  backend:
    image: petclinic:latest
    build: .
    environment:
      SPRING_MYSQL_URL: ${SPRING_MYSQL_URL}
      SPRING_MYSQL_USERNAME: ${SPRING_MYSQL_USERNAME}
      SPRING_MYSQL_PASSWORD: ${SPRING_MYSQL_PASSWORD}
    ports:
      - "${SERVER_PORT}:8080"

  mysql:
    image: mysql:8.0
    environment:
      MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD: ${MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD}
      MYSQL_DATABASE: ${MYSQL_DATABASE}
      MYSQL_USER: ${MYSQL_USER}
      MYSQL_PASSWORD: ${MYSQL_PASSWORD}
    ports:
      - "3306:3306"
    volumes:
      - mysql-data:/var/lib/mysql

volumes:
  mysql-data: ^LmCUpXGd

SPRING_MYSQL_USERNAME=petclinic

# Exposed port

ECR (Elastic Container Registry) ^P8xNT1v1

> ECR stands for Amazon Elastic Container Registry. It is a fully managed container image registry provided by AWS.
> It stores Docker images and lets you pull them when running containers on AWS services like ECS, EKS, Lambda, and EC2. ^qxyEMG0G

> Private repositories are accessible only to your AWS account and to specific IAM users, roles, or services that you allow.
  They are secure, encrypted, and controlled by IAM permissions.
> Access controlled by IAM
> Cannot be accessed publicly
> Best for production workloads
> Encrypted by default
> Supports image scanning for vulnerabilities ^vMvgoJbs

1. Private Repositories: ^Wkd3GFwi

Use case examples: ^qkCDGxFz

> Storing your company's application images
> ECS, EKS, EC2 pulling images securely
> CI/CD pipelines pushing images
> Keeping internal or sensitive images ^ncUMXyEn

Types of Repositories in ECR ^lojVtbfe

> These repositories allow anyone on the internet to pull images without authentication.
> They work similar to DockerHub public images, but with AWS-level reliability.
> Publicly accessible without login
> No rate limits like DockerHub
> Backed by AWS global infrastructure
> Good alternative to DockerHub public repos ^TKMAYB6v

2. Public Repositories: ^Rp3FP6wG

Use case examples: ^fMh4nId1

> Sharing open-source images
> Providing official company images
> Hosting base images or public tools ^WdKjaUOI

DockerHub is the most popular public container registry. It works for everyone and is great for open-source images. But 
when you deploy applications inside AWS, you need features that DockerHub cannot provide.
Here are the real differences explained clearly: ^UkRzsP5G

Why do we need ECR if we already have DockerHub? ^V8l6JTYN

1. Authentication: ^jfQsFK00

> DockerHub uses basic username and password or tokens.
> ECR uses IAM permissions, AWS CLI, AWS roles, and temporary tokens.
> Meaning: EC2, ECS, EKS machines can automatically access ECR without storing passwords.
> Companies prefer private, secured registries. ECR provides IAM-based access control.
> No plain passwords, no need to store DockerHub credentials on servers. ^UCnuVbH3

2. Security: ^DhYg2qCd

> DockerHub is public by default. Private repos cost money and you only get one private repo for free tier account.
> ECR provides private repositories by default, encrypted at rest, with full IAM security. ^K6KsJl8r

3. Speed and Performance: ^FUruznFf

> DockerHub is global but not inside AWS.
> ECR is inside AWS data centers.
> When your application in AWS pulls the image from ECR, it is extremely fast, low latency, and uses AWS backbone network.
> ECR integrates with AWS KMS (encryption) and CloudTrail (auditing).
> This is important for enterprises following security standards.
> Images download much faster inside AWS.
> Your ECS cluster pulls an ECR image almost instantly.
> CI/CD pipelines in AWS (CodeBuild, CodePipeline, GitHub Actions with AWS roles) work smoothly with ECR. ^GpsFhNhk

> DockerHub has download rate limits for free accounts.
> ECR has no such restrictions for your AWS account. ^WqOHYXbn

4. Reliability and Pull Limits: ^DJnCMIid

Docker Hub places limits on image download rates for free users, and exceeding these limits will cause requests to be 
blocked Unauthenticated users: 100 pulls per 6 hours per IPv4 or IPv6 /64 subnet.
> Authenticated free users: 200 pulls per 6 hours per IP address.
> Paid users: Unlimited pull rate limits on Docker Hub per 6 hours. ^jG3Br8aI

Pull rate limits: ^6UA5icVw

Steps to push images to Amazon ECR: ^gS08G9PJ

1) Install AWS CLI to interact with AWS from your machine. Go to your browser and search for AWS CLI install for windows.
   scroll down and select windows and select 
   "Download and run the AWS CLI MSI installer for Windows (64-bit): https://awscli.amazonaws.com/AWSCLIV2.msi."
2) Go to the downloaded location and install it.
3) Login to AWS and search for IAM. Create one IAM user (Docker User) and give these permissions while creating the user:
   > AmazonEC2ContainerRegistryFullAccess
   > AmazonElasticContainerRegistryPublicFullAccess
4) After creating this user, click on the user you have created and under these options "Permissions", "Groups", "Tags(1)", 
   "Security credentials", "Last Accessed" select Security credentials.
5) Scroll down till you get an option called as "Access keys" and click on Create access key.
6) Select "Command Line Interface (CLI): You plan to use this access key to enable the AWS CLI to access your AWS 
    account." option, scroll down and tick on this "Confirmation: I understand the above recommendation and want to 
    proceed to create an access key." and finally click on next and click on create access key. Click on "Download .csv file" 
    and click on done.
7) Open VS code from your project folder and write these commands: 

    when you give this command, it will ask you to fill these: ^WqKYv0sp

aws configure ^Cqq962fa

AWS Access Key ID [****************SSXV]: 
AWS Secret Access Key [****************h+Gi]: 
Default region name [us-east-1]: us-east-1
Default output format [jason]: jason ^e8lC5mVN

Mention the region where you are creating your private repository 
(for public it is by default N.Virginia region) ^ofvpoN6D

Write it as Jason ^7t8y2qDY

Fill these credentials from the csv file which is downloaded. ^GO387ftj

8) After this, search for ECR and then click on create. By default it will take you to create a private repository. (If you 
    want to create a public repository then in the sidebar select repositories under public registry.)
9) Under "General settings" give a repository name.
10) Under "Image tag settings" you have 2 options "Mutable" and "Immutable"  ^o1QN1Ckl

1. Mutable Tags: ^wiNN7BgQ

Example: ^7XnniWYj

You can overwrite an existing image tag with a new image. ^IXSSN3Dc

> You have an image tagged as
  latest → version1
> Later, you push a new image with the same tag:
  latest → now points to version2
> The old image is replaced by the new one. ^KJqEybsM

Why it is risky in production: ^kPTrHJBC

> If ECS, EKS, or developers pull the same tag at different times, they may get different images.
  This causes unpredictable behavior. ^XYlU3FXH

2. Immutable Tags: ^mScRH5l9

Example: ^BVqEYlL8

You cannot overwrite, modify, or replace an existing tag once it is pushed. ^pLlS5wb1

> If you push an image with tag: v1
> You cannot push another image with the same tag v1.
> You must create a new tag such as v2 or v1.1. ^ZxlLRRwr

Why it is safer: ^rFHst56f

> Guarantees consistency
> Ensures same image is pulled every time
> Avoids accidental overwrites
> Better for production environments ^P33E9JgO

11) Scroll down till the end and click on create.
12) Click on the Repository you have created and click on "View push commands" you will get list of commands with 
     explaination. Copy these commands one by one and execute it in VS code inside the project folder (Note that 
     Dockerfile should be present in that location). ^FBEI5pUi

aws ecr get-login-password --region us-east-1 | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 462219490304.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com

docker build -t sumukha-private-repository .

docker tag sumukha-private-repository:latest 462219490304.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/sumukha-private-repository:latest

docker push 462219490304.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/sumukha-private-repository:latest ^M6IWUycl

13) After executing these commands, you can see that the image and it's related metadata is stored in ECR. ^ejlhMhaw

From your output:
> amazon-version-1 – Image Index → 69.13 MB
> Image → 69.13 MB
> Image → 0.00 MB

Here’s what this means:

a) Image Index:  This is a manifest list in ECR (or multi-arch image manifest).
>  It doesn’t hold the actual image layers itself; it points to one or more images for different architectures (amd64, arm64, etc.).
>  Since you only pushed once, this manifest points to the single image you built.

b) Image (69.13 MB): This is your actual Docker image with the pushed layers.
>  This is what ECR uses to run containers when you pull amazon-version-1.

c) Image (0.00 MB): This usually happens because Docker/ECR creates a placeholder for platform compatibility, especially if your 
                          image uses multiple architectures (even if you didn’t explicitly build a multi-arch image).
>  ECR shows it as 0 MB because there are no actual layers in that entry; it’s just part of the manifest.

Why ECR shows 3 entries:
> 1 manifest list → Image Index (amazon-version-1)
> 1 actual image → The one you built
> 1 placeholder image → Usually 0 MB, sometimes for architecture compatibility

Bottom line: You only have one real image. The other two are either metadata (manifest) or placeholders. This is normal behavior in ECR. ^97HcGQVM

Drawbacks of Traditional approach: ^t478BwoR

1) No proper collaboration between the Development team and Operations team.
2) Lots of manual work.
3) May lead to human error.
4) Time consuming.
5) Slow deployment of the application.
6) Slow feedback loop. ^QEV5lbzs

To overcome this drawbacks we go for "Jenkins" ^sSDKxY8Y

What is Jenkins? ^wIedSM9J

> It is a free and open source CI/CD tool which automates the process of building, testing and 
  deploying the software into production server.
> It's written in Java and helps developers automate the non-coding parts of the software 
  development lifecycle. ^MdYT0RN2

> Jenkins automates the practice of Continuous Integration, which is the process of frequently 
  merging developers' code changes into a central repository.
> Jenkins continuously collects the source code from the GitHub and generates the build.
> This process can be automated by using build triggers.
> A build trigger is a mechanism that automatically initiates the execution of a build job or pipeline in 
  a CI/CD tool like Jenkins, GitLab, or Azure DevOps. 
> Instead of a developer manually starting the process, the trigger sets the condition or event that 
  kicks off the automated sequence of building, testing, and sometimes deploying code. ^kU5CngA1

Continuous Integration (CI): ^v1r2MJ62

> Continuous Deployment is an extension of Continuous Delivery, where the entire process is fully 
  automated, removing all human intervention from the pipeline.
> Here we are continuously installing the build into production server or client server.
> The pipeline automates every single step, from a successful code commit all the way through to 
  deployment in the production environment.
> If a code change passes all the automated quality gates (unit tests, integration tests, performance 
  tests, security scans), it is automatically released to the end-users. Only a failed test will stop the 
  process.
> There is no manual approval for the final production deployment. The trust is placed entirely in the 
  automated tests and monitoring systems.
> This provides the fastest possible feedback loop, allowing organizations to release features and
  fixes multiple times a day, which is common for major web services and SaaS applications.
> It requires a high level of testing maturity and robust production monitoring, as any bug that slips
  past the automated checks goes immediately live. ^Rqn4tt8o

> Continuous Delivery is a software engineering practice where code changes are built, tested, and 
  prepared for a release to a production environment.
> Here we are preparing the build ready to be deployed into production server or client server. 
> The key principle is that the code is always in a deployable state and ready to go live at any moment.
> The deployment to the final production environment requires a manual approval or a deliberate action 
  (like the click of a button) by a human. This allows the team to make a business decision about when 
  to release—for instance, coordinating with a marketing launch, avoiding peak traffic hours, or getting 
  final sign-off from management. (This can't be fully automated).
> It provides speed and reliability, while retaining human control over the final release timing. ^RoTlyaOR

Continuous Delivery (CD): ^eJiXQkOx

Continuous Deployment (CD): ^FVdUFCeX

History of Jenkins: ^zmduaDbK

2004: Kohsuke Kawaguchi created a project called as "Hudson" to automate the process of building 
        and testing the software. He was working in sun microsystem.
2010: Oracle accuired sun microsystem which created conflict between Hudson and Oracle.
2011: Hudson project was renamed as "Jenkins". ^vPHl0w8n

Features of Jenkins: ^pte6RslY

> It is free and open source: The software costs nothing and its source code is publicly available for 
                                    anyone to use and modify.
> It supports extensible plugins: Its core functionality can be easily expanded by adding various 
                                         specialized extensions and add-ons.
> It supports pipeline as a code: You can define and manage your entire build and deployment workflow 
                                         using code checked into your source control repository.
> It is user friendly: The interface is generally intuitive, making it easy for both new and experienced 
                          users to set up and manage automation jobs.
> It is scalable because it supports master-slave architecture: 
  It can distribute workloads across multiple machines, allowing it to easily handle a very large number 
  of projects simultaneously.
> It supports authentication (username and password) and authorization (permission): 
  It ensures security by requiring users to log in and grants them specific access permissions based on 
  their roles.
> It can be integrated with third-party application: It easily connects and works with many other 
                                                                external development, testing, and deployment tools. ^yIlvLToZ

Alternative tools: ^yWAdLsQ3

> GitHub Actions: It's a workflow automation platform deeply integrated into GitHub repositories, 
                      allowing you to easily build, test, and deploy code directly from pull requests and 
                      commits. It simplifies CI/CD by treating your pipelines as code right alongside your 
                      source code.
> GitLab CI/CD: A single, built-in solution that is part of the entire GitLab DevOps platform, 
                     automating everything from code validation to security scanning and deployment. It 
                     uses a single .gitlab-ci.yml file to offer full transparency and management for your 
                     pipeline within one tool.
> TeamCity: A powerful and mature Java-based CI/CD server from JetBrains, known for its extensive 
               features, strong integrations, and easy configuration via a comprehensive web interface. 
               It’s highly favored by larger teams needing advanced configuration and enterprise-level 
               reporting.
> Bamboo: Atlassian's CI/CD tool that is tightly integrated with Jira and Bitbucket, making it a natural
             choice for teams already using the Atlassian suite. It offers powerful, user-friendly options 
             for both graphical and code-based pipeline configuration.
> Travis CI: A popular, early cloud-based CI/CD service, best known for its ease of use and its strong 
               focus on open-source projects, where it remains a widely adopted choice. It’s defined by a 
               simple .travis.yml file and offers a quick setup for testing code in the cloud.
> CircleCI: A fast, cloud-based CI/CD platform that focuses on speed and efficiency with features like 
             caching and parallelism to optimize build times. It’s valued for its quick setup, scalability, and 
             robust security features for deploying software. ^3u5SSfZL

Jobs in Jenkins ^8JQAWYIk

> A job is a specific task performed in Jenkins and through the job we can automate the process of 
  generating build, testing and deploying the projects. ^ebZFAw5O

Types of jobs in Jenkins: ^PKSBs4y8

1) Freestyle Job: ^N1f2Hl8q

> A Freestyle Project is the most basic and flexible type of Jenkins job. 
> It allows you to define simple build, test, and deploy steps directly through the Jenkins graphical 
  interface — no code or Jenkinsfile required.
> It is a type of job which doesn't require any additional installation of tools where as in freestyle project,
  all the tools are pre-installed. While performing the job we just need to do manual configurations for setting
  up the job.    ^Phk4yK6X

> For simple automation tasks that don’t need version-controlled pipelines.
> When you’re starting out with Jenkins or want to quickly test something.
> For small projects or one-time scripts. ^7SllyPPe

> Provide a project name and description.
> Choose a Source Code Management (SCM) tool (like Git).
> Add Build Triggers (like Poll SCM, GitHub webhook, or manual start).
> Specify Build Steps (run shell commands, execute batch commands, call build tools).
> Set Post-build Actions (send notifications, archive files, deploy artifacts). ^PnZofi4C

How It Works: ^2f5mCTqD

When to Use: ^R7ZRMlds

> You create a Freestyle job to:
> Pull code from GitHub
> Compile Java files with javac ^FC0DN3Io

    repository.

    command" option. Then write these two commands: ^VyhdYOM5

Workspace in Jobs: ^NqaZqU5G

> In Jenkins, a workspace is the directory on the Jenkins server (or agent) where Jenkins checks out your 
  project files, performs build operations, and stores temporary data while a job is running.
> In simple terms: The workspace is the “working folder” Jenkins uses for a job’s execution.
> When a Jenkins job (Freestyle, Maven, or Pipeline) runs, Jenkins creates a workspace for that job.
  Inside that workspace, it places the source code, build scripts, artifacts, and any intermediate files needed 
  during the build process. ^5rjRdVIH

2) Maven project Job: ^WyUvpYoC

> A Maven Project job type is a specialized Jenkins job designed specifically for Apache Maven-based Java 
  projects. 
> It understands Maven’s pom.xml file and automatically handles build lifecycles (compile, test, 
  package, deploy, etc.). ^llhv2Tch

> You configure a Maven job to:
> Pull code from GitHub
> Run mvn clean test package
> Deploy the .war file to a Tomcat server ^h74nRJOs

How It Works: ^m7TTWkpk

> Instead of manually defining build steps, Jenkins reads your project’s pom.xml and executes the 
  corresponding Maven phases automatically. You just need to:
> Specify the Git repository or project source.
> Define the Maven goals and options (e.g., clean install).
> Jenkins will automatically download dependencies, compile, test, and build. ^zzvHrDQy

> When your project uses Maven as the build tool.
> When you want Jenkins to automatically handle Maven lifecycle and dependency management.
> For Java-based enterprise projects that follow Maven standards. ^sEpSOe0l

When to Use: ^ewzYB7Vw

Example Scenario: ^ynUlbWv8

1) Open a browser and search localhost:8080 it will open Jenkins dashboard.
2) In the sidebar you have an option called as "New Item" click on it. Give a job name and below select the 
    option called as "Maven project" then click on OK.
3) If it is not visible then follow this step:
   In the top right corner you have an option called as "Manage Jenkins" click on that. Once you are inside, 
   under "System Configuration" select the "Plugins" option. In the sidebar you have "Available plugins" option
   select that and in the searchbar write "Maven Integration" and select the plugin and give Install.
4) Here after installing Maven plugin, you need to install one more tool. In the top right corner you have an 
   option called as "Manage Jenkins" click on that. Once you are inside, under "System Configuration" select 
   the "Tools" option. Select that and scroll down till you find "Maven installations". There click on 
   "Add Maven",  under which give any name you want and untick the "Install automatically" option. Under
   "MAVEN_HOME" copy the path where you have installed maven locally to your system. It looks something 
   like this:                                               Then click on Apply and then click on Save.
5) After doing all this once again select option called as "New Item" click on it. Give a job name and below 
   select the option called as "Maven project" then click on OK. 
6) Give a description, scroll down and under "Source Code Management" select Git. It will ask for Repository
    URL paste the GitHub repository URL
7) Scroll down and under "Branches to Build" specify the branch name as master or main based on the GitHub
    repository.
8) Scroll down and under "Triggers" you have an option "Build whenever a SNAPSHOT dependency is built"
    untick that. It is optional.
9) Scroll down and under "Build" you get an option "Goals and options" just give the goal. Example: If you
    want to create a package don't give                 instead give           You can give multiple goals and 
    options in this format:  
10) Scroll down and finally click on Apply and then click on Save.
11) In the side bar you have an option called as "Build Now" click on that. You can see a build is being 
    generated click on that once after it is generated. ^KdIqhubA

   on add security group rule. Under port range give 8080 and under Source Type give "Anywhere".

    link which says "Linux" under which you have an option as "Debian/Ubuntu" select that option.
4) Scroll down and you will get a section "Long Term Support release" under which you will be getting these
    commands: ^wSaNYzpM

  https://pkg.jenkins.io/debian-stable/jenkins.io-2023.key
echo "deb [signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/jenkins-keyring.asc]" \
  https://pkg.jenkins.io/debian-stable binary/ | sudo tee \
  /etc/apt/sources.list.d/jenkins.list > /dev/null
sudo apt update
sudo apt install jenkins -y ^f5nERyqp

Build triggers in Jenkins ^0dxtmIFX

> A build trigger in Jenkins is an event or condition that automatically starts (triggers) a build job.
> Instead of manually clicking “Build Now,” triggers let Jenkins start jobs automatically — for example, when 
  code changes in GitHub, on a schedule, or after another job finishes.
> Build triggers make Jenkins truly automated and continuous. They form the heart of Continuous Integration
  (CI) and Continuous Deployment (CD) pipelines because they remove the need for human intervention and 
  ensure that software delivery happens smoothly and consistently. ^oqA5F5yg

Types of build triggers: ^0Id1bTv7

1. Build Periodically: ^UC9HHxS4

> The “Build periodically” trigger is used to schedule Jenkins jobs to run automatically at specific times, 
  dates, or intervals, similar to how cron jobs work in Linux.
> It is used to execute a job on a fixed schedule, even if there are no changes in the source code or other 
   inputs.
> Here you define a cron-like schedule in the Jenkins job configuration under the “Build Triggers” section. 
  Jenkins will then execute that job based on this schedule.
> Build periodically will generate the build based on the time interval which is specified and additionally if there
   is any build failure still the build will get generated irrespective of the result. ^y4X3K3NU

Cron Syntax Format: ^tDLdzSty

MINUTE   HOUR   DOM    MONTH    DOW ^Z1EaeY4t

where:
> MINUTE: 0–59
> HOUR: 0–23
> DOM: Day of Month (1–31)
> MONTH: 1–12
> DOW: Day of Week (0–7, where 0 and 7 represent Sunday)
> The asterisk * means “every possible value” for that field. In other words, Jenkins interprets * as 
   “match all”.
> 0-> Exactly ^53PHPyCy

Examples: ^GSBXFCdG

Use Case: ^qvRHFdq3

> Nightly builds
> Regular test runs
> Backup jobs
> Automated reports ^QQJH8i6t

1) If you write: * * * * *
> It means Jenkins will run the job every minute of every hour, every day.
> Explanation:
  * in the minute field → every minute
  * in the hour field → every hour
  * in the day of month field → every day of the month
  * in the month field → every month
  * in the day of week field → every day of the week
> So overall, this means: “Run the job all the time.”

2) If you write: 0 * * * *
>  It means Jenkins runs the job every hour, at minute 0 (for example, 12:00, 1:00, 2:00, etc.).

3) If you write: 0 2 * * *
>  It means the job will run once every day at 2 AM.

4) If you wanted it to run only on the 1st day of the month, you would write: 0 2 1 * *
>  This means: “Run at 2 AM on the 1st of every month.”

5) If you want to run the job at 2:00 AM on the 1st, 5th, 10th, and 15th days of the 
    month: 0 2 1,5,10,15 * *
>  This means: “Run at 2 AM on the 1st, 5th, 10th, and 15th of every month.”

6) If you want to run the job at 2AM of 1st day of every month: 0 2 1 * *
>  runs at 2 AM on the 1st day of every month.
>  If you wanted it to run only in January and July: 0 2 1 1,7 *
>  runs at 2 AM on the 1st of January and July.

7) If you wanted to run it only on weekdays: 0 2 * * 1-5
>  That means “run at 2 AM from Monday to Friday.”

8) If you wanted to run it for every 5 minutes: */5 * * * *
>  That means “It will trigger the build for every 5 minutes.”

9) If you wanted to run it for exactly 2 hours: 0 */2 * * *
>   That means “It will trigger the build for exactly 2 hours.” ^AGKBxhiT

    repository.

   you select this it will ask you to write your schedule, write any cron schedule as you want.

    command" option. Then write these two commands: ^MOio73zi

2. Poll SCM (Source Code Management): ^AllKEbnx

> The “Poll SCM” trigger makes Jenkins periodically check the source code repository (for example, Git) to 
  detect if there are new commits or changes.
> It is used to automatically trigger a build when new code is detected in the repository. ^OiYQlRCF

> Jenkins connects to the configured SCM repository at the specified time intervals.
> If a change is found (new commit, updated branch, etc.), the job is triggered.
> If no changes are found, Jenkins waits until the next scheduled poll. ^N918jYe6

Use Case: ^jAE9jqMR

How It Works: ^J2vqEhNC

> Poll SCM doesn’t build every time — only when it finds new changes.
> It’s useful when webhooks can’t be configured due to firewall or network restrictions. ^1AZvvmvi

Important Points: ^7GGta7zY

> Legacy systems where GitHub or GitLab webhooks are unavailable.
> Internal or private Git repositories.
> Projects where developers push code irregularly but Jenkins still needs to check. ^oVwk8oTW

Steps to run the build using Poll SCM in Windows: ^kAe3pk3y

1) Open a browser and search localhost:8080 it will open Jenkins dashboard.
2) In the sidebar you have an option called as "New Item" click on it. Give a job name and below select the 
    option called as "Freestyle project" then click on OK.
3) Give a description, scroll down and under "Source Code Management" select Git. It will ask for Repository
   URL paste the GitHub repository URL
4) Scroll down and under "Branches to Build" specify the branch name as master or main based on the GitHub
    repository.
5) Scroll down and you will get a section called as "Triggers" under that select "Poll SCM". As soon as
   you select this it will ask you to write your schedule, write any cron schedule as you want.
6) Scroll down and under "Build Steps" click on Add build step under that choose "Execute Windows batch
    command" option. Then write these two commands: ^0wHQaxsr

javac Test.java
java Test.java ^LeiStcZp

This command is used for compiling java project ^71ORv0JR

This command is used for executing java project ^5ZPEfvmR

7) Scroll down and finally click on Apply and then click on Save.
8) Here no need to click on "Build Now" it will automatically generate the first build and then if there is any 
   change in the GitHub (new commit, updated branch, etc.) only then the second build is generated based on 
   the schedule you gave. ^qaAdx0UI

Advantages: ^u4NxdKno

> This trigger enables Jenkins to start builds immediately when GitHub detects changes (for example, a new 
   commit or pull request) — without Jenkins having to poll the repository.
> It is used to provide real-time, event-driven builds instead of time-based polling.
> This works via GitHub Webhooks — that is, GitHub sends an HTTP POST request to Jenkins’ endpoint 
  (http://<jenkins-server>/github-webhook/) notifying it that something has changed in the repository. ^euvJsYNO

3. GitHub Hook Trigger for GIT SCM Polling: ^VHPX5eRk

How It Works: ^7bprMkoL

> You install and enable the GitHub Integration Plugin in Jenkins.
> You create a webhook in your GitHub repository that points to your Jenkins server, typically at this 
  endpoint: 
> Whenever someone pushes new code or merges a pull request, GitHub automatically sends a notification 
  (a webhook event) to Jenkins.
> Jenkins receives the event and immediately triggers the corresponding build. ^UAI93v2F

http://<jenkins_url>/github-webhook/ ^U0C9GejB

Use Case: ^Dc8Z9pSu

> Continuous integration pipelines for projects hosted on GitHub.
> Teams needing fast feedback on every commit or pull request. ^AlUeQXlZ

> No need to schedule periodic polling.
> Builds start instantly when code changes.
> Reduces server load. ^GPiCyUFO

Why it doesn’t work easily on Windows local machines: ^7irRPuv1

> If Jenkins is running locally on Windows (for example, http://localhost:8080), GitHub cannot reach the 
  machine directly because:
> Localhost is private and not accessible from the internet.
> GitHub Webhooks require a publicly reachable URL to send the trigger notification.
> A local Windows machine is typically behind a NAT or firewall, so GitHub cannot deliver the webhook payload.
> Even if the trigger is configured correctly, Jenkins will not receive the webhook unless it is publicly accessible. ^9ibCZM5S

Why it works on AWS (EC2): ^BRSi5hJm

> When Jenkins runs on an AWS EC2 instance, it becomes publicly accessible because:
> It has a public IP address (or DNS name like ec2-xx-xx-xx.compute.amazonaws.com).
> The necessary ports (8080, 80, or 443) can be opened in the EC2 security group to allow inbound traffic.
> The EC2 instance is always online, which is suitable for continuous integration environments.
> Therefore, GitHub can successfully send webhook events to:
> and Jenkins will immediately start the build process upon receiving the notification. ^d2oQg8yS

http://<EC2-public-dns>:8080/github-webhook/ ^ckH9Urd9

4. Build After Other Projects Are Built ^zL92lovk

> This trigger allows one Jenkins job to automatically start after another job completes successfully.
> To create a sequence or dependency between jobs, where the output of one becomes the input or condition for 
  another. ^v0gkY9V1

Suppose you have:
> Job A: Compiles and tests the source code.
> Job B: Deploys the application to staging.

You can configure Job B to “Build after other projects are built” and specify Job A as the upstream job.
This means Job B will automatically start after Job A finishes successfully. ^4uOF4eb2

Example: ^THl57B9y

> Creating a simple CI/CD chain (build → test → deploy).
> Multi-stage build processes.
> Jobs that depend on the successful completion of others.
> You can also specify whether to trigger the job on success only, or regardless of the upstream job’s result. ^reoWqFi9
