> Role-Based Authorization in Jenkins is a security system that lets you control who can access or modify 
  different parts of Jenkins.
> Instead of giving everyone full control, you assign specific permissions through roles.
> Each role defines what a user or group of users can do, such as creating jobs, building pipelines, managing 
  nodes, or viewing results. 
> It ensures that people only have access to what they need. ^ToL6BwOk

Role-Based Authorization in Jenkins ^kkWBFFOS

> By default, Jenkins either allows everyone to access it freely or restricts everything to administrators.
> This is fine for learning or a single-user setup but becomes unsafe in a shared environment.
> In a company or team project, multiple users work on Jenkins simultaneously.
> Without access control, anyone could delete jobs, expose credentials, or break pipelines.
> Role-Based Authorization fixes this by allowing fine-grained control.
> It is essential for security, order, and accountability in multi-user Jenkins servers. ^vrvcUiIN

Why Jenkins Needs Role-Based Authorization: ^aA49elh2

How It Works: ^32EUiwU5

> Jenkins uses a plugin called “Role-Based Authorization Strategy.”
> Once installed, you can define different levels of access through roles.
> Roles can be applied globally across Jenkins or only to specific jobs or nodes.
> After defining roles, you assign users to those roles.
> When users log in, they can only perform the actions allowed by their assigned role. ^vEQecFjN

Types of Roles: ^S9UDkfNM

1. Global Roles: ^4Drpz4Mp

> Global roles apply to the entire Jenkins system.
> Anyone with a global role has the same level of access everywhere — across all jobs, nodes, and configurations. ^ySqBhSEW

Example: ^uECgTBKy

> Suppose you assign the role “Admin” globally to yourself — you can do anything across Jenkins (create jobs, 
  delete jobs, configure security, etc.).
> If you assign the role “Viewer” globally to someone — that person can only view all jobs and builds, but cannot 
  modify anything. ^e4onxfvs

2. Item Roles: ^ZLU1Do4Y

> Item roles are specific to individual jobs, pipelines, or folders inside Jenkins.
> They let you restrict or allow access only to certain projects. ^b5vyxZLz

> You can create a role called “FrontendDev” and give it permissions only for jobs whose names start with 
  “frontend-*”.
> That means a user with that role can only view or build frontend-related jobs, not backend ones.
> In a company, the frontend team works only on the website UI and cannot touch backend code.
  So they get access only to “frontend” projects in Jenkins. ^JLE8FLE7

Example: ^QuqUgseP

3. Agent Roles: ^jIeUsbEx

> Agent roles define permissions for Jenkins build nodes (agents/slaves) — the machines where jobs actually run.
> They control who can configure, connect, or disconnect those agents. ^i0zhuNJv

Example: ^t1TD2Ftj

> A DevOps engineer might have an “AgentManager” role that allows connecting and disconnecting agents.
> A developer, on the other hand, does not need this permission — they only need to trigger builds.
> In an office, only IT staff can manage servers and machines, while developers just use them.
  So IT staff = Agent role, developers = no agent access. ^ut7gHyDo

Example Setup in a Team: ^pD7xOAgr

Imagine a company setup:

> You are the administrator.
> You create an EC2 instance and install Jenkins on it.
> You enable the Role-Based Authorization Strategy plugin.
> You create accounts for each team, such as developers, testers, and operations engineers.
> You define roles like Admin, Developer, and QA.
> You assign permissions according to each team’s responsibility.
> Finally, you share the credentials and login URLs with your team members.
> This way, everyone uses the same Jenkins instance safely with limited access. ^zTcI6IaS

> Every action in Jenkins (like viewing jobs, building them, deleting them, or configuring settings) is controlled by 
  a permission.
> Each permission belongs to a category such as Overall, Job, Credentials, Run, View, Agent, or SCM.
> When you create a role (for example, “Developer”), you tick certain permissions under these categories.
  Those checkboxes decide what that role is allowed to do inside Jenkins.
> So, permissions are like “keys” that unlock specific features or actions for a user. ^I6UTA6zG

Example of Permissions: ^3Dk9MKJr

Main Permission Categories: ^2ZwFGGY6

> Administer: Grants full control over Jenkins. The user can configure the entire system, install plugins, create 
                 or delete anything, manage credentials, and even change security settings. This is the highest-level
                 permission.
> Read: Allows a user to log in and view Jenkins pages but not make any changes. It’s the most basic permission, 
          often given to “Viewer” roles. ^36Hxa9Ot

These permissions control access to Jenkins as a whole. ^Jd0Mjl3e

A. Overall: ^awBSjX5K

> Developers may only need “View” to use stored credentials in builds.
> Admins usually have all credential permissions. ^EFrvxYGy

> Create: Allows adding new credentials (for example, adding a GitHub token).
> Delete: Allows deleting saved credentials.
> ManageDomains: Allows organizing credentials into separate groups or domains.
> Update: Allows modifying existing credentials (for example, changing a password).
> View: Allows viewing the list of credentials (but not the actual passwords or keys). ^F1Jd7MZS

These permissions control who can access or modify the stored secrets in Jenkins (like passwords, 
SSH keys, AWS keys, tokens, etc.). ^flAPkMQt

B. Credentials: ^T4mgekry

Example: ^EqucQEYE

> A Developer role may have Create, Configure, Build, and Read.
> A Viewer role usually has only Read.
> Only Admins should have Delete or Move, because deleting a job can remove all build history. ^acAI5SnR

Create: Allows creating new jobs or pipelines.
Configure: Allows editing job settings, such as build triggers or SCM details.
Build: Allows manually starting a build.
Read: Allows viewing the job configuration, console output, and build history.
Delete: Allows deleting an entire job.
Cancel: Allows stopping a currently running build.
Move: Allows moving a job between folders.
Workspace: Allows accessing the workspace (the folder on the agent where build files are stored). ^GOr1U674

These permissions control what users can do with Jenkins jobs or pipelines. ^Qnw4vrI6

C. Job: ^k11kZx7w

Example: ^O8CuMQpe

> A QA or Developer role might have Replay (to rerun failed builds) and Read.
> Admins usually have all Run permissions. ^z7PlHNDj

> Delete: Allows deleting old builds.
> Replay: Allows re-running a previous pipeline build with the same configuration.
> Update: Allows editing metadata of a specific build, such as its description or labels. ^W9ADjDrL

These permissions control access to specific build runs of jobs (for example, Build #1, Build #2, etc.). ^gXuTw9iZ

D. Run: ^B1Rr4T0d

Example: ^sh07qFut

> A Team Lead could have Create and Configure to organize jobs.
> Regular developers or QA usually only need Read. ^krZSx5KZ

> Create: Allows creating a new view (for example, “QA Jobs”).
> Configure: Allows editing what jobs appear in a view.
> Delete: Allows deleting views.
> Read: Allows seeing the views. ^hPcL29a9

These permissions control who can create or edit dashboard views (custom pages grouping certain jobs). ^69tjXSET

E. View: ^Nl9NAuVN

Example: ^KDitn1Fp

> Developers and QA usually don’t touch agents.
> DevOps or Admin roles have these permissions because they manage the Jenkins infrastructure. ^CY6okxpC

> Connect: Allows connecting a build node to Jenkins.
> Create: Allows adding a new agent.
> Delete: Allows removing an agent.
> Disconnect: Allows disconnecting an agent temporarily.
> Provision: Allows provisioning agents dynamically (for example, from a cloud).
> Build: Allows running builds on an agent. ^ElpTQd0J

These permissions deal with Jenkins agents (build nodes that actually run the jobs). ^cHQhzJUa

F. Agent: ^aeUtHyiq

Example: ^hItFuHSW

> A Release Manager or DevOps engineer may have this permission to create release tags in Git. ^djz2oZW1

> Tag: Allows tagging a source control repository during a build. ^HechpjdO

This stands for Source Code Management (like Git or Subversion). ^2ZppzGUO

G. SCM: ^xkqApJkX

Example: ^x6qYeJNS

1) Copy the public ip of jenkins instance and paste it in a new tab along with the port number 8080 in this 
   format:
   this will open Jenkins in that tab.
2) Once you are inside Jenkins, Go to Manage Jenkins option. Under "System Configuration" select "Plugins".
    under Available plugins, search "Role-based Authorization Strategy" and install it.
3) Then Go back to Manage Jenkins, and this time under "Security" select "Security". Under "Security Realm"
    select "Jenkins' own user database" and under "Authorization" select "Role-Based Strategy". Scroll down
    till the end and click on Apply and then click on Save.
4) Next under "Security" select "Manage and Assign Roles" option and then under "Role to add" add the role
   name write Developer for example (Roles --- Developer, Test engineer, DevOps enggineer etc ...) and click 
   on Add.
5) You will see that as soon as you click on Add, under "Global Roles" Developer option is created. Here
    Tick on these: Overall: Read, Job: Build, Configure, Create, Read, Workspace. These permissions are 
    enough for a Developers.
6) To create the users for the Developers role go to "Manage Jenkins" and under "Security", select "Users".
    then click on "Create User". It will ask for the Username, Password, e-mail ..... Fill all the credentials for
    the users for who you want to assign the developer role.
7) Now that the user is created go to "Manage Jenkins" option and under "Security" select 
   "Manage and Assign Roles" option and in the sidebar you can see an option called as "Assign roles" click on
    it. Here you have a button called as "Add user" click on it. It will ask you for UserID, give the username
    and then tick on user based on his/her role (Developer). Finally scroll down and click on Apply and Save.
8) To cross verify, logout from your account and log in to the account by using credentials of the user you
    have created and then verify the permissions you gave under roles are working or not. ^qrvwbo2Z

Steps to assign role based authorization: ^IPLYx86O

http://13.208.244.230:8080 ^b070CNjU

3) Pipeline Jobs and it's types: ^VCOLVhQW

> A Pipeline Job in Jenkins is a type of job that allows you to define your entire build process using a 
  Jenkinsfile — a script written in Groovy-based DSL (Domain Specific Language).
> This file describes stages, steps, and conditions that define how your CI/CD pipeline runs.
> It turns your build process into code, often called Pipeline as Code.
> Basically it is a script to perform sequence of steps which will perform tasks such as Building, testing and
  deploying. ^9ytu1IYA

Traditional jobs (like Freestyle or Maven) are limited — you click buttons to configure steps manually. 
Pipeline jobs, however:
> Are automated and repeatable
> Can be version controlled (Jenkinsfile stored in Git)
> Handle complex workflows like parallel builds, multiple environments, approvals, etc.
> Survive Jenkins restarts (thanks to durability and checkpoints)
> Are portable between Jenkins instances ^mGUA0l6a

Why Pipelines? ^PFWPuT2o

Types of Pipelines: ^cw6TxzmQ

1) Declarative Pipeline
2) Scripted Pipeline ^TaMlZahb

1. Declarative Pipeline: ^ThthNfwH

> A Declarative Pipeline in Jenkins is a simplified and structured way to define your 
  Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) process using a predefined syntax.
> It is designed to make pipeline creation easier and more readable.
> Declarative pipelines use the keyword pipeline {} as the root block, and have a defined hierarchy with specific 
  sections such as agent, stages, steps, post, etc.
> They are preferred for most use cases because they are easier to read, less error-prone, and automatically 
   handle errors. ^yJLYOmTJ

Steps to implement declarative pipeline ^oAGTyefU

1) Open a browser and search localhost:8080 it will open Jenkins dashboard.
2) In the sidebar you have an option called as "New Item" click on it. Give a job name and below select the option 
   called as "Pipeline" then click on OK.
3) Give a description, scroll down and under "Pipeline" you have to write a script. Instead of writing the script choose
   the option "Hello world" from the right corner. It will provide you a sample script.
4) If you want to clone a GitHub repository then click on the "Pipeline Syntax" link. It will open a new tab where under
   "Sample Step" choose git:Git
5) Paste the URL of the repository:
6) Then verify the branch as master or main and then click on "Generate Pipeline Script". It will give you a readymade
    command:
7) Copy that command and paste it under stage('Git-Clone') and steps like this: ^O5848rDV

https://github.com/Sumukha47/java.git ^kToIS4nB

git 'https://github.com/Sumukha47/java.git' ^Z2iKDd18

8) Now if you want to execute java commands then once again click on "Pipeline Syntax" link. It will open a new tab 
   where under "Sample Step" choose "bat: Windows Batch Script". Under "Batch Script" write down these commands:



  After writing these commands, click on "Generate Pipeline Script" it will give you a command, copy the command and 
  paste it as shown in the above script. The command looks like this: ^4T8J1YfW

pipeline {
    agent any

    stages {
        stage('Git-Clone') {
            steps {
                git 'https://github.com/Sumukha47/java.git'
            }
        }
        stage('Java-Project') {
            steps {
                bat '''javac Test.java
                    java Test.java'''
            }
        }
    }
} ^j1RWkNtT

javac Test.java
java Test.java ^aGOLrGAw

bat '''javac Test.java
java Test.java''' ^PX4oCJfp

9) Scroll down and finally click on Apply and then click on Save.
10) In the side bar you have an option called as "Build Now" click on that. You can see a build is being generated click 
    on that once after it is generated. ^PBXrqKSl

2. Scripted Pipeline: ^OIWsHPPH

> A Scripted Pipeline is written entirely in Groovy syntax.
> Unlike Declarative Pipelines (which follow a fixed structure), scripted pipelines give maximum control over build flow using 
  standard Groovy programming constructs like:
-> Variables
-> Loops
-> Conditions (if, else)
-> Functions (methods)
-> Exception handling (try, catch) ^p3G8xlAI

> Groovy is a dynamic scripting language built on top of Java.
> It runs on the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) and integrates seamlessly with Java code and libraries.
> You can think of it as a simpler, more flexible version of Java — designed for automation, configuration, and scripting
   tasks.
> (Groovy is both a programming and scripting language, but in Jenkins pipelines, it is primarily used 
  as a scripting language to automate and control the pipeline flow) ^uLZoOWM6

What is Groovy scripting language? ^9UQtmcHv

> Shell scripts (bash, sh, etc.) are designed to execute operating system commands. They have no inherent knowledge 
  of, or control over, the Jenkins automation server itself. When you use the sh step inside a Jenkinsfile, you are 
  actually asking Groovy (the orchestrator) to call the Shell interpreter (the external tool) on a specific agent machine 
  to run a sequence of commands.
> The keywords like pipeline, agent, stages, and steps are not recognized Shell commands; they are Groovy methods 
  (or syntax elements) provided by the Jenkins Pipeline plugin. The Jenkins Controller executes the Jenkinsfile by 
  interpreting this Groovy code.
> Groovy: Defines the control flow and manages the Jenkins environment. It tells Jenkins: "Execute this stage on that 
             agent, and only if this condition is met."
> Shell: Performs the low-level work. It tells the operating system: "Run this compiler, copy this file, or execute this 
         test."
> A sandbox is a safe environment where Jenkins runs your Groovy code, allowing only approved and safe operations. ^uhPjQU1J

Diffference between Shell scripting and Groovy scripting: ^fy0c0X4E

> Both Declarative and Scripted Pipelines in Jenkins use the Groovy language — but they use it in different ways and 
  at different abstraction levels.

Scripted pipeline: Pure Groovy (programming/scripting language): 
> You write actual Groovy code directly — with full access to Groovy features like variables, loops, conditions, and 
  functions.

Declarative pipeline: Domain-Specific Language (DSL) built on top of Groovy: 
> You use a structured syntax (pipeline {}, stages {}, steps {}) that Jenkins internally converts into Groovy before 
  execution. ^KbzW4JjN

Languages used by Declarative pipeline and scripted pipeline: ^lvq8CF1T

Declarative Pipeline vs Scripted Pipeline — When to Use Which ^tetFONl5

> Declarative pipelines are designed to be simple, structured, and human-readable. They are best for standard, 
   predictable, and maintainable Jenkins pipelines.
> Use declarative pipeline when you want Jenkins to handle error recovery automatically (post block handles success, 
  failure, always.)
> Declarative pipeline is ideal for standard build/test/deploy pipelines with clear structure.
> within Declarative pipelines, you can use script {} blocks to insert small Groovy logic when needed.
> Scripted pipelines give you full control and programming flexibility because they are written in pure Groovy. They are 
  best when your pipeline logic depends on dynamic conditions, loops, or custom Groovy code.
> Scripted pipelines provides you full flexibility (custom logic possible).
> Scripting pipelines can be used for complex logic, loops, dynamic decisions, or older pipeline setups. ^QiDAbwUB

Steps to implement scripted pipeline ^h94gmBBV

1) Open a browser and search localhost:8080 it will open Jenkins dashboard.
2) In the sidebar you have an option called as "New Item" click on it. Give a job name and below select the option 
   called as "Pipeline" then click on OK.
3) Give a description, scroll down and under "Pipeline" you have to write a script. Instead of writing the script choose
   the option "Scripted Pipeline" from the right corner. It will provide you a sample script. Modify the sample script 
   however you want.
4) If you want to clone a GitHub repository then click on the "Pipeline Syntax" link. It will open a new tab where under
   "Sample Step" choose git:Git
5) Paste the URL of the repository:
6) Then verify the branch as master or main and then click on "Generate Pipeline Script". It will give you a readymade
    command:
7) Copy that command and paste it under stage('Git-Clone') and steps like this: ^ac2FCQRD

https://github.com/Sumukha47/java.git ^zDGFCzQy

git 'https://github.com/Sumukha47/java.git' ^1kPlVTA3

8) Now if you want to execute java commands then once again click on "Pipeline Syntax" link. It will open a new tab 
   where under "Sample Step" choose "bat: Windows Batch Script". Under "Batch Script" write down these commands:



  After writing these commands, click on "Generate Pipeline Script" it will give you a command, copy the command and 
  paste it as shown in the above script. The command looks like this: ^skm5Fr62

node {
    stage('Git-clone') {
        git 'https://github.com/Sumukha47/java.git'
    }
      stage('java-commands') {
        bat '''javac Test.java
        java Test.java'''
    }
} ^vKjOuHaZ

javac Test.java
java Test.java ^AbUHqnmS

bat '''javac Test.java
java Test.java''' ^Zd45txYJ

9) Scroll down and finally click on Apply and then click on Save.
10) In the side bar you have an option called as "Build Now" click on that. You can see a build is being generated click 
    on that once after it is generated. ^1n8NpKN8

Commonly Used Jenkins Environment Variables ^KoIGiAXz

Variable                             Description                                               Example ^TU1mWpDI

env.JOB_NAME                    Name of the current job                spring-petclinic

env.BUILD_NUMBER             Build number                                     42

env.BUILD_ID                    Build ID (includes date/time)      2025-11-04_09-45-21

env.BUILD_URL                  URL of the build in Jenkins         http://localhost:8080/job/spring-petclinic/42/

env.WORKSPACE                  Path to workspace directory       C:\Jenkins\workspace\spring-petclinic

env.NODE_NAME                  Node/agent name                     master

env.GIT_COMMIT                Git commit hash (only if Git 
                                      SCM is used)                          a7c9d3f

env.GIT_BRANCH                 Git branch name                        main

env.JENKINS_URL                 Base URL of Jenkins                  http://localhost:8080/

env.BUILD_TIMESTAMP        you can configure time format 
                                      globally                                  Build started at: 2025-11-04 11:42:18 IST

${env.BUILD_URL}               Full Jenkins build URL             http://localhost:8080/job/sample-java-declarative-job/15/

${env.BUILD_URL}artifact/    Direct link to artifacts
                                       for that build                       http://localhost:8080/job/sample-java-declarative-job/15/artifact/

${env.BUILD_URL}console      Direct link to build logs            http://localhost:8080/job/sample-java-declarative-job/15/console ^hwMmtIjO

node {
    
    def gitrepo = 'https://github.com/Sumukha47/mavenproject.git'
    def stages = ['compile','test','package']
    
    stage('Git-clone'){
        git gitrepo
    }
        for (i in stages) 
        {
            stage (i)
            {
                if (i == 'compile')
                {
                     bat 'mvn compile'
                }
               if (i == 'test')
                {
                     bat 'mvn test'
                }
                 if (i == 'package')
                {
                     bat 'mvn package'
                }
            }
        }
} ^X6WGiSsI

Example script for sample maven project: ^TXW2J2Rz

A Declarative Pipeline in Jenkins follows a strict structure. Every valid pipeline starts with a pipeline {} block and
contains several optional sections. Here’s the general structure: ^4y7MPHEF

The Structure of a Declarative Pipeline ^mS6Od40m

pipeline {
    agent any
    environment { }
    parameters { }
    options { }
    triggers { }
    tools { }
    stages {
        stage('Example') {
            steps {
                script { }
            }
        }
    }
    post {
        success { }
        failure { }
        always { }
    }
} ^E03YXyqr

> This is the root block of any Jenkins Declarative Pipeline.
> All other sections must be defined inside this block.
> It defines the beginning and end of the pipeline.
> Without this block, Jenkins will not recognize the file as a declarative pipeline. ^OS5RMWDl

Explanation of Each Block ^GhPY39He

1. pipeline ^Mx03Djzl

Example: ^NfIYAcaH

pipeline {
    // all inner blocks go here
} ^3rYxG4QU

> This block defines where the pipeline or a stage will run.
> It tells Jenkins which node, machine, or environment to use to execute the job. ^eSWWVLOA

2. agent ^Q3rYflyd

Common Options: ^8A3Cj4El

> agent any → Run on any available Jenkins agent.
> agent none → Used if you want to specify agent separately inside each stage.
> agent { label 'linux' } → Run on a node with a specific label.
> agent { docker 'maven:3.9.0' } → Run inside a Docker container. ^s7HXXu4M

> This block is used to define environment variables that are available throughout the pipeline.
> Environment variables store reusable values such as file paths, credentials, or application names.
> You can use these later as ${APP_NAME} or ${env.APP_NAME}. ^y5d0wuM7

3. environment ^slsTVGuK

environment {
    APP_NAME = "spring-petclinic"
    BUILD_DIR = "target"
} ^dcGy2XOm

Example: ^xw0a5U1x

> This block allows users to provide input to the pipeline before it runs.
> It is useful when you want to build different branches, versions, or configurations using the same Jenkinsfile.
> This creates a text box on the Jenkins job page where you can specify a branch name before running. ^KD6EmUVV

4. parameters ^J5HRfADj

parameters {
    string(name: 'BRANCH', defaultValue: 'main', description: 'Branch to build from')
} ^VnnnvuzT

Example: ^e8VghOfF

> This block defines pipeline-wide settings that control behavior such as timeouts, build history retention, and concurrency. ^vMPLJy0C

5. options ^rxhHlkVW

> timeout(time: 10, unit: 'MINUTES') – Stops the build if it runs longer than 10 minutes.
> buildDiscarder(logRotator(numToKeepStr: '5')) – Keeps only the last 5 builds.
> disableConcurrentBuilds() – Prevents overlapping builds. ^3LQCkpoQ

Common options: ^0LiNvtWd

Example: ^4LQD85nQ

options {
    timeout(time: 15, unit: 'MINUTES')
    buildDiscarder(logRotator(numToKeepStr: '5'))
} ^8rqHn46T

> A stage is a logical section within stages.
> Each stage can have multiple steps that define the commands to execute.
> It is used to separate tasks like building, testing, packaging, and deploying. ^fl3WTSJO

9. stage ^m1h1q21k

Example: ^dzdgd2Jb

stage('Build') {
    steps {
        bat 'mvn clean package'
    }
} ^1NKIihDo

10. steps ^WoRhvrWl

> This block defines the actual commands or tasks Jenkins will execute inside a stage. ^HFqL0Urz

Examples of steps: ^ZmHZ1bAe

> bat – Run Windows batch commands.
> sh – Run shell commands on Linux/Mac.
> git – Clone a Git repository.
> archiveArtifacts – Save build artifacts in Jenkins. ^uIAMEHiw

Example: ^3MQpK7GX

steps {
    bat 'mvn clean package'
} ^1IngTTWX

script {
    if (env.BRANCH_NAME == 'main') {
        echo 'This is the main branch build.'
    }
} ^UlZH5lUb

11. script ^paAfT4pm

> This block allows custom Groovy scripting inside a Declarative Pipeline.
> Useful when you need logic such as if, for, or variable manipulation. ^aRTXnvU4

Example: ^ylF2Qbjg

> This block defines actions that happen after the pipeline finishes, regardless of success or failure.
> You can place this block at the pipeline level or inside an individual stage. ^Fl9SKjN8

12. post ^ITwOSHUe

Subsections: ^HbcZdMjM

Example: ^kntQJn3U

> success – Runs only if the build succeeds.
> failure – Runs only if the build fails.
> always – Runs every time (cleanup or notifications). ^8kXmiFHY

post {
    success {
        echo 'Build succeeded!'
    }
    failure {
        echo 'Build failed.'
    }
    always {
        echo 'Cleaning up...'
        cleanWs()
    }
} ^ri4Km5qB

node('agent-label') {

    // 1. PARAMETERS (accessed using params.PARAM_NAME)
    // 2. ENVIRONMENT VARIABLES
    // 3. TOOL SETUP
    // 4. BUILD TRIGGERS
    // 5. MAIN LOGIC INSIDE TRY-CATCH-FINALLY
    // 6. FAILURE HANDLING
    // 8. END MESSAGE

} ^S7dXxJ97

The Structure of a Scripted Pipeline ^Sis6tPwD

Explanation of Each Block ^9S2TCuRf

node('windows') {
    echo "Running on Windows node"
} ^2KF61wwJ

1. node ^hUg72UT6

> The node block allocates an executor (agent) on a Jenkins node (where the build will run).
> All actual build steps are written inside this block.
> It acts like the “workspace” where all operations occur. ^IYlAL0Gy

Example: ^AMtPCrfa

> A logical division of your pipeline — each stage represents one phase (e.g., Checkout, Build, Test, Deploy).
> It is used for visualization and separation of steps in Jenkins UI. ^tvkC2x3x

2. stage Block ^ksPZYU30

Example: ^vSts194i

stage('Test') {
    echo "Running unit tests..."
} ^qyUERYf5

3. environment Variables ^Mq7cf3c5

> You can define variables to be reused throughout your pipeline.
> Stores data like credentials, paths, or configuration that multiple stages use. ^tkxbAmyf

Example: ^Ku6uCyco

env.BRANCH_NAME = "main"
echo "Building branch ${env.BRANCH_NAME}" ^Hj0XA9KD

> Used to get user input before the job starts (e.g., branch name, build type).
> It makes the pipeline configurable at runtime. ^pFXzzqYY

4. Parameters ^XwY9Rbmc

Syntax: ^LNIXBRY7

properties([
    parameters([
        string(name: 'PARAM1', defaultValue: 'default', description: 'Example parameter'),
        booleanParam(name: 'DEPLOY', defaultValue: true, description: 'Deploy after build')
    ])
]) ^fnnBgSf0

echo "Parameter received: ${params.PARAM1}" ^kNdIqltm

Example: ^EWu1Ni35

5. Tools ^CWZLny15

> Automatically installs and configures required tools (e.g., JDK, Maven, Git).
> It simplifies environment setup. ^Apd2W48P

def mvnHome = tool name: 'Maven3', type: 'maven' ^rtAj1vbQ

Syntax: ^K5uV8QQL

Example: ^jHeyCK1B

def javaHome = tool name: 'JDK11', type: 'jdk'
env.PATH = "${javaHome}/bin:${env.PATH}" ^dtr0NVBO

6. Triggers ^CGFCmNB8

> Used to schedule automatic build execution.
> It automates the pipeline (e.g., daily builds, SCM polling). ^QRW4nFsa

properties([
    pipelineTriggers([
        cron('H 2 * * 1-5'),       // Run every weekday at 2 AM
        pollSCM('* * * * *')       // Poll every minute
    ])
]) ^eHQrACje

Syntax: ^mP71JSoT

7. try-catch-finally (Post Actions) ^Gaka2N2L

> Scripted Pipelines do not have a post {} block like Declarative Pipelines.
> Instead, we handle success, failure, and always conditions using try-catch-finally.
> Control flow management and handling notifications. ^fAAsw6oc

try {
    // Normal build steps
} catch (err) {
    // Runs on failure
} finally {
    // Runs always (cleanup, notifications)
} ^is1CfEfq

Syntax: ^ttIcUeDf

> This block defines automatic build triggers, meaning when and how Jenkins should automatically start this job. ^vNaqp9oR

6. triggers ^SwLGllF9

Common Triggers: ^GJ4j05it

> pollSCM('*/5 * * * *') – Poll the Git repository every 5 minutes for changes.
> cron('0 2 * * 1-5') – Schedule builds using a cron expression (e.g., every weekday at 2 AM).
> upstream('OtherJob') – Trigger when another Jenkins job completes. ^qCIsQuTV

Example: ^UuxtQNsR

triggers {
    pollSCM('*/5 * * * *')
} ^pRMByYQ6

7. tools ^obBEmthl

> This block declares auto-installed tools (like JDK, Maven, or Git) that Jenkins should use.
> The tools must already be configured in Manage Jenkins → Global Tool Configuration.
> This ensures that Jenkins uses the specified JDK and Maven versions for the build. ^gaWQfkwT

Example: ^ZdAHEl5Q

tools {
    jdk 'JDK11'
    maven 'Maven3'
} ^qiKe3v1c

Example: ^WF5OFIUa

8. stages ^LKXOULSN

> This block contains all the stages of the pipeline.
> Each stage represents a major phase of the build, such as “Build”, “Test”, “Deploy”.
> Organizes your CI/CD process into logical steps and provides visual clarity in Jenkins. ^paZ2cSn0

stages {
    stage('Build') {
        steps {
            echo 'Building the project...'
        }
    }
} ^WDxmQL7g

Steps to enable email notification in jenkins ^IZil8gaq

1) Open a browser and search localhost:8080 it will open Jenkins dashboard.
2) In the sidebar you have an option called as "New Item" click on it. Give a job name and below select the option 
   called as "Pipeline" then click on OK.
3) Give a description, scroll down and under "Pipeline" you have to write a script. Instead of writing the script choose
   the option "Hello world" from the right corner. It will provide you a sample script.
4) If you want to clone a GitHub repository then click on the "Pipeline Syntax" link. It will open a new tab where under
   "Sample Step" choose git:Git
5) Paste the URL of the repository:
6) Then verify the branch as master or main and then click on "Generate Pipeline Script". It will give you a readymade
    command:
7) Copy that command and paste it under stage('Git-Clone') and steps like this: ^BgTJRAGf

https://github.com/Sumukha47/java.git ^syVmYaz2

git 'https://github.com/Sumukha47/java.git' ^2Ak5Wg24

8) Now if you want to execute java commands then once again click on "Pipeline Syntax" link. It will open a new tab 
   where under "Sample Step" choose "bat: Windows Batch Script". Under "Batch Script" write down these commands:



  After writing these commands, click on "Generate Pipeline Script" it will give you a command, copy the command and 
  paste it as shown in the above script. The command looks like this: ^obuK6NP8

pipeline {
    agent any

    stages {
        stage('Git-Clone') {
            steps {
                git 'https://github.com/Sumukha47/java.git'
            }
        }
        stage('Java-Project') {
            steps {
                bat '''javac Test.java
                    java Test.java'''
            }
        }
    }
} ^jSadXcJx

javac Test.java
java Test.java ^RSKEfJLD

bat '''javac Test.java
java Test.java''' ^MsB4RuoY

9) Scroll down and finally click on Apply and then click on Save.
10) Now that you have created a declarative pipeline, we need to enable e-mail notification for the successful build we 
    have created. For that go to manage jenkins and install "Email Extension Template Plugin".


11) Go to your gmail and at the top right corner you can see your account logo. click on it and select "Manage your 
    Google account".
12) After this in the sidebar you will find an option called as "Security" select that and scroll down and make sure
     "2 step verification is enabled". If it is not enabled, enable it.
13) Next search for "App passwords" in the searchbar and select it.
14) Under "To create a new app specific password, type a name for it below..." write the name as Jenkins and then
     click on create.
15) You will get a password copy and paste it somewhere in your machine.
16) Now once again go back to Jenkins and go to manage jenkins. Then under "System Configuration" select "System"
     scroll down till the end and you will find an option called as "E-mail Notification". Under this option, you will find
     "SMTP server" write
17) Then click on "Advanced" and tick on "Use SMTP Authentication" and give your e-mail. Next paste the password which
     you have saved. Tick on "Use SSL". Under "SMTP Port" give 465. Then tick on "Test configuration by sending test 
     e-mail" and give your e-mail and then click on test configuration. Check whether the e-mail is sent or not. 
18) Go to the declarative-pipeline which you have created and click on configure and then under script add a post script
     after stages like one given in example. ^lT3FqQST

smtp.gmail.com ^WTMvaOU5

19) To get this part: ^QuOjPUjK

mail bcc: '', 
     body: 'This is to inform you that the build is successful', 
     cc: 'akshathabm20@gmail.com', 
     from: 'sumukhadaring@gmail.com',
     replyTo: '', 
     subject: 'Build Success', 
     to: 'sumukhadaring@gmail.com' ^ZMLJqftH

Click on "Pipeline Syntax". It will open a new tab where under "Sample step" choose "mail:Mail" and specify for whom you 
want to send the mail (to, cc, bcc, subject, body) and finally click on "Generate pipeline script". You will be getting a script.
copy and paste it under post ----> success block and also make necessary changes like given in the example. ^40NQD6AH

pipeline {

    agent any

    environment {
        APP_NAME = "spring-petclinic"
    }

    parameters {
        string(name: 'BRANCH', defaultValue: 'main', description: 'Branch to build from')
    }

    options {
        timeout(time: 10, unit: 'MINUTES')
    }

    triggers {
        githubPush()
    }


    tools {
        jdk "java-home-25"
        maven "maven-home"
    }

    stages {

        stage('Git Checkout') {
            steps {
                git branch: "${params.BRANCH}", url: 'https://github.com/Sumukha47/spring-petclinic.git'
            }
        }

        stage('Build and Package') {
            steps {
                sh 'mvn package'
            }
        }

        stage('Artifact Extraction') {
            steps {
                archiveArtifacts artifacts: 'target/*.jar', fingerprint: true
            }
        }
    }

    post {

        success {
            echo "Build succeeded for ${APP_NAME}"

            mail(
                to: 'sumukhadaring@gmail.com',
                cc: 'akshathabm20@gmail.com',
                subject: "SUCCESS: ${APP_NAME} Build #${env.BUILD_NUMBER}",
                body: """Hello Developer,

Jenkins Pipeline for **${env.JOB_NAME}** completed successfully.

Build Details:
- Build Number: ${env.BUILD_NUMBER}
- Build URL: ${env.BUILD_URL}
- Console Logs: ${env.BUILD_URL}console
- Artifacts Download: ${env.BUILD_URL}artifact/target/

Best regards,
Sumukha Upadhyaya
"""
            )
        }

        failure {
            mail(
                to: 'sumukhadaring@gmail.com',
                cc: 'akshathabm20@gmail.com',
                subject: "FAILURE: ${APP_NAME} Build #${env.BUILD_NUMBER}",
                body: """Hello Developer,

Jenkins Pipeline for **${env.JOB_NAME}** has failed.

Build URL: ${env.BUILD_URL}
Console Logs: ${env.BUILD_URL}console
(If generated) Artifacts: ${env.BUILD_URL}artifact/target/

Please check the logs to resolve the issue.

Best regards,
Sumukha Upadhyaya
"""
            )
        }
    }
} ^0JyJiwla

Note:
> To configure Jenkins to automatically install the necessary Java and Maven versions install a plugin called as "Eclipse 
  Temurin installer Plugin". Depending on your Jenkins version — this plugin adds “Install from adoptium.net” option.
  Same works for Maven.
> If you want your Build_stamp to be used while writing e-mail notification, Install a plugin called as "Build Timestamp 
  plugin". ^NYx9t6Gy

node {

    // --- Environment variables ---
    env.APP_NAME = "spring-petclinic"

    // --- Tools configuration ---
    def JAVA_HOME = tool name: 'java-home-25', type: 'jdk'
    def MAVEN_HOME = tool name: 'maven-home', type: 'maven'
    env.PATH = "${JAVA_HOME}/bin:${MAVEN_HOME}/bin:${env.PATH}"

    // --- Pipeline properties ---
    properties([
        parameters([
            string(name: 'BRANCH', defaultValue: 'main', description: 'Branch to build from')
        ]),
        pipelineTriggers([
            githubPush()  // Webhook trigger for GitHub push events
        ])
    ])

    // Correct usage: timeout wraps your entire build logic
    timeout(time: 10, unit: 'MINUTES') {
        try {
            stage('Git Checkout') {
                echo "Checking out branch: ${params.BRANCH}"
                git branch: "${params.BRANCH}", url: 'https://github.com/Sumukha47/spring-petclinic.git'
            }

            stage('Build and Package') {
                echo "Building project using Maven..."
                sh 'mvn package'
            }

            stage('Artifact Extraction') {
                echo "Archiving build artifacts..."
                archiveArtifacts artifacts: 'target/*.jar', fingerprint: true
            }

            // --- Success message ---
            echo "Build succeeded for ${env.APP_NAME}"

            // --- Success email ---
            mail(
                to: 'sumukhadaring@gmail.com',
                cc: 'akshathabm20@gmail.com',
                subject: "SUCCESS: ${env.APP_NAME} Build #${env.BUILD_NUMBER}",
                body: """Hello Developer,

Jenkins Pipeline for **${env.JOB_NAME}** completed successfully.

Build Details:
- Build Number: ${env.BUILD_NUMBER}
- Build URL: ${env.BUILD_URL}
- Console Logs: ${env.BUILD_URL}console
- Artifacts Download: ${env.BUILD_URL}artifact/target/

Best regards,
Sumukha Upadhyaya
"""
            )

        } catch (err) {
            // --- Failure message ---
            echo "Build failed for ${env.APP_NAME}: ${err}"

            // --- Failure email ---
            mail(
                to: 'sumukhadaring@gmail.com',
                cc: 'akshathabm20@gmail.com',
                subject: "FAILURE: ${env.APP_NAME} Build #${env.BUILD_NUMBER}",
                body: """Hello Developer,

Jenkins Pipeline for **${env.JOB_NAME}** has failed.

Build URL: ${env.BUILD_URL}
Console Logs: ${env.BUILD_URL}console
(If generated) Artifacts: ${env.BUILD_URL}artifact/target/

Please check the logs to resolve the issue.

Best regards,
Sumukha Upadhyaya
"""
            )

            currentBuild.result = 'FAILURE'
            throw err
        }
    }
} ^DTaOjKKn

Example using declarative pipeline (For Jenkins instance) ^dJW8qAPb

Example using scripted pipeline (For Jenkins instance) ^gryKFCNy

pipeline {
    agent any

    environment {
        APP_NAME = "spring-framework-petclinic"
    }

    stages {
        stage('Git-Clone') {
            steps {
                git branch: 'main', url: 'https://github.com/Sumukha47/spring-framework-petclinic.git'
            }
        }

        stage('Package') {
            steps {
                sh 'mvn clean package'
            }
        }
        stage('Artifact Extraction') {
            steps {
                archiveArtifacts artifacts: 'target/*.war', fingerprint: true
            }
        }
        stage('SonarQube Analysis') {
            steps {
                withSonarQubeEnv(installationName: 'sonarqube', credentialsId: 'jenkins-sonar-token') {
                    sh 'mvn verify sonar:sonar'
                }
            }
        }

        stage('Upload to Nexus') {
            steps {
                nexusArtifactUploader artifacts: [[
                    artifactId: 'spring-framework-petclinic',
                    classifier: '',
                    file: 'target/petclinic.war',
                    type: 'war'
                ]],
                credentialsId: 'nexus',
                groupId: 'org.springframework.samples',
                nexusUrl: '18.60.117.115:8081',
                nexusVersion: 'nexus3',
                protocol: 'http',
                repository: 'spring-petclinic',
                version: '6.2.8'
            }
        }

        stage('Deploy to Tomcat') {
            steps {
                sshagent(['tomcat']) {
                    sh 'scp -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no target/petclinic.war ubuntu@16.112.59.99:/home/ubuntu/apache-tomcat-10.1.48/webapps/'
                }
            }
        }
    }

    post {
        success {
            mail bcc: '', 
                 body: 'This is to inform you that the build is successful', 
                 cc: 'akshathabm20@gmail.com', 
                 from: 'sumukhadaring@gmail.com',
                 replyTo: '', 
                 subject: 'Build Success', 
                 to: 'sumukhadaring@gmail.com'
        }

        failure {
            mail bcc: '', 
                 body: 'This is to inform you that the build generation failed', 
                 cc: 'akshathabm20@gmail.com', 
                 from: 'sumukhadaring@gmail.com',
                 replyTo: '', 
                 subject: 'Build failed', 
                 to: 'sumukhadaring@gmail.com'

        }
    }
}
 ^P8vfRkIc

Master-slave architecture ^ygw769J8

> The Master–Slave Architecture (also called Controller–Agent in newer terminology) is a distributed computing model 
  where:
-> One system (the Master) controls and coordinates multiple Slave systems.
-> The Slave systems execute tasks as directed by the Master and report results back.
> This structure allows parallel processing, scalability, and fault isolation — essential for large-scale applications and 
  CI/CD systems. ^18daV2GF

Process Example: ^aCKKFoMV

> The Master receives a job or task request.
> It breaks it into smaller sub-tasks.
> Each Slave executes one or more sub-tasks.
> The results are sent back to the Master, which aggregates or processes the results. ^UnFnrGc5

Slave Instances ^AI69YPkd

to the slaves ^SeQIZhxh

Steps to implement master slave architecture: ^mmsxxiq4

1) Create an instance and install Jenkins on it and login to Jenkins. This is my master instance.
2) Create another instance and install Java on it (No need to install Jenkins on this instance). This instance is my 
    slave instance. Also while creating Instance create a new key-pair and save it in your machine for further use.
3) Go to Jenkins and select "Manage Jenkins" option. In this under "System Configuration" you have an option called
   as "Nodes" select that and click on "New Node".
4) Give the node name and then select "Permanent Agent" option and then click on create.
5) Once you click on create then give any description. Under "Number of executors" give it as 2 (Number is based on 
    the instance's vCPUs).
6) For "Remote root directory", go to your Jenkins instance (Master) and then write these commands:




   You will get                            paste it for "Remote root directory".
7) For "Labels" give any name (like developer, test-engineer etc....).
8) For "Usage" you will get 2 options: 
   a) "Use this node as much as possible"
   b) "Only build jobs with label expressions matching this node"
   select a) option.
9) For "Launch method" you will get 2 options:
    a) "Launch agent by connecting it to the controller"
    b) "Launch agents via SSH"
   select b) option.
10) Once you select b) option, It will ask for host, copy the public IP of the slave instance and paste it. For 
     credentials click on Add and then select Jenkins.
11) Once after selecting this it will ask for "Kind". select "SSH username with private key".
12) For ID give any number (like: 01, 1 etc .....), Give a proper description, Give the username (To get the username go 
     to your slave instance and type the command "whoami". You will get your username). For "Private Key" select the
     option, "Enter directly" and then click on "Add". Copy the private key of the slave instance (RSA/ED25519 
     algorithm) which you have downloaded from AWS and paste it here.
13) After this scroll down and click on Add. Credentials are added. Select the credentials which you have created.
14) For "Host Key Verification Strategy" select "Non verifying verification strategy".
15) Scroll down till the end and then click on save. Similarly you can create multiple slaves.


16) After adding slave, go to Jenkins dashboard and click on "New Item" give a project name. Select any type of 
     project like "Freestyle project", "Maven project" or "Pipeline" and then click on OK. If you have choosen "Freestyle 
     project" then give any description, scroll down little bit you will get an option "Restrict where this project can be 
     run" select that and under "Label Expression" you can either give the slave name or label name. 
17) Under "Source Code Management" select Git and then paste the spring-petclinic URL, for "Credentials" select the
     credential which you have created.
18) Under Branches to build -----> Branch Specifier (blank for 'any') write the branch name based on your GitHub repo.
19) Scroll down till the end and then under "Build Steps" select "Execute shell" and then write the command:


20) Finally click on Apply and Save and then click on Build now. Now if you observe, the job is done by the slave 
     machine and if you want to see the .jar file, then go to the slave instance and then go to this path: ^zPfPSJVj

Virtualization ^39ieCRtJ

Virtualization is the technology that you can use to create virtual representations of servers, 
storage, Networking equipments and other physical machines. Virtual software mimics the functions of
a physical hardware to run multiple virtual machines simultaneously on a physical machine. ^qVDQIXef

We mainly have 2 components: ^JMSyQuT3

1) Virtual machine
2) Hypervisor ^t2er8iYE

Virtual machine: ^DVtcqVrg

It is a software defined computer that runs on a physical computer with a seperate OS and a 
computing resources. The physical computer is called as host machine and virtual machines are guest 
machines. We can run multiple VMs on a physical machine. VMs are abstracted from the computer 
hardware by hypervisor. ^9WJ675nK

Hypervisor: ^cKpE5XFe

The hypervisor is a software component that manages multiple VMs in a computer. It ensures that each 
VMs gets the allocated resources and does not interfere with the operations of other virtual machines.
There are 2 types of Hypervisors: ^XiPf84gL

a) Type 1 hypervisor (Bare metal hypervisor): ^Nd9PaJtN

Hardware ^b3df9fhC

The hypervisor runs directly on a physical hardware without the host OS. It manages the hardware
and VMs directly. Example: VMware ESXI, KVM (Linux), Citrix xen server. ^ChT5SPFi

b) Type 2 hypervisor (Hosted hypervisor): ^swSl5ifk

The hypervisor runs on top of an existing OS, like a regular application and uses the host OS
to manage the hardware access. Example: Oracle virtual box, VMware workstation, Parallels Desktop 
(MacOs). ^Gz9y6QG5

one data center fails, another can continue the service. ^6EA3dKfl

AWS has regions, availability zones, edge locations, regional edge cache worldwide.
This ensures low latency, high availability and disaster recovery. ^cS0ZZNB8

What is CDN (Content delivery network) ?

> A Content Delivery Network (CDN) is a geographically distributed network of servers designed to deliver
  web content to users faster and more efficiently. It does this by caching, or temporarily storing, copies 
  of your website's static and dynamic content at multiple locations around the world. 
  These locations are called "points of presence" (PoPs) or "edge locations."

  How it Works in AWS: Amazon CloudFront

> Amazon CloudFront is the CDN service offered by Amazon Web Services. It's a key component for delivering
  content with low latency and high data transfer speeds. CloudFront integrates seamlessly with other AWS 
  services like Amazon S3 (for static content storage) and Amazon EC2 (for dynamic content).

1) A user requests content from your website, which is configured to use CloudFront.

2) The DNS routes the request to the nearest CloudFront edge location.

3) The edge location checks its cache for the requested content.
   > Cache Hit: If the content is in the cache, it's delivered to the user immediately.
   > Cache Miss: If not, the edge location checks the regional edge cache.

4) The regional edge cache checks its cache.
    > Cache Hit: If the content is found, it's served to the edge location, which then serves it to the user
      and caches a copy.
    > Cache Miss: If not, the regional edge cache forwards the request to the origin server.

5) The origin server retrieves the content and sends it to the regional edge cache, which then sends it to
    the edge location.

6) The edge location serves the content to the user and caches it locally for future requests. ^7XhZSk2y

1) User Request: A user sends a request for a file (like an image or a video) that has never been requested 
   before. The request is routed to the nearest edge location based on latency.

2) Edge Location Cache Miss: The edge location checks its cache, finds nothing, and determines it's a cache miss.

3) Request to Regional Edge Cache: The edge location forwards the request to its corresponding regional edge cache.

4) Regional Edge Cache Miss: The regional edge cache also checks its larger cache and finds nothing, as this is the 
   very first request for the content. It's a cache miss at this level too.

5) Request to Origin: The regional edge cache forwards the request to your origin server 
    (e.g., an Amazon S3 bucket, an EC2 instance, or an external web server).

6) Origin Response and Caching: The origin server retrieves the file and sends it back. This response travels 
   back through the CloudFront network.
   > The regional edge cache receives the file from the origin and caches a copy of it.
   > The regional edge cache then sends the file to the requesting edge location, which also caches a copy.

7) Final Delivery to User: The edge location delivers the file to the user. ^xz5MeZPB

Workflow of Regional edge cache: ^8vKfMJNv

Youtube is 
cached to REC ^PkrzBFf2

5) Local zones: ^QAsM19rA

> A local zone is an extension of AWS region where you can run your latency sensitive application. It is designed to bring a broader range of 
  AWS services to a specific city or metro area.

> AWS Local Zones are a type of AWS infrastructure deployment that place compute, storage, database, and other select
  services closer to large population, industry, and IT centers, enabling you to deliver applications that require single-digit 
  millisecond latency to end users.

> You can use AWS Local Zones to run various AWS services such as Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2), Amazon 
  Virtual Private Cloud (Amazon VPC), Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS), Amazon FSx, Application Load Balancer (ALB), 
  Amazon EMR, Amazon ElastiCache, and Amazon Relational Database Service (Amazon RDS) in geographic proximity to your end 
  users, with more services to be added in the future. ^SyyiJ1oW

How They Work:

1) Extension of a Region: A Local Zone is not a full-fledged AWS Region; it's a logical extension of an existing one. 
   For example, a Local Zone in Los Angeles is an extension of the US West (Oregon) Region. This architecture allows you to
   use the same APIs, tools, and management consoles you're familiar with in the parent region to deploy and manage 
   resources in the Local Zone.

2) VPC Subnet: To use a Local Zone, you first enable it in your account. Then, you can extend your existing Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)
    into the Local Zone by creating a new subnet and assigning it to that specific Local Zone.

3) Local Resources: You can now launch a select set of AWS services within this new subnet, such as Amazon EC2 instances or Amazon 
   EBS volumes. These resources are physically located in the Local Zone's data center.

4) Low-Latency Connectivity: Resources in the Local Zone communicate with each other with very low latency. They also have a dedicated, 
   high-bandwidth connection to the parent AWS Region, which gives them seamless access to the full range of AWS services not available
   in the Local Zone itself. This is crucial for hybrid architectures where some parts of the application need to run locally, while 
   others reside in the main region.

5) Direct Internet Connection: Local Zones have their own direct internet connections. This means that traffic from a user to an application
    running in a Local Zone doesn't need to "hairpin" back to the parent region, further reducing latency. ^EJ2v5QbR

Difference between Local zones and Edge location

> Edge Locations and Regional Edge Caches are part of Amazon CloudFront, a Content Delivery Network (CDN). Their primary purpose is 
   to cache static and dynamic content (like images, videos, and HTML files) and deliver it to users quickly. They don't run a full suite of 
   AWS services; they're just caching points. Think of them as delivery depots for pre-packaged goods.

> Local Zones are an extension of an AWS Region. They contain actual compute, storage, and database services like Amazon EC2, Amazon
  EBS, and Amazon ECS. This allows you to deploy and run your entire application closer to the end-users. Think of them as small, regional 
  data centers. ^liqJ0nKZ

6) Wavelength zones ^EV2pG0KA

> Wavelength Zones are a specialized type of AWS infrastructure used to provide ultra-low latency for applications serving mobile users
  on a 5G network. They bring a subset of AWS compute and storage services directly into a mobile carrier's data center at the edge of 
  the 5G network. ^rz43SE68

How it works ?

1) User Request: A user on a 5G-enabled mobile device connected to a specific mobile carrier's network makes a request to your application.
   This could be anything from a multiplayer game action to a real-time IoT command.

2) Carrier Network: The request travels directly through the mobile carrier's 5G network. Instead of going out to a public internet gateway, 
    the network recognizes that the destination is within a Wavelength Zone.

3) Carrier Gateway: The request hits the Carrier Gateway, a networking component within the Wavelength Zone. The Carrier Gateway serves 
    as the entry point for traffic from the mobile carrier's network.

4) Application Processing: The request is routed to the AWS resources (e.g., an EC2 instance) you have deployed in the Wavelength Zone's 
    subnet. Because the compute resources are physically located at the edge of the 5G network, the time it takes for the request to travel
    from the user's device to the application server is minimal, often in the single-digit millisecond range.

5) Response to User: The application processes the request and sends the response back to the user's device. This response also travels 
    through the Carrier Gateway and back over the 5G network, maintaining the low latency.  ^SUJgWMjJ

Difference between local zones, wavelength zones, REC and EL ^CeZ7tCxG

> Target Audience: Wavelength Zones are specifically for applications that need to be within single-digit milliseconds of mobile devices on a 
   specific carrier's 5G network. Local Zones, on the other hand, are for general-purpose applications that need low latency for a broader 
   metropolitan area, regardless of the user's connection type (fiber, Wi-Fi, etc.).

> Purpose: While Edge Locations and Regional Edge Caches are for caching content, and Local Zones are for running a subset of 
  general-purpose services, Wavelength Zones are about running services that are highly dependent on the speed and low latency of a 
  5G network.

> Network Path: The most significant difference is the network path. With Wavelength, traffic from a 5G device is routed to the application
   running in the Wavelength Zone without ever leaving the mobile carrier's private network. This direct path is what provides the ultra-low 
   latency. Requests to Local Zones and CDNs, while optimized, still traverse the public internet to some degree. ^M6JTXbEJ

> Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) is a core service of AWS that provides resizable compute capacity in the cloud. 
> Essentially, it lets you rent virtual servers, called instances, on which you can run applications. 
> This service eliminates the need for you to buy and maintain your own physical servers. ^TPHnbdmI

Why is EC2 Used? ^GpqY3S4k

> Elasticity and Scalability: EC2 allows you to easily scale your computing resources up or down to meet changing demands.
  For example, a retail website can automatically launch more instances during a sale and then scale back down afterward, saving money.

> Cost-Effectiveness: You convert a large fixed cost (buying servers) into a small variable cost (paying for what you use). You can
   choose from various pricing models to optimize costs, such as On-Demand Instances (pay-as-you-go), Reserved Instances 
   (long-term commitment for a discount), or Spot Instances (bid for unused capacity at a steep discount).

> Flexibility and Control: You have complete control over your instances, including the operating system, storage, and software 
  configuration. This allows you to tailor the environment to your specific application needs.

> Reliability and Security: AWS provides a highly reliable infrastructure with a global network of data centers and built-in redundancy.
   EC2 also integrates with other AWS services like VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) and Security Groups to ensure robust network security. ^TgskBSAt

Components of EC2: ^V7SUUWjT

> These are the virtual servers themselves. You select an instance type based on your workload's requirements for CPU, 
   memory, storage, and networking.
> With the help of instance you can create a Virtual machine. ^R7rhSjkY

  lists), Elastic IP addresses (EIPs), Internet gateway). ^nY7ehqhi

4. Amazon machine image (AMI):   ^Ql03yvIN

> It is used to provide exact copy of an instance. 
> It contains the operating system and any pre-installed software, allowing you to quickly launch instances with a consistent configuration.
   You can use AMIs provided by AWS, the community, or create your own custom AMIs. ^OABrv3J3

5. Elastic load balancing (ELB): ^0FN28LJM

> A service that automatically distributes incoming application traffic across multiple EC2 instances to improve availability and fault tolerance.
> It is used to balance the load on multiple instances. ^LqJZ6vMH

6. Auto scaling groups (ASG): ^MHFtTM79

> Automatically adjusts the number of EC2 instances in your application in response to changing demand, ensuring you have the right
  amount of compute capacity available at all times.
> It is used to scale up or down the instances automatically. ^4GbeYuYY

There are four primary types of AMIs, each with different characteristics and use cases:

1) Quick Start AMIs (or Amazon-provided AMIs):
 
   > These are official, pre-configured AMIs created and maintained by AWS. They provide a quick and easy way to launch a basic instance 
      with a common operating system, like Amazon Linux, Ubuntu, or Windows Server. They are considered trustworthy, secure, and are regularly
      updated by AWS.

   > Who uses it: New users or anyone who needs to quickly launch a standard EC2 instance with a common OS, like Amazon Linux 2, 
     Ubuntu Server, or Windows Server.

   > Example: You want to launch a new web server. You select the "Amazon Linux 2023 AMI" from the Quick Start list. This gives you a clean, 
      secure OS ready for you to install your application code.

2) My AMIs: 
   
   > These are custom AMIs that you create yourself from an existing EC2 instance. After you configure an instance with the specific
      software, applications, and settings your workload needs, you can save it as an AMI. This is useful for creating a standardized environment
      that you can quickly replicate across multiple instances for consistency and efficiency.

   >  Who uses it: DevOps engineers or teams who need to create a standardized, repeatable environment. For example, to launch multiple identical 
      application servers.

   > Example: You have an EC2 instance with a web server (like Apache) and your application code already installed and configured. You create an 
     AMI from this instance. The next time you need to launch a new web server, you can use your custom AMI to get an identical server with 
     everything ready to go.  

3) AWS Marketplace AMIs: 

   > These are AMIs created by third-party software vendors and available for purchase in the AWS Marketplace.
     They often come pre-installed with licensed software, such as databases, security tools, or developer environments. You are billed for the use
     of the software directly through your AWS account, simplifying licensing and deployment. These are vetted by AWS for security and functionality.

   > Who uses it: Businesses who want to deploy commercial software quickly without having to manually install and license it.

   > Example: You need to set up a firewall for your VPC. Instead of manually installing and configuring the software, you can search the AWS
     Marketplace for an "F5 BIG-IP" or a "Palo Alto Networks Next-Generation Firewall" AMI and launch an instance with the software already
     pre-installed and ready to configure. 


4) Community AMIs: 

   > These are publicly shared AMIs created by other AWS users. They are often free to use and can be a great resource for
      specialized configurations. However, they are not vetted by AWS, so it's important to be cautious and only use AMIs from a trusted source to 
      avoid security risks. You can't be sure what a community AMI contains without checking it yourself.

   > Who uses it: Users looking for a very specific or niche software configuration that isn't available elsewhere.

   > Example: A developer has created a custom AMI with a specific version of a programming language and a set of libraries for a niche project and
     has made it public. Another developer with the same project can use this AMI instead of building the environment from scratch. ^CqM2U3yA

AWS EC2 instance types: ^57IXHX8m

> AWS EC2 offers a wide selection of instance types, each optimized for different use cases and workloads. Choosing the right instance type is crucial
   for balancing performance and cost. These instances are grouped into families based on their core capabilities.

Understanding the Naming Convention:

> Every EC2 instance type has a name that follows a clear pattern: family.generation.processor (optional).size. For example, in m8g.large:

> m: The family, which indicates the instance's purpose (e.g., General Purpose).

> 8: The generation, where a higher number means newer hardware with better performance and often a lower price per unit of performance.

> g: An optional additional capability, such as the processor type (e.g., g for Graviton, i for Intel, a for AMD, d for local instance storage).

> .large: The size, which determines the amount of CPU, memory, and storage. The sizes range from nano to 24xlarge and beyond, with each step 
   typically doubling the resources of the previous one. ^xoyew46K

> Metal (.metal): This is a unique instance size that provides direct access to the underlying
  hardware. It's not a virtual machine. A metal instance gives you full control over the host 
  server, which is useful for workloads that require bare-metal access for licensing or performance 
  reasons. For example, running an application that needs to interact directly with the hardware or 
  migrating existing on-premise workloads that are not virtualized. ^pPXijMIx

                                           AWS groups instances into six main families, each tailored for a 
specific type of workload.


> These instances offer a balanced mix of compute, memory, and networking resources. They're a 
   good choice for a wide range of applications that don't have a specific resource bottleneck.

         M-series, T-series


> T-series (t): Ideal for general-purpose workloads that are not consistently busy, such as small
  databases, web servers, development environments, and test servers.

> M-series (m): Best for applications that require a steady, consistent balance of resources, 
  such as application servers, mid-sized databases, and caching fleets. ^Kl3IlVK1
