> Amazon CloudFront is a fast and secure Content Delivery Network (CDN) service provided by Amazon Web Services (AWS).
> It is designed to deliver web content—such as images, videos, and APIs—with low latency and high transfer speeds to users worldwide. ^f2du3zjJ

Cloudfront ^Ao3M0riQ

> Faster Content Delivery: It caches content at edge locations worldwide, so users get data from the nearest server instead of waiting
                                 for a response from the origin.
> Scalability: Handles sudden traffic spikes without you having to manage servers.
> Cost Optimization: Reduces the load on your origin servers by serving cached data, which can save bandwidth and compute costs.
> Global Reach: Useful if your users are spread across different countries—everyone gets faster access to your content. ^xTrS3OJm

Why We Use CloudFront ^pbgQOaQn

Example: ^zyoPmqnl

Imagine you have a website hosted in Mumbai region. A user from New York visits it.
> Without CloudFront: request travels all the way to Mumbai region which results in higher latency.
> With CloudFront: CloudFront serves cached content from the nearest edge location (e.g., New York) hence decreases latency. ^KPvfUXeA

S3 Bucket ^nzRRuqET

Static web hosting ^1OaWWDN6

Object ^2DxfzH8d

Permission: ^KJ2pd2nj

GetObject ^dq3fqogL

> url from browser
> End users access the bucket ^outfacVd

Cloudfront ^IQsakJus

It will provide a
default domain name
or url ^uRxdRuPH

Users ^Mu7a1AUX

1) Create a S3 bucket. While creating, under "Block Public Access settings for this bucket" 
   option, tick Block all public access option and then create bucket.
2) Upload the files and folders related to static website and follow the steps of static web
    hosting.
3) In a new tab open AWS cloudfront and click on "Create a CloudFront distribution" button
4) Give the distribution name and description and under Distribution type select single website or app and click on next.
5) Under origin type, select Amazon S3 and click on browse S3 to choose the bucket which you have created. Then scroll down
    towards the end and click on next.
6) Under Web Application Firewall (WAF) select "Do not enable security protections" and click on next.
7) Scroll down and click on create distribution.
8) A new page is loaded and in this page we have an option called as origins. Click on origins, select the origin and click on edit.
9) If you scroll down you will get "You must allow access to CloudFront using this policy statement. Learn more about giving CloudFront 
    permission to access the S3 bucket" policy message. Copy that policy and go to S3 bucket permissions select bucket policy click on edit and 
    paste the policy and then click on save changes.
10) Come back to CloudFront, Under General you have settings click on edit. If you scroll down you will get "Default root object - optional" 
    option, write index.html and click on save changes.
11) Under General, you have "Distribution domain name" copy that domain name and paste it in the search bar of the browser. ^yrJb1O1J

Static web hosting using S3 and cloudfront ^maHQh7iI

> A CloudFront distribution is the core configuration that connects your content to the Amazon CloudFront global network. 
> It's the set of instructions that tells CloudFront where to find your original content, what to do with it, and how to deliver it to your users.
> Think of it as a blueprint for your content's journey across the internet. 
> When you create a distribution, you're essentially setting up a new content delivery service with a unique name (a CloudFront domain name, 
  like d1234.cloudfront.net). You then point your website or application to this new domain name. ^22Rw25Ui

Cloudfront distribution ^gSKMNCrG

Feature                      Amazon DynamoDB               Amazon RDS

Database Type          NoSQL (Non-Relational)          Relational (SQL)

Data Model           Key-Value and Document store.    Fixed tables with rows and columns.

Schema                Schema-less/Flexible. Items can    


                         have different attributes.         
Querying              Primarily through primary key 
                        lookups or secondary indexes.  


                        Not designed for complex joins.        
Scalability            Horizontal (scale-out) by 
                        automatically partitioning data 
                        across many servers. Near-limitless 
                        scale.         ^TriHhpVy

Fixed Schema. Requires predefined 
columns and data types. ^4vaZGx6U

Uses SQL for complex queries, joins, and transactions across multiple tables. ^BdT1PIVZ

Primarily Vertical (scale-up) by upgrading 
instance size, and Read Replicas for read scaling ^8yFjmDBH

DynamoDB ^LOuvTcfb

> Amazon DynamoDB is a fully managed, serverless NoSQL (non-relational) database service offered by Amazon 
  Web Services (AWS). 
> It supports key-value and document data structures and is known for delivering single-digit millisecond performance at any scale. ^6YiIbZs7

> High performance at scale – handles millions of requests per second with very low latency.
> Scalability – automatically scales storage and throughput.
> Serverless database – you don’t manage hardware, patching, or scaling.
> Flexible schema – unlike RDS, you don’t need to define strict schemas. You can just store items with varying attributes.
> Global reach – supports Global Tables to replicate across AWS regions for low-latency worldwide access.
> Integration – works well with other AWS services (Lambda, API Gateway, Kinesis, etc.) for serverless applications. ^CIJqs1l0

Why is DynamoDB used: ^JWwQ0juO

> Gaming leaderboards
> IoT device data storage
> Shopping carts / session management
> Real-time bidding, chats, and messaging apps ^a6AUr7mf

Common use cases: ^7W2np7RX

Steps to create a database using DynamoDB: ^0CfqZFZd

1) Search for DynamoDB in the searchbar of AWS.
2) Click on create DynamoBD
3) Give a name for the table and under "Partition key" give a key name and select the data type.
4) Click on create table.
5) After the table is created, click on the table then click on "Explore table items"
6) Scroll down and click on create item
7) Give the value for the key and if you want more columns then click on Add new attribute.
8) Add as many attributes as you want and then click on create item.
9) Similarly if you want to add one more item then click on create item. ^xlo6G4PX

Steps to create VPC using VPC only option: ^L3AmjeM8

Steps to manage EC2 instance with the help of Lambda ^8CWjqPBm

1) Go to EC2 and create 2 instances and keep it in running state.
2) Search for IAM and select Roles option from the sidebar then click on create role button.
3) Under "Trusted entity type" section select AWS service and under "Use case" section, choose Lambda then click 
   on next.
4) Under "permission policies" section search for "AmazonEC2FullAccess" and select, search for "EC2InstanceConnect"
    and select then click on next.
5) Under "Role details" section give a Role name and scroll to the end and click on create role button. 
 

6) Search for lambda from the search bar.
7) Click on create function button which is in the landing page. If not found then click functions option which is 
   present in the sidebar then click on create function.
8) You will be getting three options "Author from scratch", "Use a blueprint", "Container image" choose Author from
    scratch option.
9) Under "Basic Information" section give a function name, and select the runtime as python 3.13 and then scroll to
    the bottom and click on create function button.

10) After the 5th step, Function will be created. Scroll down and you will get a sections such as "Code", "Test", 
    "Monitor", "Configuration", "Aliases", "Versions" select Configuration option and click on edit.
11)  Give a description, then scroll down and you will find "Timeout" section increase the timeout to 15 seconds.
     then at the end under "Existing role" section select the role which you have created  and then click on save 
     button.  

12) Under "Code" section you will be having "Code source" section there you will be having default code, remove that 
    and paste any python script.
13) In the script if you have a line of code for region then replace the 'region' with the region id and if you have a
     line of code for instance-id, replace that with the instance id which you will be copying from the EC2 instance 
     which you have created (Go to EC2 ----> click on the instance which is running -----> under "details" section
     copy the instance id ).
14) Click on deploy button and then click on test button. Sometimes you will get an option near the searchbar of the
    terminal which says "Create new test event". Select that and give a name to the event and then save it. After
    that click on test button. In the output if you get "Status: Succeeded" then the whatever action you have 
    written in the script has been performed (Start, Stop, Reboot etc .......).
15) Try using different scripts and see the results. ^rf1zz2NW

Steps to get SNS notification to email with the help of Lambda ^IgCBvQt5

1) Search for lambda from the search bar.
2) Click on create function button which is in the landing page. If not found then click functions option which is 
   present in the sidebar then click on create function.
3) You will be getting three options "Author from scratch", "Use a blueprint", "Container image" choose Use a blueprint
    option.
4) Under blueprint name search for "canary" and select it. Then give a function name.
5) Scroll down and you will get "EventBridge (CloudWatch Events) Trigger" section, Under that there is a section
   called as "Rule", select "Create a new Rule" option.
6) Give a rule name and description and under Rule type select Schedule expression option and write rate(1 minute).
    then click on create function.

7) Once it is done, search for SNS in the searchbar and open it in the new tab.
8) In the sidebar you have an option called as "Topic" select that and click on Create Topic button.
9) Under details section, you have two options "FIFO" and "Standard" select Standard. Give a topic name and scroll
   towards the end and click on create topic.
10) Under Subscriptions section click on Create subscription button.
11) Under Details you have Protocol section, select Email as the option then under Endpoint write your email id and
     then click on create subscription.

12) Go back to Lambda and select the Canary function which you have created.
13) You will be having a button called as "+ Add destination" click on it.
14) Under Destination configuration you have Condition section, select On success option and then under Destination
     section you will be getting the Topic which you have created, select that and then click on save.
15) Check your email for the email notification (If not found search in spam). ^sZ7q6L7C

aws ec2 create-default-subnet --availability-zone eu-north-1a

aws ec2 create-default-subnet --availability-zone eu-north-1c ^NfOeW1d1

Here are all the commands to copy files from your EC2 instance to your local machine using scp.
These examples are written for Windows PowerShell or Command Prompt.

1. General syntax


2. Copy a single file from instance to local: This copies the file script_s3_sns_lambda.txt from the instance to your local folder D:\Batch notes\.
   The destination must be an existing folder on your PC.


3. Copy a whole folder: Use -r when copying directories. This will create a folder BatchNotes inside D:\.


4. Copy multiple files: You can copy multiple specific files by listing them. Copies both file1.txt and file2.txt to your local folder.


5. Copy multiple folders: Use -r for each folder.


6. Copy all files and all folders: To copy everything (all files and subfolders) from /home/ubuntu. This copies all contents (but not /home/ubuntu itself) into your
    local folder D:\EC2_Backup\. ^X4tbSZWX

SCP stands for Secure Copy Protocol: ^7unRqUn0

Why is SCP used: ^tzzxlVzM

> It’s a command-line utility used to transfer files and folders securely between two systems over a network.
> SCP is built on top of SSH (Secure Shell), which means:
> It uses encryption to protect data during transfer.
> It uses SSH authentication (like .pem key files or passwords). ^Vnyfs4iI

> SCP is used when you need to copy files between your local computer and a remote server (like an EC2 instance) in a secure way.
> It’s especially useful when working with Linux servers, AWS EC2 instances, or remote machines where you connect using SSH. ^4rwAAsGl

Common Use Cases: ^wDAgQGsR

1) Copying from local to remote instance: ^nWmZ8xfz

Upload scripts, configuration files, or project folders to your EC2 instance. ^zCm73Y8Q

Here are all the commands to copy files from your local machine to your EC2 instance using scp.
These examples are written for Windows PowerShell or Command Prompt.

1. General syntax: This copies data from your local machine to the remote instance.


2. Copy a single file: Copies the file script_s3_sns_lambda.txt to /home/ubuntu on the instance.


3. Copy a whole folder: Copies the entire folder Batch notes (with all its files and subfolders) to /home/ubuntu.


4. Copy multiple files: Copies file1.txt and file2.txt from your local folder to the instance.


5. Copy multiple folders: Copies both folders Batch notes and Project data to /home/ubuntu.


6. Copy all files and all folders: Copies everything inside the Batch notes folder (all files and subfolders) to /home/ubuntu, but not the folder itself. ^Rb0f2LRE

scp -i <key.pem> <local_path> [user]@[instance_ip]:<remote_path> ^59BqX8Bp

scp -i INS-1-KEY.pem "D:\Batch notes\script_s3_sns_lambda.txt" ubuntu@98.130.57.251:/home/ubuntu ^W9AuS0WN

scp -i INS-1-KEY.pem -r "D:\Batch notes" ubuntu@98.130.57.251:/home/ubuntu ^mIYUKC60

scp -i INS-1-KEY.pem "D:\Batch notes\file1.txt" "D:\Batch notes\file2.txt" ubuntu@98.130.57.251:/home/ubuntu ^Bddfmcux

scp -i INS-1-KEY.pem -r "D:\Batch notes" -r "D:\Project data" ubuntu@98.130.57.251:/home/ubuntu ^96YL34m9

scp -i INS-1-KEY.pem -r "D:\Batch notes\*" ubuntu@98.130.57.251:/home/ubuntu ^gpi9cbKN

2) Copying from remote instance to local: ^Nx9Gm85N

Download logs, output files, or backups from the instance to your computer. ^jazl5fan

scp -i <key.pem> [user]@[instance_ip]:<remote_path> <local_path> ^066rgb0a

scp -i INS-1-KEY.pem ubuntu@98.130.57.251:/home/ubuntu/script_s3_sns_lambda.txt "D:\Batch notes\" ^djm9qKSE

scp -i INS-1-KEY.pem -r ubuntu@98.130.57.251:/home/ubuntu/BatchNotes "D:\" ^JHedIrYP

scp -i INS-1-KEY.pem ubuntu@98.130.57.251:/home/ubuntu/file1.txt ubuntu@98.130.57.251:/home/ubuntu/file2.txt "D:\Batch notes\" ^6Zvf1mo1

scp -i INS-1-KEY.pem -r ubuntu@98.130.57.251:/home/ubuntu/BatchNotes -r ubuntu@98.130.57.251:/home/ubuntu/ProjectData "D:\" ^VtCoLUU4

scp -i INS-1-KEY.pem -r ubuntu@98.130.57.251:/home/ubuntu/* "D:\EC2_Backup\" ^zjZYv6af

3) Copying between two remote servers: ^SVDOUlOM

Transfer files directly from one remote system to another (if you have SSH access to both). ^mPMe9L0b

NOTE: Connect to your instance using "SSH client" option then write the below mentioned commands ^or3o2ec9

> Extract the public key from your .pem file (which contains the private key).
> Run this command on your local terminal (Linux/Mac/WSL):
> This creates a file called your-key.pub containing the public key.
> Go to AWS Console → EC2 → Key Pairs
> In the left menu, select “Key Pairs” under Network & Security.
> Click “Import key pair”.
> Import Your Public Key
> Give it a name (for example, my-imported-key).
> Open your your-key.pub file in a text editor.
> Copy its contents (it starts with something like ssh-rsa AAAAB3NzaC1yc2EAAAADAQABAAABAQ...).
> Paste it in the Public key contents box.
> Click Import key pair.
> Now your key (my-imported-key) will appear in the Key pair list when creating a new EC2 instance.
> You can use your existing .pem private key file to connect via SSH. ^BV1LUXPS

Steps to import the key from local machine to AWS: ^w2RnRUVC

ssh-keygen -y -f your-key.pem > your-key.pub ^yc7eo4gq

Ansible ^U1XEpCWs

Ansible is an open-source IT automation tool used for:
> Configuration management
> Application deployment
> Task automation
> Orchestration ^lRBVW2ru

What is Ansible? ^6lmtx2CD

Problems before Ansible ^v5XPETyN

Before tools like Ansible existed, infrastructure and configuration management were mostly manual or 
script-based, which caused several issues: ^O5OsAt4P

a) Manual Server Configuration: ^5QNElPnX

Admins used to:
> SSH into each server
> Run commands manually
> Install software, edit config files

Real-life Example (E-commerce Company):
> Imagine a company like a mid-scale e-commerce platform:
> They have 20 servers initially
> DevOps engineer logs into each server:
-> sudo apt install nginx
-> Edit /etc/nginx/nginx.conf
-> Restart service

> Now suddenly:
-> Traffic increases (festival sale)
-> They scale to 200 servers

> Problem:
-> Engineer must repeat same steps 200 times
-> One typo in a command → one server behaves differently
-> Takes hours or days ^PnrIIcw3

b) Lack of Consistency: ^Iluutb8u

Even if admins try to configure servers the same way:
> Minor differences creep in

Real-life Example (Banking Application)
> A banking system runs on multiple servers:
-> Server A → Java version 11
-> Server B → Java version 17
-> Server C → Missing environment variable

Now:
> Application works perfectly on Server A
> Fails on Server B ^oH0B4LJK

c) Scaling Issues: ^xzPTSm2T

Manual or script-based approaches don’t scale well.

Real-life Example (Streaming Platform)
> Think of a video streaming platform:
-> Initially: 10 servers → manageable manually
-> After growth: 1000+ servers globally

Now:
> Updating a config file on all servers:
-> Manual → impossible
-> Script → risky ^jbUWmCz7

d) Poor Version Control: ^07HswBtk

> Configurations were not stored properly in version control systems
> Changes were not tracked

Real-life Example (Production Outage)
> A DevOps engineer:
> Edits config file directly on server
> Changes port from 8080 → 9090

Next day:
> Application stops working

Problem:
> No record of change
> No backup of previous config ^UKT80fAF

Before Ansible, several tools were used for automation and configuration management: ^SYNghzT9

Tools Used Before Ansible: ^uLqR0pmM

1) Shell Scripting (Bash): ^SzErzJCv

> Shell scripting (mainly Bash) is used to automate tasks by writing command sequences.
> It was used for
-> Installing packages
-> Starting/stopping services
-> File manipulation
-> Basic deployments ^GMlCb2rF

Drawbacks of Shell Scripting: ^97ASffR4

> Running script multiple times can break things
> Real Scenario
-> First run → installs nginx
-> Second run → tries to install again → may fail or behave unexpectedly ^3I4LglUR

1. Not Idempotent: ^G89X8vXT

> Script grows to 500+ lines
> Multiple conditions, loops, dependencies
> Debugging becomes difficult
> New engineer cannot understand easily ^mPPKVPDm

2. Hard to Maintain for Large Systems: ^nrMg1UbE

> Script must be copied to 100+ servers
> Need tools like SSH loops ^67VOxs52

3. Not Scalable: ^EQ1Kj0Mi

2) Python Scripts: ^ABdKm5L7

> It was used to automate infrastructure tasks.
> It was used for:
-> Complex automation
-> API integrations
-> Dynamic workflows ^2qOLdPHU

Drawbacks of Python Scripting: ^0AX5z021

> Not beginner-friendly.
> DevOps engineers must know Python deeply. ^AdWkm99B

1. Requires Programming Knowledge: ^QdNTGfEs

> Add conditions, retries, logging, error handling
> Script becomes 1000+ lines ^DppagcQZ

2. Scripts Become Complex Quickly: ^VxWZVKR2

3) Configuration Management Tools: ^TcVEQPSC

Before Ansible, tools like:
> Puppet
> Chef
> CFEngine ^ZKwlmMxg

Drawbacks of Configuration Management Tools: ^NEqbou85

> Each tool uses its own language:
-> Puppet → DSL
-> Chef → Ruby
-> CFEngine → complex policy syntax
> Engineers must learn new syntax instead of using simple formats ^7KXJLEir

1. Steep Learning Curve: ^JuuVcibx

> Requires installing agents/clients on every server
> Problems
-> Maintenance overhead (install, update, monitor agents)
-> Security concerns (agents running continuously)
-> Version compatibility issues ^6HeQob79

2. Agent-Based Architecture: ^Day6PIMH

Ansible VS Terraform: ^2ify7qlq

> Ansible is a configuration management and automation tool.
> It Works inside already created servers
> Manages:
-> Software installation
-> Configuration files
-> Services
> Example
-> Install Nginx
-> Configure /etc/nginx/nginx.conf
-> Start/stop services ^QS7ZsTid

Ansible: ^FJN1ZW1W

> Terraform is an Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tool.
> It Works outside servers
-> Creates and manages infrastructure:
-> Virtual machines
-> Networks
-> Load balancers
-> Cloud resources
> Example
-> Create AWS EC2 instance
-> Create VPC, subnet
-> Setup security groups ^cFLZ3gbE

Terraform: ^mggxKicK

What is Ansible? ^X1BEiBwW

> Ansible is an open-source IT automation tool used for:
-> Configuration Management
-> Application Deployment
-> Task Automation
-> Orchestration
> Ansible is a tool that allows you to automate IT tasks and manage
   multiple servers from a single system. ^I7LD5AfS

> Uses SSH to connect to remote machines
> No need to install agents (No need to install ansible in multiple machines)
> Executes tasks using Playbooks (YAML files) ^xuf1d0G6

How Ansible Works (Basic Idea): ^u4k9FKUy

History of Ansible: ^cmZjbIln

> in the year 2012 Michael DeHaan created Ansible.
> He wanted to create a simple, agentless, easy-to-use automation tool
> In the year 2015 Ansible was acquired by Red Hat. Later it became part 
  of Red Hat Ansible Automation Platform ^Gka1ojcV

Features of Ansible: ^BBVcMQNM

1. Agentless Architecture: ^JT90sDnN

Ansible operates without requiring any agents on managed 
nodes. It uses standard communication protocols like SSH 
for Linux systems and WinRM for Windows systems to execute
tasks remotely. This simplifies setup, reduces maintenance 
overhead, and eliminates issues related to agent installation and updates. ^gJTEjJNk

2. Simple YAML Syntax (Playbooks): ^fUl5DFjI

Ansible uses YAML (Yet Another Markup Language) to write playbooks,
which describe the desired state of systems. The syntax is simple 
and easy to understand, even for non-programmers. This makes 
automation accessible to both developers and operations teams, 
reducing the need for deep programming knowledge. ^udYECiBb

3. Idempotency: ^nlnQgTjv

In Ansible, tasks are designed to ensure that the desired state is achieved without 
causing unintended changes if executed repeatedly. For example, if a package like Nginx 
is already installed, Ansible will skip the installation step instead of reinstalling it. 
This ensures safe, reliable, and predictable automation, especially in repeated deployments. ^G6nwCP55

4. Push-Based Mechanism: ^24dzwzSQ

In Ansible, the control node pushes playbooks to 
target servers over SSH. Unlike tools like Puppet or 
Chef, there is no need for agents to continuously 
poll the master for updates. This results in faster 
execution, reduced network overhead, and simpler architecture. ^CatUn7nb

5. Inventory Management: ^PS6FGgWe

Ansible uses an inventory file to store server details and
group them logically (e.g., webservers, databases). This 
allows administrators to target specific groups of machines
easily, enabling efficient management of large infrastructures. ^04UvPZ1y

6. Modules: ^StNYAJjD

Ansible provides a wide range of modules for tasks like package installation, 
file management, and user creation. These modules eliminate the need for 
writing custom scripts, making automation faster, consistent, and less error-prone. ^FyL56OJ1

Drawbacks: ^8WWTO0eN

> As the modifications were done in the same file, so any changes made at any
  particular instance, tracking them was difficult.
> There was no proper collaboration between developers. ^lzbKTZAU

One developer must be able to access or modify
another developers source code. ^QAOWg0yC

> Integrating the source code was a problem.
> If there was any damage to the machine, it would lead to loss of source code.
> It might lead to lots of rework and hence there will be delay in delivering the software. ^lXulQcKT

In order to overcome the drawbacks we started using a system called as
Version Control System (VCS) ^tLqX8a32

Version control system (VCS) ^VSUQtT8u

> Used to manage/track the versions of the source code. ^o1Dbg0qV

Anytime we can go to any of the versions and if needed we can
do modifications as well. ^0ceB4K9j

1) Manage: ^q6hBBM0R

who: Who made the changes?
when: When the changes were made?
what: What changes/modifications are? ^5hipPHqr

2) Track: ^JlVbIIXm

Each time we make any modifications/changes in the source code
it is treated as version. ^KicWbNPI

3) Versions: ^AZy4BP9Y

Types of VCS ^e1szknUB

Saving the versions of the source code seperately on the local
machine. ^FeCNBQdg

1) Local/Localized VCS: ^Foflrmov

Drawbacks: ^Jaaac9eb

> No Collaboration between developers.
> Physical damage to the machine can lead to the loss of source code.
> If there is any damage to the local machine then, developer has to start
  from scratch which is too much time consuming. ^oT9xZHPZ

> Saving the versions of the source code separately on the 
  centralized machine.
> Since all the developers have to upload the versions of the files
  in the same repository, hence there is a collaboration between all
  of them.
> Example: Subversion (SVN), Perforce ^brsFYsWI

2) Central/Centralized VCS: ^qpTvAxD0

> Distributed VCS can be defined as the combination of local VCS as well as central
  VCS.
> Here we will be saving the versions locally as well as remotely in the remote server
  over the internet.
> Example: GIT, Mercurial, BitKeeper ^I5J9NUZf

History of GIT ^Wqf3audN

> Linus Torvalds was using a tool called as "BitKeeper". It was used to manage and track the
  source code.
> The problem was that BitKeeper was not free and open source.
> In the year 2005, he introduced GIT which was totally free to use and was an open source
  software. ^j6RecqDC

Features of Git ^UxfaMHdJ

> Free and open source.
> Used to track the changes in the source code.
> It supports collaboration.
> Certain operations such as branching, merging are way more faster in comparision to other
  version control tools. ^siWlX2P8

Terraform ^nhYF4kEa

Introduction ^W85ANKwf

What is IaC (Infrastructure as a code)? ^GiP6KYWx

> Infrastructure as Code (IaC) is a way of managing and provisioning infrastructure using code 
  instead of manual processes.
> Instead of manually creating servers, networks, databases, etc., you write code 
  (scripts/config files) that automatically sets up everything.
> In IaC, infrastructure is defined using code (such as YAML, JSON, or domain-specific languages).
> Tools like Terraform or AWS CloudFormation automatically create and manage resources based 
  on this code.
> Infrastructure can be version-controlled, reused, and consistently deployed across environments.
> IaC reduces manual effort, minimizes errors, and ensures reliable and scalable infrastructure. ^URBWTcsm

Example: ^hcI3iFRN

1. Vegetables
2. Rice
3. Masala
4. Salt ^6kr96CjR

Recipe Book ^Itq9lfVl

By referring to this you can cook
any number of times. You don't have
to consult the main chef every time. ^Tck9Sbgr

Cook ^W8ECZWzN

1. Server
2. Storage
3. Networking
4. Database ^XusUMChR

YAML/JASON ^NyN0WgLl

Similarly, By writing a configuration file,
You can create any infrastructure. No
need of creating everything manually. ^CVdJ7srL

Infrastructure
created ^jcvTUMng

Infrastructure
file ^SN9Dw5u8

Why do we need IaC? ^aTgSzOGa

1. Automation of Infrastructure: ^jZoS9Tob

IaC eliminates manual configuration by allowing you to 
automatically create and manage resources such as servers, 
networks, and databases using code. This reduces time and 
effort required for setup. ^wu5ABNFR

2. Consistency and Standardization: ^lQnd2R8K

When infrastructure is defined in code, the same 
configuration can be reused multiple times. This ensures 
that environments (development, testing, production) are 
identical, avoiding inconsistencies and configuration drift. ^PCcZTcZw

3. Faster Deployment: ^1Ij94S7B

With IaC, infrastructure can be provisioned in minutes instead of hours or 
days. This speeds up application development and deployment cycles. ^WGKrdXPw

4. Version Control and Traceability: ^loQEl51o

IaC files can be stored in version control systems like Git. This allows teams to:
-> Track changes
-> Roll back to previous versions
-> Maintain history of infrastructure changes ^0mDY1cbA

5. Scalability: ^ivUYS0Mx

IaC makes it easy to scale infrastructure up or down by modifying code. For 
example, increasing the number of servers requires only a small change in 
configuration. ^ezFmRrq6

6. Reduced Human Errors: ^YOAfhzlw

Manual configuration often leads to mistakes. IaC minimizes these 
errors by automating repetitive tasks and ensuring the same steps are 
executed every time. ^RYwG7gTY

7. Improved Collaboration: ^JD9z9YSd

Teams can work together on infrastructure just like they do with 
application code. Developers, testers, and operations teams can all 
understand and modify the infrastructure setup. ^fS5W1Mkp

8. Disaster Recovery and Reproducibility: ^jQO6pooE

In case of system failure, infrastructure can be quickly rebuilt using the same code. This ensures 
reliability and business continuity. ^0elo74Wn

A Kubernetes Object is a record stored in the Kubernetes API that describes:
> What you want Kubernetes to create
> What you want Kubernetes to manage
> The desired state that Kubernetes must maintain automatically
> You create an object using YAML, JSON, or kubectl. ^dX8sWyMh

Kubernetes Object: ^VFvRH36O

• Pod
• Deployment
• ReplicaSet
• Service
• Namespace
• ConfigMap
• Secret
• Ingress
• Ingress controller
• PersistentVolume
• PersistentVolumeClaim
• Job
• CronJob
• ServiceAccount
• Role / RoleBinding
• Stateful sets ^o0KFvDpD

These are all Kubernetes objects: ^sWMQatb9

1. Namespaces: ^1uWtRqVg

> A Namespace in Kubernetes is a logical partition inside a cluster.
> Think of it as creating separate environments within the same Kubernetes cluster.
> A Namespace does not isolate machines or networks physically.
> Instead, it provides organizational and logical isolation like:
-> grouping
-> access control
-> resource limits
-> environment separation
-> easier management of many users and many applications ^2B9kPeUP

Purpose of Namespaces: ^heHYPSIi

Companies use names like:
-> development
-> testing
-> staging
-> production
> Each environment has separate workloads, secrets, config, and policies.
> Namespaces allow you to run all of them in a single cluster instead of buying multiple clusters. ^qtt9nzxH

1. Environment Separation: ^Lm9QLSDO

> Namespaces work closely with RBAC (Role-Based Access Control).
> Example: A developer from the frontend team should not modify the backend team’s deployments.
> RBAC permissions are applied at the namespace level:
-> Role
-> RoleBinding
> So each team gets isolated access. ^4H6DlAEi

2. Access Control (RBAC): ^GNBxgw7A

> In production clusters, you can prevent one team from consuming all CPU or memory.
> For example:
-> payments namespace → allowed 8 CPUs
-> analytics namespace → allowed 16 CPUs
> This prevents resource starvation. ^lnwk8y3d

3. Resource Quotas and Limits: ^lj8MK5x5

Kubernetes Namespace Commands: ^pJLzkawQ

1. List all namespaces: ^oFkLUA3G

Command: ^myazkl05

kubectl get namespaces/ns ^yCm8w5ag

Explanation: ^uiRFgK0C

This command fetches all namespaces in the cluster. It shows their status 
(Active/Terminating) and age. ^bWoiDVHh

Useful flags: ^iLo05D3Y

> -o wide: Shows additional metadata about namespaces.
> -o yaml: Displays each namespace in full YAML format.
> --show-labels: Shows labels attached to each namespace (useful when organizations use labels for 
                    team ownership). ^F3sehmw9

Example: ^T5egZkD2

kubectl get ns -o yaml ^OtaZNgKz

2. Create a namespace: ^7jGSa159

Command: ^WrbXEqRO

kubectl create namespace my-namespace ^IlHySDRU

Explanation: ^9Qy7bndQ

Creates a new empty namespace. Useful for separating environments like dev, test, prod, or 
separating teams. ^CWcjOQGp

> -o yaml: Prints the YAML definition of the namespace instead of creating it. ^DmIqTqHb

Generate YAML of namespace: ^ZtJyLDRx

kubectl create ns project-a --dry-run=client -o yaml ^btPNHO67

3. Delete a namespace: ^5B36LhQM

Command: ^8DEgfKZb

kubectl delete namespace my-namespace ^OMKEmV6a

Explanation: ^lcammwzh

Removes a namespace and all resources inside that namespace (pods, services, configmaps, 
secrets, deployments). Deletion may take time because Kubernetes must clean up all resources. ^ICs8ep4Z

Useful flags: ^62PJjyf2

> --force --grace-period=0: Force deletion (rarely recommended; may cause dangling resources). ^a7aHKsBV

4. View details of a namespace: ^kiLQ6bkU

Command: ^qj69QDgf

kubectl describe namespace my-namespace ^uVTvxODC

Explanation: ^njcDfqoL

Shows detailed information such as:
> Labels
> Annotations
> Resource quotas applied
> Limit ranges
> Finalizers
> Status
This is helpful for debugging or understanding namespace policies in an enterprise environment. ^P7c55xkC

5. Set a default namespace for the kubectl context: ^FvhSnvtW

Command: ^dnz9svql

kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=my-namespace ^fHwx9vVI

Explanation: ^lSeopV2k

Changes your Kubernetes context so that all future commands run inside this namespace 
automatically, unless you override using -n.
This is extremely useful if you work repeatedly in:
> dev
> test
> team-specific namespaces ^nKi5L2E9

To verify: ^uvaYHnUz

kubectl config view --minify | grep namespace ^pCVzQ3E0

To reset to default: ^Gig7kF6Q

kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=default ^8ChSg12u

Additional Useful Namespace Commands: ^MOqYggdj

Get all resources in a namespace: ^4qbg8ixc

kubectl get all -n my-namespace ^aQVfjrFp

Delete all resources inside a namespace: ^8uyXnzb7

kubectl delete all --all -n my-namespace ^r3gpCGVN

View events in a namespace: ^R6X1VuYp

kubectl get events -n my-namespace ^uXPBoUzP

Switch namespace using kubens (if installed): ^Fx2KZLaH

kubens dev ^YUSPVPnT

Default Namespaces in Kubernetes: ^jemjg1s7

1. default: ^um8IDQp3

This is the namespace used when you do not specify one. ^lThPFwQt

Purpose: ^dibET8oU

Used for small workloads, testing, learning, or for users who forget to specify a namespace. ^pr0aUSbG

It contains internal Kubernetes system components such as:
• kube-apiserver
• kube-scheduler
• kube-controller-manager
• etcd
• CoreDNS
• kube-proxy
> Never delete workloads in kube-system.
> DevOps engineers monitor kube-system very closely, because cluster health depends on these pods. ^mGLOUuia

2. kube-system: ^suKqmvbf

This is the heart of the Kubernetes cluster. ^8zPuECpi

> Used for cluster-wide public configurations.
> Example: ConfigMaps used for cluster info.
> It is rarely used manually. Mostly used by cluster bootstrap processes. ^0KLuiEjs

3. kube-public: ^I6LYbq6P

This namespace is readable by everyone (even unauthenticated users). ^56lJeAbX

> Stores the "lease" objects that help determine node heartbeat.
> “Lease” is how Kubernetes checks if a node is still alive.
> Used internally by node health checking; you never deploy apps here. ^MA1egKvF

4. kube-node-lease: ^A1sb54Z1

Introduced in newer Kubernetes versions. Used to check the node health. ^uE1UhFAj

1. A user (Developer / Production team / Test Engineer) sends a request to create a Pod (Cart / Product / Login) using kubectl or an API call.
2. The API Server receives the request and validates it.
3. The API Server writes the Pod definition + Namespace information (Developer / Production / Test Engineers) into etcd, the cluster’s database.
4. The Controller Manager constantly watches etcd to understand the desired state of the cluster.
5. When the Controller Manager notices a new desired object (Cart pod / Login pod / Product pod), it decides that Kubernetes must take action.
6. The Controller Manager informs the Scheduler that this Pod needs a Worker Node.
7. The Scheduler checks all Worker Nodes and chooses the best suitable node based on:
   > CPU & RAM availability
   > Node conditions
   > Namespace policies
   > Affinity / anti-affinity
8. The Scheduler sends the placement decision back to the API Server.
9. The API Server notifies the Kubelet running on the chosen Worker Node.
10. The Kubelet pulls the required container images (cont1, cont2) and creates the Pod using the Container Runtime (containerd/Docker/CRI-O).
11. The Kubelet continuously monitors the Pod and sends status updates back to the API Server.
12. Kube-Proxy on the Worker Node sets up networking, assigns a Pod IP, and configures routing for cross-namespace communication.
13. Namespace boundaries ensure:
    > Developer Namespace Pods (Cart + Product) are isolated from Production
    > Test Engineers Namespace Pods (Login) are isolated from others
    > Production Namespace Pods (Cart) are protected and separated
14. If any Pod fails (Cart/Login/Product), the Controller Manager detects the mismatch between desired state and actual state.
15. The Controller Manager instructs Kubernetes to recreate the Pod—possibly on the same node or a different node.
16. The API Server updates etcd with the new actual state.
17. This loop continues indefinitely, ensuring every Namespace always has the Pods it needs. ^2rAf8WKN

How Namespaces work? ^bLWuO36O

1. The Kubelet reports to the API Server that the Pod has stopped running.
2. The API Server updates etcd — actual state becomes: “Pod is dead.”
3. The Controller Manager notices the mismatch between:
    > Desired state: Pod should exist in its Namespace
    > Actual state: Pod does not exist
4. The Controller Manager does NOT talk to the Scheduler directly. It updates the API Server by creating a new unscheduled Pod in the 
   correct Namespace.
5. The Scheduler sees the new unscheduled Pod via the API Server.
6. The Scheduler picks the best Worker Node and sends the decision back to the API Server.
7. The API Server informs the Kubelet on that Worker Node.
8. The Kubelet recreates the Pod by pulling container images and starting cont1 + cont2 again. ^b3hqGLXb

When a Pod Dies (Cart / Login / Product): ^8YKcbk6d

Important Notes: ^Gd2XWUEO

> The Controller Manager communicates ONLY with the API Server.
> The Scheduler communicates ONLY with the API Server.
> They never communicate with each other directly.
> Namespaces only provide logical isolation, not physical isolation.
> Multiple namespaces can schedule Pods on the same Worker Node, as shown in the diagram. ^LipMz3H4

1. A user (Developer / QA Engineer / Production Release Team) sends a request to create or update a Pod (cart / login / 
  payment / product) inside:
2. dev namespace in the Dev cluster
3. test namespace in the Test cluster
4. prod namespace in the Production cluster
5. This can be done using kubectl or via an API call.
6. The API Server receives the request and validates it.
7. The API Server writes the Pod definition + namespace information (dev / test / prod) into etcd, the cluster database.
8. The Controller Manager continuously watches etcd to understand the desired state of the cluster.
9. Every Deployment (cart, login, payment, product) has a desired number of Pods.
10. When the Controller Manager notices a new desired object (cart pod / login pod / payment pod / product pod), it decides that
    Kubernetes must take action.
11. The Controller Manager informs the Scheduler that a Pod needs to be placed on a worker node.
12. The Scheduler checks all worker nodes inside that cluster and selects the best node based on:
    > CPU + RAM availability
    > Node conditions
    > Namespace quotas (dev/test/prod)
13. The Scheduler sends the chosen node decision back to the API Server.
14. The API Server notifies the Kubelet running on that specific worker node.
15. The Kubelet pulls the required container images (cont1, cont2) for that microservice (cart/login/payment/product) and starts
    the Pod using the container runtime (containerd/Docker/CRI-O).
16. The Kubelet continuously reports Pod status back to the API Server.
17. Kube-proxy on the worker node sets up networking, assigns a Pod IP, and configures service routing for communication inside 
    the namespace.
18. Namespace Boundaries Ensure (Real-Life Behavior)
19. Dev namespace Pods (cart/login/payment/product) are isolated from the Test namespace.
20. Test namespace Pods are isolated from Prod namespace.
21. Production Pods are strictly protected and separated from Dev/Test.
22. ConfigMaps, Secrets, and resource quotas are different for dev, test, and prod.
23. Even though Pods run on the same worker node, they cannot access Pods from another namespace unless explicitly allowed 
     using NetworkPolicies.
24. This ensures logical isolation, not physical isolation.
25. When a Pod Dies (Cart / Login / Payment / Product):
     -> The Kubelet reports to the API Server that the Pod has stopped running.
     -> The API Server updates etcd with the new state: “Pod is dead.”
     -> The Controller Manager notices the mismatch:
     -> Desired state: Pod should exist in the namespace.
     -> Actual state: Pod does not exist.
26. The Controller Manager does NOT contact the Scheduler directly.
27. It updates the API Server by creating a new unscheduled Pod object in the namespace.
28. The Scheduler sees the new unscheduled Pod via the API Server.
29. The Scheduler chooses the best worker node and sends the placement decision back to the API Server.
30. The API Server informs the Kubelet on that worker node.
31. The Kubelet recreates the Pod by pulling the container images and starting cont1 + cont2 again.
32. This loop continues indefinitely.
33. It ensures dev/test/prod namespaces always have the Pods they need, even if failures occur. ^pbQ9g1KH

Important Notes (Real-Life System Behavior): ^2NE936BV

> The Controller Manager communicates only with the API Server.
> The Scheduler communicates only with the API Server.
> Worker nodes (kubelet) communicate only with the API Server.
> Namespaces only provide logical isolation; they do not provide physical isolation.
> Multiple namespaces can run Pods on the same worker node (same machine).
> Dev, Test, and Prod clusters are fully isolated from each other — no shared API Server, no shared etcd.
> The same microservices exist in all clusters, but the configs/secrets/scale are different for each environment. ^wOuHYwDl

> IPv4 (Internet Protocol version 4) is the most widely used protocol for identifying devices on a network and enabling 
  communication over the internet.
> IPv4 is a system that assigns a unique numerical address to every device connected to a network so they can find and 
  communicate with each other. ^8qbKHd0Z

VPC (Virtual private cloud) ^IjRmkikK

Region ^37puPLUn

> We are able to isolate a region because of VPC.
> VPC provides a network which isolates the resources.
> Subnets – Divide your VPC into smaller networks (like rooms in a house).
> Route table – It is used to Connect multiple subnets.
> Internet gateway – Data accessed from the internet must pass through Internet gateway.
> NAT Gateway – Lets private subnet talk out to the internet (but not receive requests).
> ACLs (Access control lists) – It decides whether traffic is allowed in or out of a subnet.
> Security groups – It decides which traffic is allowed in or out of a particular EC2 instance (or resource). ^ikJOnsKd

VPC ^6iOMPHal

Public
Subnet ^b2B3V3vT

10.0.0.1 - 10.255.255.254 ---> This is one range of IP address ^SA9zmkXM

Example: Amazon app ^bxTfWjAx

> 10 servers for frontend
> 10 servers for middleware
> 10-servers for backend ^YzvMp1e6

Internet gateway ^3QAStNhP

VPC (Virtual Private Cloud): 
> A VPC is a logically isolated virtual network where you launch your AWS resources. 
> It's like your own private data center in the cloud, giving you control over your IP address range, subnets, and network 
  gateways. ^Nk0iyCw5

NAT (Network Address Translation) gateway: 
> This service allows resources in a private subnet to connect to the internet (e.g., for software updates) while preventing 
  external, unsolicited inbound connections. 
> The NAT gateway is placed in a public subnet, and instances in the private subnet send their internet-bound traffic to 
   the NAT gateway, which then routes it to the internet gateway. ^ry0UFe2f

Subnets: 
> A subnet is a segment of your VPC's IP address range. 
> They are tied to a single Availability Zone (AZ). 
> You can have public subnets, which can route traffic to the internet, and private subnets, which are isolated from direct 
   internet access. ^aX7337vv

Route Tables: 
> A route table contains a set of rules that determine where network traffic from a subnet is directed. 
> For a public subnet, the route table includes a route to an Internet Gateway (IGW). 
> For a private subnet, it directs internet-bound traffic to a NAT Gateway. ^DeFeDE6K

Internet Gateway (IGW): 
> This component allows communication between a VPC and the internet. 
> It is attached to the VPC and enables instances in a public subnet to have a public IP address and be directly accessible 
  from the internet. ^AJF419uM

Security Groups: 
> A security group is a stateful firewall that controls traffic at the instance level. 
> They operate on an "allow-only" basis, so you can't explicitly deny traffic. 
> Because they are stateful, if you allow a packet in, the response packet is automatically allowed out. ^eFqJu217

Network Access Control Lists (NACLs): 
> A NACL is an optional, stateless firewall that controls traffic at the subnet level. 
> They have both "allow" and "deny" rules, which are processed in numbered order. 
> Unlike security groups, NACLs are stateless, meaning you must create both an inbound and an outbound rule for two-way 
  communication to work. ^8EGV2WYz

Firewall: 
> A firewall is a security system that monitors and controls network traffic. 
> It acts as a barrier, allowing non-threatening traffic and blocking dangerous traffic based on a set of rules.
> Inbound traffic is any data entering your network.
> Outbound traffic is any data leaving your network. ^uR6OhEHo

Data flows to VPC (inbound traffic) ^EVKr9KPp

Data flows out of VPC (outbound traffic) ^a5wb1Amf

Format of IPv4 Address: ^apbrw5YH

> An IPv4 address is a 32-bit number, usually written in decimal format like this:
> It has 4 parts (octets) separated by dots
> Each part ranges from 0 to 255.
> Each part (octet) of an IPv4 address is 8 bits long.
> In binary, a bit can be 0 or 1
> So with 8 bits, the maximum value is: 255 ^Y7N9DmLy

Integrating Jenkins with SonarQube, Nexus and Tomcat ^XQ7IgXON

1) Setup 4 instances --- one for Jenkins, one for SonarQube, One for Nexus and One for Tomcat.
   while setting up enable port numbers 9000 (for sonarqube), 8081 (for Nexus), 8080 (for Tomcat
   and Jenkins).
2) Download and install Jenkins, SonarQube, Nexus (For nexus, create a repository also) and Tomcat on their
    respective instances.
3) Log in to Jenkins instance and create a pipeline job for creating a .war file (use spring-framework-petclinic).




















4) To integrate SonarQube with Jenkins, first log in to SonarQube. You will see the homepage of sonarqube.
   at the top right corner, you will find 'A' symbol. Click that Button it is for "Administrator". Click on A and then
   under Administrator, select "My account".
5) At the sidebar, you will find these options, "Profile", "Security", "Notifications", "Projects", "Appearance". Select
    "Security" option.
6) Under "Generate Tokens" give the name "jenkins-sonar-token" and under type select "Global Analysis token"
   then click on Generate. You will get a token like this:
7) Copy the token and save it inside the jenkins instance by creating a text file. Inside the file save the token
   and also the name which you have given like this: 


8) Go to Jenkins and in Jenkins go to manage jenkins and click on plugins. Under available plugins select a plugins
   called as "SonarQube Scanner" and install it.
9) Once after installing, once again go to manage jenkins, this time click on Credentials. Here you will see something
    like "Stores scoped to Jenkins" under which you will see System. Click on it. Then click on Global and finally click
    on Add credentials. Select Secret text, click on next. Under Secret give the token which you have saved in the
    jenkins instance. Under ID give the name of the token which is also saved in the file in the jenkins instance.
    Secret:
    ID:
    Description: 
10) After setting up the credentials, once again go to manage jenkins. This time select System. Scroll down till you
     find "SonarQube servers" under that click on Add SonarQube. Under Name, give any name (I gave it as sonarqube).
     Under server URL, copy and paste the SonarQube URL:
     and under Credentials, select the credentials which you just created. Then click on apply and save.
11) Now go to your pipeline project and paste a block like this: 
    you will get this block in Pipeline syntax. Search for withSonarQubeENV.








12) After pasting the block, generate the build.



13) Now talking about Nexus first of all, connect to nexus instance and login to nexus. Check whether, the repository is
    present or not.
14) Go to Jenkins and then Manage jenkins and download a plugin called as: "Nexus Artifact Uploader"
15) Restart Jenkins and just like how we uploaded the credentials of SonarQube, same way we need to upload the 
    credentials of Nexus.
16) After installing the plugin, Once again go to manage Jenkins, click on credentials, at the bottom, we have system. Click
    on it. Then click on Global and then click on Add Credentials. Select "Username with password" and then under username,
    give it as admin (same username as Nexus) and password admin123 (same password as Nexus). Give the id as anything you
    want (Example: nexus) and description can be given anything (Example: nexus credentials). Finally click on create.
17) Go the project that you have created, and after sonarqube block, add one more block for nexus. You will get the block from
    pipeline syntax.
18) Click on pipeline syntax and search for "nexusArtifactUploader: Nexus Artifact Uploader". For Nexus version, select NEXUS3,
     For nexus URL copy paste the URL:                       , For credentials, Select the nexus credentials.
19) For GroupId, version and repository name, Go the GitHub, and there click on pom.xml. You will find some thing like this in the
     beginning:








    Here you can find groupId (org.springframework.samples), version(6.2.8) and for repository name,
    go to nexus and see the name which you have given for your repository.
    fill in all these.
20) After filling all these information, Under Artifacts, click on Add. Here for artifactId, go to pom.xml in GitHub, there you will
     find the artifactId paste the artifactId. For Type, write it as war. Skip Classifier and then for File write target/petclinic.war.
21) Finally click on Generate pipeline syntax. You will get something like this: 




22) Copy this and paste it in nexus block like this then click on Apply and save and finally click on Build now.


















23) To connect Tomcat with Jenkins, first log in to tomcat instance and open tomcat. Then Go to Jenkins, manage jenkins and 
     install a plugin "ssh agent" and restart Jenkins.
24) After logging in, Once again go to manage Jenkins, click on credentials, at the bottom, we have system. Click
     on it. Then click on Global and then click on Add Credentials. Select "SSH username with private key". For ID, write
     anything (Example: tomcat) Give a proper description (Example: tomcat), Give the username (To get the username go 
     to your slave instance and type the command "whoami". You will get your username). For "Private Key" select the
     option, "Enter directly" and then click on "Add". Copy the private key of the tomcat instance (RSA/ED25519 
     algorithm) which you have downloaded from AWS and paste it here. After this scroll down and click on Add. Credentials are 
     added. ^Eslf4sfr

                    sh 'mvn verify sonar:sonar'

pipeline {
    agent any

    environment {
        APP_NAME = "spring-framework-petclinic"
    }

    stages {
        stage('Git-Clone') {
            steps {
                git branch: 'main', url: 'https://github.com/Sumukha47/spring-framework-petclinic.git'
            }
        }

        stage('Package') {
            steps {
                sh 'mvn clean package'
            }
        } ^gVMkCFv6

> So, Total number of IPv6 addresses that can be created is: ^OIinn1WA

=> 16-bit X 16-bit X 16-bit X 16-bit X 16-bitX 16-bitX 16-bitX 16-bit 
=>  65536 X 65536 X65536 X65536 X65536 X65536 X65536 X 65536
=> 340,282,366,920,938,463,426,481,119,284,349,108,225 ~ 340 undecillion IPv6 addresses ^AQBVv9Kv

> IPv6 (Internet Protocol version 6) is the new version of IP addressing designed to replace IPv4 and solve the problem of 
  limited IP addresses.
> IPv4 has: ~4.3 billion addresses (not enough today)
> IPv6 provides: 2^128 addresses
> That’s an extremely huge number (~340 undecillion)
> Practically unlimited for the future
> IPv6 uses 128 bits and is written in hexadecimal format:
-> 8 groups (called hextets)
-> Each group = 16 bits
-> Separated by colon (:) ^zMOuypfN

1) Public IP Address: A public IP address is a dynamic IP address that is automatically assigned to an EC2 instance when 
                           it is launched in a public subnet. It allows the instance to be directly accessible from the internet.

> Routable on the internet.
> It's dynamic, meaning it changes every time you stop and start the instance. This makes it unsuitable for services that 
  require a consistent public endpoint, like a web server with a DNS record.
> It is ideal for temporary instances or those that don't need a fixed, publicly accessible address. ^0rf03Vfc

Types of IP addersses ^VQ3FSNiq

2) Private IP Address: A private IP address is a non-routable address assigned to an instance within your Virtual Private 
                             Cloud (VPC). It is used for communication between resources within the same VPC.

> Only accessible within your VPC. Not reachable from the internet.
> It's static. Once assigned to an instance, it remains the same even if the instance is stopped and started.
> It is used for internal communication between different instances, databases, or application servers, providing an 
  additional layer of security by keeping them isolated from the public internet. ^PlzB0Fx5

3) Elastic IP Address: An Elastic IP is a static, public IP address that you can associate with your AWS account and 
                            then with an EC2 instance. It provides a fixed IP address for your instance, which is independent 
                            of the instance's state.

> Routable on the internet.
> It's static and persistent. Unlike a regular public IP, it doesn't change when the instance is stopped and started. You 
  can also reassign an Elastic IP from one instance to another, which is crucial for building highly available and 
  fault-tolerant applications.
> It is perfect for mission-critical services like production web servers, NAT gateways, and other resources that need a 
  consistent, unchanging public IP address.
> While you can have a certain number of Elastic IPs, AWS charges a small fee for any Elastic IP that is not associated 
  with a running instance or not being used. This encourages users to release unused Elastic IPs back to the public pool. ^CkhHknV1

11) After pasting this and making necessary changes, click on next.
12) Give a policy name and then click on Create policy.
13) Go to Roles in the sidebar and click on Create role.
14) Under "Trusted entity type" select AWS service and under "Use case" select Lambda and then click on next.
15) Search for the policy that we have created and then select the policy and then click on next.
16) Give a role name and then click on Create role.
17) Search for lambda from the search bar.
18) Click on create function button which is in the landing page. If not found then click functions option which is 
    present in the sidebar then click on create function.
19) You will be getting three options "Author from scratch", "Use a blueprint", "Container image" choose Author from
     scratch option.
20) Under "Basic Information" section give a function name, and select the runtime as python 3.13 and then under
     "Change default execution role" option you will find an option called as "Use an existing role" select this option and
     then select the role that we have created and thne finally scroll to the bottom and click on create function.
21) Under "Code" section you will be having "Code source" section there you will be having default code, remove that 
    and paste this script: ^MIGI0zAS

import json
import urllib.parse
import boto3

print("Loading function...")

s3 = boto3.client("s3")
sns = boto3.client("sns")

def lambda_handler(event, context):
    """Lambda function to handle S3 events and send a formatted SNS notification."""
    bucket = key = event_name = None  # safe defaults

    try:
        # Validate event structure
        if "Records" not in event or not event["Records"]:
            return {
                "status": "failed",
                "reason": "Invalid event format — expected S3 event with 'Records'.",
                "received_event": event
            }

        record = event["Records"][0]
        bucket = record.get("s3", {}).get("bucket", {}).get("name")
        key = urllib.parse.unquote_plus(record.get("s3", {}).get("object", {}).get("key", ""))
        event_name = record.get("eventName", "UnknownEvent")

        if not bucket or not key:
            return {
                "status": "failed",
                "reason": "Missing bucket name or object key.",
                "event": event
            }

        print(f"Processing {event_name} for {bucket}/{key}")

        # Prepare base JSON message
        message_data = {
            "Bucket": bucket,
            "FileName": key,
            "Event": event_name,
            "Region": record.get("awsRegion", "Unknown"),
        }

        # Handle object creation or deletion
        if event_name.startswith("ObjectRemoved"):
            message_data["Action"] = "File Deleted"
        else:
            # Try fetching file metadata
            try:
                response = s3.get_object(Bucket=bucket, Key=key)
                content_type = response.get("ContentType", "Unknown")
                message_data["ContentType"] = content_type
            except Exception as file_error:
                # File may have restricted permissions
                message_data["ContentType"] = "AccessDenied"
                message_data["Error"] = str(file_error)

        # Pretty JSON message for SNS
        sns_message = json.dumps(message_data, indent=4)

        # SNS subject
        subject = f"S3 Bucket [{bucket}] Event [{event_name}]"

        # Publish message to SNS
        sns.publish(
            TargetArn=                                                               ,
            Message=sns_message,
            Subject=subject
        )

        print("SNS message sent successfully.")
        return {"status": "success", "message": message_data}

    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error processing event: {e}")
        return {
            "status": "error",
            "error": str(e),
            "bucket": bucket,
            "key": key,
            "event_name": event_name
        } ^atHPEJX6

"YOUR ARN PLEASE (Paste the arn along with "")" ^FkehwPhG

22) After pasting this script, Click on deploy button and then click on test button. Sometimes you will get an option 
     near the searchbar of the terminal which says "Create new test event". Select that and give a name to the event 
     and then save it. After that click on test button. In the output if you get "Status: Succeeded" then the 
     whatever action you have written in the script has been performed.
23) Scroll up and under "Function overview" click on "Add trigger" option.
24) Select S3 as your "Trigger configuration" and then choose the S3 bucket and under "Event types" select
     "All object create events" and "All object delete events" and then tick on "I acknowledge that using the same S3 
     bucket for both input and output is not recommended and that this configuration can cause recursive invocations, 
     increased Lambda usage, and increased costs." and click on save.
25) Select "Add destination" option under "Function overview".
26) Select the "Condition" as "On success", destination type "SNS", under "Destination" select the topic name that we
     created and then click on save.
27) Now if you upload or delete any files in your S3 bucket you will get a notification inn your email.  ^fKTkQcia

Steps to get SNS notification to email when there is any object in S3 bucket with the help of Lambda ^Ny9wte8X

1) Create a S3 bucket.
2) Search SNS (Simple Notification Service) 7) in the searchbar and open it in the new tab.
3) In the sidebar you have an option called as "Topic" select that and click on Create Topic button.
4) Under details section, you have two options "FIFO" and "Standard" select Standard. Give a topic name and scroll
   towards the end and click on create topic.
5) Under Subscriptions section click on Create subscription button.
6) Under Details you have Protocol section, select Email as the option then under Endpoint write your email id and
     then click on create subscription. 
7) When you click on create subscription, you will get an email confirmation go to your email and click on "confirm 
   subscription"
8) Next search for IAM in the searchbar and open in a new tab.
9) Select Policies from the sidebar and then click on Create policy.
10) Here you will be getting two options "Visual" and "JASON" select JASON and paste this:  ^mXAeQHIg

   stage('Nexus') {
            steps {
                nexusArtifactUploader artifacts: [[artifactId: 'spring-framework-petclinic', 
                classifier: '', 
                file: 'target/petclinic.war',

                groupId: 'org.springframework.samples',

25) After adding the credentials, Go back to the pipeline project and add one more block for tomcat. click on Pipeline syntax and
     select "sshagent: SSH Agent", scroll down and click on Generate Pipeline Syntax. You will get something like this:






     here you have to add one line:


     


     



     If you want to add this line then once again click on "Pipeline Syntax" link. It will open a new tab where under "Sample Step" choose 
     "sh: Shell Script". Under "Shell Script" write down this line. Overall, the tomcat block looks like this:









26) Once after everything is done, click on Apply and Save. Then click on Build Now.  Go to the Apache Tomcat website, refresh the page.
     Copy the public ip of Tomcat instance and paste it like this:

27) After this add a post block for configuring e-mail notification and also add GitHub webhook trigger for Git SCM polling. Finally make
     some changes in the GitHub and see whether everything is working or not. ^u7co6nH7

stage('Deploy to Tomcat') {
            steps {
                sshagent(['tomcat']) {
                    sh 'scp -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no target/petclinic.war ubuntu@16.112.59.99:/home/ubuntu/apache-tomcat-10.1.48/webapps/'
                }
            }
        } ^c2UIlgB1

sshagent(['tomcat']) {

//some block           

} ^XVkWlHYW

sh 'scp -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no target/petclinic.war ubuntu@16.112.59.99:/home/ubuntu/apache-tomcat-10.1.48/webapps/' ^pzvLjgNb

This disables SSH’s host verification prompt.
(Normally: First time connecting → asks:
Are you sure you want to continue connecting (yes/no)?
With this option: It automatically accepts the remote server.) ^8HeU2x3C

Source Path
(Jenkins instance) ^Or5j4Zd0

Destination Path
(Tomcat instance) ^kg7zty9U

http://15.168.240.55:8080/petclinic ^lDD1fgtK

pipeline {
    agent any

    environment {
        APP_NAME = "spring-framework-petclinic"
    }

    stages {
        stage('Git-Clone') {
            steps {
                git branch: 'main', url: 'https://github.com/Sumukha47/spring-framework-petclinic.git'
            }
        }

        stage('Package') {
            steps {
                sh 'mvn clean package'
            }
        }
        stage('Artifact Extraction') {
            steps {
                archiveArtifacts artifacts: 'target/*.war', fingerprint: true
            }
        }
        stage('SonarQube Analysis') {
            steps {
                withSonarQubeEnv(installationName: 'sonarqube', credentialsId: 'jenkins-sonar-token') {
                    sh 'mvn verify sonar:sonar'
                }
            }
        }

        stage('Upload to Nexus') {
            steps {
                nexusArtifactUploader artifacts: [[
                    artifactId: 'spring-framework-petclinic',
                    classifier: '',
                    file: 'target/petclinic.war',
                    type: 'war'
                ]],
                credentialsId: 'nexus',
                groupId: 'org.springframework.samples',
                nexusUrl: '18.60.117.115:8081',
                nexusVersion: 'nexus3',
                protocol: 'http',
                repository: 'spring-petclinic',
                version: '6.2.8'
            }
        }

        stage('Deploy to Tomcat') {
            steps {
                sshagent(['tomcat']) {
                    sh 'scp -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no target/petclinic.war ubuntu@16.112.59.99:/home/ubuntu/apache-tomcat-10.1.48/webapps/'
                }
            }
        }
    }

    post {
        success {
            echo "Build succeeded for ${APP_NAME}"

            mail(
                to: 'sumukhadaring@gmail.com',
                cc: 'akshathabm20@gmail.com',
                subject: "SUCCESS: ${APP_NAME} Build #${env.BUILD_NUMBER}",
                body: """
                <html>
                <body>
                    <p>Hello Developer,</p>
                    <p>
                        The Jenkins pipeline for <b>${env.JOB_NAME}</b> has completed successfully.
                    </p>
                    <h3>Build Details:</h3>
                    <ul>
                        <li><b>Build Number:</b> ${env.BUILD_NUMBER}</li>
                        <li><b>Build URL:</b> <a href='${env.BUILD_URL}'>${env.BUILD_URL}</a></li>
                        <li><b>Console Logs:</b> <a href='${env.BUILD_URL}console'>View Logs</a></li>
                        <li><b>Artifacts:</b> <a href='${env.BUILD_URL}artifact/target/'>Download WAR</a></li>
                    </ul>
                    <p>Best regards,<br><b>Sumukha Upadhyaya</b></p>
                </body>
                </html>
                """,
                mimeType: 'text/html'
            )
        }

        failure {
            echo "Build failed for ${APP_NAME}"

            mail(
                to: 'sumukhadaring@gmail.com',
                cc: 'akshathabm20@gmail.com',
                subject: "FAILURE: ${APP_NAME} Build #${env.BUILD_NUMBER}",
                body: """
                <html>
                <body>
                    <p>Hello Developer,</p>
                    <p>
                        The Jenkins pipeline for <b>${env.JOB_NAME}</b> has failed.
                    </p>
                    <h3>Build Information:</h3>
                    <ul>
                        <li><b>Build Number:</b> ${env.BUILD_NUMBER}</li>
                        <li><b>Build URL:</b> <a href='${env.BUILD_URL}'>${env.BUILD_URL}</a></li>
                        <li><b>Console Logs:</b> <a href='${env.BUILD_URL}console'>View Logs</a></li>
                        <li><b>Artifacts (if generated):</b> <a href='${env.BUILD_URL}artifact/target/'>Download</a></li>
                    </ul>
                    <p>Please check the logs to identify the cause of failure.</p>
                    <p>Best regards,<br><b>Sumukha Upadhyaya</b></p>
                </body>
                </html>
                """,
                mimeType: 'text/html'
            )
        }
    }
}
 ^zoL879IN
